topics.title: V-I Characteristics of Diodes and Zener Diodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** V-I Characteristics of Diodes and Zener Diodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**V-I Characteristics** of a diode refers to the graphical and mathematical relationship between the **voltage applied across the diode terminals** and the **current flowing through it** under both forward and reverse bias conditions. This curve is the fundamental "fingerprint" that defines the electrical behavior of any p-n junction device.

For a **standard p-n junction diode** (such as 1N4007 or 1N4148), the curve is asymmetric:
- **Forward Bias Region:** Current rises exponentially once the applied voltage exceeds the **knee voltage** (≈ **0.7 V** for Silicon, **0.3 V** for Germanium).
- **Reverse Bias Region:** A tiny, nearly constant **reverse saturation current (I₀ ≈ µA to nA)** flows until breakdown occurs at very high voltages.

For a **Zener Diode** (heavily doped p-n junction, e.g., 1N4733, BZX55C), the V-I curve is distinguished by a sharp, controlled **Zener Breakdown** at a specific reverse voltage called the **Zener Voltage (V_Z)**. Beyond this point, the voltage across the diode remains nearly constant while current changes drastically — this is the **voltage regulation** property exploited in DC power supply design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment expects you to **plot the V-I curve** on a graph sheet, **identify the knee voltage / Zener voltage**, and **calculate both static (DC) and dynamic (AC) resistance** at a specified operating point. Marks are awarded for **neat graphs with labeled axes and units**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Stick

Think of a diode as a **one-way water valve** connected to a pipeline:

- **Forward Bias = Pushing water in the allowed direction.** The valve has a small "stickiness" — you need a minimum pressure (the **knee voltage / cut-in voltage**, ~0.7 V for Si) to unstick it. After that, water flows freely and any small increase in pressure causes a huge increase in flow (exponential current rise).
- **Reverse Bias = Pushing water in the blocked direction.** The valve holds tight, and only a tiny trickle leaks through (the **reverse saturation current** I₀). This trickle is so small it's almost negligible.
- **Zener Diode = A pressure-relief valve with a preset crack-pressure.** In reverse bias, it behaves like a normal diode until the reverse pressure reaches its **Zener voltage (V_Z)**. At that exact pressure, the valve deliberately "cracks open" and maintains that pressure precisely even as the flow changes wildly. This is **voltage regulation** — like a tap that always gives exactly 6 V output regardless of input fluctuations.

This analogy maps directly onto the experiment: you vary the input voltage (via a rheostat/potentiometer), measure the corresponding current (via ammeter) and voltage (via voltmeter), and **plot the curve** to "fingerprint" the device.

## 1.3 Apparatus & Components Required

| Item | Specification | Purpose |
|------|---------------|---------|
| Diode (Si) | 1N4007 or 1N4148 | Forward characteristics study |
| Zener Diode | 1N4733 (5.1 V) or BZX55C | Reverse breakdown / regulation study |
| DC Power Supply | 0–30 V regulated variable | Biasing the diode |
| Resistor | 1 kΩ, ½ W | Current limiting / protection |
| Rheostat / Potentiometer | 1 kΩ, 5 W | Smooth voltage variation |
| Ammeter | 0–30 mA (DC) | Measures diode current |
| Voltmeter | 0–30 V (DC) | Measures diode voltage |
| Breadboard & Connecting Wires | Standard | Circuit assembly |
| Graph Sheet | mm-scale | Plotting the V-I curve |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use a **series current-limiting resistor** with the Zener diode to prevent thermal runaway and device destruction once breakdown begins.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Diode Equation

The static V-I characteristic of a p-n junction diode is governed by the **Shockley Diode Equation**:

$$I_D = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}} - 1 \right)$$

Where each symbol has precise physical meaning that KTU examiners love to test:

- $I_D$ = Diode forward current (Amperes, A)
- $I_0$ = Reverse saturation current (typically 1 nA to 1 µA for Si)
- $V_D$ = Voltage applied across the diode (Volts, V)
- $\eta$ = **Ideality factor** (1 for ideal diode, 1–2 for real Si diodes)
- $V_T$ = **Thermal voltage** = $\frac{kT}{q} \approx$ **25.85 mV** at room temperature (T = 300 K)
- $k$ = Boltzmann's constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $q$ = Electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**

For $V_D \gg V_T$ (which is true in the forward active region), the "-1" becomes negligible, simplifying to:

$$I_D \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}}$$

This is the origin of the **exponential rise** in the forward region of the V-I curve.

## 2.2 Key Operating Regions of the V-I Curve

The V-I curve is divided into three distinct operating zones that you must label on your graph sheet:

1. **Forward Bias Region (Quadrant I):**
   - For $V_D < V_{knee}$: Current is negligible (microamps).
   - For $V_D \geq V_{knee}$: Current rises **exponentially**.
   - $V_{knee} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ for Silicon, $\approx 0.3\text{ V}$ for Germanium.

2. **Reverse Bias Region (Quadrant III):**
   - For $|V_D| < V_{breakdown}$: Tiny reverse current $I_0$ flows (almost constant).
   - For $|V_D| \geq V_{breakdown}$: Avalanche/Zener breakdown occurs.

3. **Breakdown Region (Zener Diode only):**
   - At $V_D = -V_Z$: Voltage clamps to $V_Z$ while current increases sharply.
   - This is the **regulation zone** — the operating region for the Zener as a voltage regulator.

## 2.3 Static and Dynamic Resistance — The Two Critical Calculations

This is the **most heavily tested numerical concept** in KTU lab viva and records. Understand both clearly:

### Static (DC) Resistance — $R_{DC}$

It is the **Ohm's Law** ratio of the total DC voltage to the total DC current at a specific Q-point (operating point):

$$R_{DC} = \frac{V_D}{I_D}$$

- It is the slope of the line joining the **operating point to the origin**, not the slope of the curve itself.
- $R_{DC}$ is **large** in the low-current region and **small** in the high-current region.

### Dynamic (AC) Resistance — $r_d$

It is the **reciprocal of the slope of the V-I curve** at the Q-point, representing the small-signal opposition to AC variations:

$$r_d = \frac{\Delta V_D}{\Delta I_D} = \frac{dV_D}{dI_D}$$

Differentiating the Shockley equation:

$$r_d = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_D} = \frac{25.85 \text{ mV}}{I_D \text{ (mA)}} \quad \text{(at room temp, } \eta = 1\text{)}$$

- $r_d$ is **small** at high forward currents and **large** at low currents.
- For $I_D = 1$ mA, $r_d \approx$ **25.85 Ω** (this is a classic viva question!).

### Thermal Voltage Variation

Remember that $V_T$ depends linearly on temperature:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \propto T$$

- At $T = 300$ K, $V_T \approx 25.85$ mV (rounded to **26 mV** in most KTU textbooks).
- At $T = 290$ K, $V_T \approx 25$ mV.
- At $T = 310$ K, $V_T \approx 26.7$ mV.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "what is the dynamic resistance of a silicon diode at 1 mA?", the standard textbook answer is **25 Ω** (using $V_T = 25$ mV). Always quote both the formula AND the numerical value to earn full marks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | Typical Value |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $I_D = I_0\left(e^{V_D/\eta V_T} - 1\right)$ | Shockley diode equation | A | — |
| 2 | $V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage at temp T | V | 25.85 mV at 300 K |
| 3 | $V_{knee}$ (Silicon) | Cut-in / knee voltage | V | 0.7 V |
| 4 | $V_{knee}$ (Germanium) | Cut-in / knee voltage | V | 0.3 V |
| 5 | $R_{DC} = \frac{V_D}{I_D}$ | Static / DC resistance at Q-point | Ω | Varies |
| 6 | $r_d = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_D}$ | Dynamic / AC resistance | Ω | 25 Ω at 1 mA |
| 7 | $r_d = \frac{\Delta V_D}{\Delta I_D}$ | Slope-based definition | Ω | — |
| 8 | $V_Z$ (Zener) | Zener breakdown voltage | V | 5.1 V (1N4733) |
| 9 | $P_Z = V_Z \times I_Z$ | Zener power dissipation | W | < rated $P_{ZM}$ |
| 10 | $I_{Z(min)} \leq I_Z \leq I_{Z(max)}$ | Safe Zener operating range | A | From datasheet |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding V-I characteristics is the **gateway to all semiconductor electronics**:

- **Rectifier Design:** The forward characteristic determines the **conduction drop** and **efficiency** of half-wave, full-wave, and bridge rectifiers used in every smartphone charger.
- **Voltage Regulators:** Zener diodes exploit the **near-vertical breakdown region** to provide stable reference voltages (5.1 V, 12 V) in power supplies, ADC reference circuits, and over-voltage protection.
- **Signal Clipping & Clamping:** Diode characteristics in forward and reverse regions are used to **clip waveform peaks** in TV receivers and **clamp DC levels** in communication circuits.
- **LED Lighting:** LEDs are diodes whose characteristic photon-emission starts at the knee voltage, which determines the **forward voltage drop** (1.8 V for red, 3.2 V for blue).
- **Logic Gates:** Diode logic (DL) and diode-transistor logic (DTL) — the ancestors of all modern digital ICs — rely on these V-I curves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Dynamic Resistance Formula

**Objective:** Starting from the Shockley equation, derive the closed-form expression for the dynamic resistance $r_d$ of a diode.

### Step 1 — Start with the full diode equation

The current through a p-n junction diode is given by:

$$I_D = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}} - 1 \right)$$

This is the empirical relation that captures both forward and reverse behavior.

### Step 2 — Apply the forward-bias simplification

In forward bias, when $V_D \gg \eta V_T$ (which is true for $V_D > 0.1$ V), the exponential term completely dominates the "-1" term. For example, at $V_D = 0.7$ V and $V_T = 26$ mV, $e^{0.7/0.026} \approx e^{26.9} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{11}$, which is astronomically larger than 1. So we may safely write:

$$I_D \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}}$$

The "-1" contributes less than one part in $10^{11}$ and is dropped for analytical tractability.

### Step 3 — Differentiate $I_D$ with respect to $V_D$

To find the dynamic (small-signal) conductance, differentiate both sides of the simplified equation with respect to $V_D$:

$$\frac{dI_D}{dV_D} = I_0 \cdot \frac{1}{\eta V_T} \cdot e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}}$$

This follows directly from the standard derivative rule: $\frac{d}{dx} e^{u} = e^{u} \cdot \frac{du}{dx}$, applied with $u = \frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}$.

### Step 4 — Recognize the exponential as the current itself

From Step 2, we have $I_0 \cdot e^{V_D/\eta V_T} = I_D$ (the diode current). Substituting this back:

$$\frac{dI_D}{dV_D} = \frac{I_D}{\eta V_T}$$

The right-hand side collapses to a clean ratio because the exponential has been replaced by its physical equivalent.

### Step 5 — Invert to obtain dynamic resistance

By definition, dynamic resistance is the reciprocal of the slope (conductance):

$$r_d = \frac{dV_D}{dI_D} = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_D}$$

This is the **KTU golden result** that you will use in every AC analysis. The units work out: volts / amperes = ohms, and $\eta V_T$ is in volts, $I_D$ is in amperes.

### Step 6 — Insert numerical constants at room temperature

For a silicon diode with $\eta \approx 1$ and $T = 300$ K, $\eta V_T \approx 25.85$ mV. If the diode current is expressed in milliamperes, we get the convenient form:

$$r_d \, (\Omega) = \frac{25 \text{ mV}}{I_D \text{ (mA)}}$$

This is the form used in every KTU lab manual and numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Calculation

> **Problem (KTU University Exam Style):** In a forward-biased silicon diode experiment, the following readings were recorded: $V_{D1} = 0.65$ V at $I_{D1} = 5$ mA, and $V_{D2} = 0.72$ V at $I_{D2} = 20$ mA. Calculate (a) the static resistance at each operating point, and (b) the dynamic resistance of the diode.

### Part (a) — Static Resistance

**At Point 1:** Apply Ohm's law directly using DC values:

$$R_{DC1} = \frac{V_{D1}}{I_{D1}} = \frac{0.65 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ mA}} = \frac{0.65}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 130 \, \Omega$$

**At Point 2:** Same definition, different operating point:

$$R_{DC2} = \frac{V_{D2}}{I_{D2}} = \frac{0.72 \text{ V}}{20 \text{ mA}} = \frac{0.72}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 36 \, \Omega$$

**Observation:** $R_{DC}$ decreases as we move up the curve into higher current regions — this is a property the examiner expects you to mention.

### Part (b) — Dynamic Resistance

Use the slope definition (equivalent to the closed-form result):

$$r_d = \frac{\Delta V_D}{\Delta I_D} = \frac{V_{D2} - V_{D1}}{I_{D2} - I_{D1}}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$r_d = \frac{0.72 - 0.65}{(20 - 5) \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.07}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.07}{0.015} \approx 4.67 \, \Omega$$

### Verification using the Thermal Voltage Formula

Cross-check using the theoretical formula with $V_T = 25.85$ mV, $\eta = 1$, and the average current $I_D \approx 12.5$ mA:

$$r_d^{theory} = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_D} = \frac{0.02585}{0.0125} \approx 2.07 \, \Omega$$

**Note:** The two values differ because the slope definition is approximate (uses a secant rather than a tangent). The theoretical formula is the exact derivative. KTU expects you to state both and explain the small discrepancy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Zener Diode as Voltage Regulator

For a Zener regulator circuit with input $V_{in}$, series resistor $R_S$, and load $R_L$ in parallel with the Zener, the operating equations are:

| Quantity | Expression | Derivation Logic |
|----------|------------|------------------|
| Current through $R_S$ | $I_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$ | KVL around the loop |
| Load current | $I_L = \frac{V_Z}{R_L}$ | Ohm's law on the load |
| Zener current | $I_Z = I_S - I_L$ | KCL at the regulator node |
| Maximum Zener current | $I_{Z(max)} = \frac{P_{ZM}}{V_Z}$ | From power rating |
| Regulation condition | $V_{in(min)} \geq \frac{V_Z (R_S + R_L)}{R_L}$ | To keep Zener in breakdown |
| Power dissipated in Zener | $P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z$ | Must be less than $P_{ZM}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favorite:** *"What happens if $R_L$ is removed (open circuit)?"* Answer: All of $I_S$ flows through the Zener; you must verify $I_Z \leq I_{Z(max)}$ or the Zener will burn out.

## 3.4 The Three-Step Procedure for Plotting the V-I Curve

1. **Apply bias** using the variable DC source and rheostat. For forward bias, connect the diode with anode to the positive terminal of the supply through the ammeter and series resistor.
2. **Vary the input** in small steps (e.g., 0.1 V increments below the knee, 0.05 V increments near the knee, and 1 mA current steps above the knee).
3. **Record pairs** $(V_D, I_D)$ in a tabular column, then plot $I_D$ on the **y-axis** and $V_D$ on the **x-axis** on a graph sheet. **Always** mark the knee voltage and the Zener voltage with dashed lines dropping to the x-axis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Diagram — Forward V-I Characteristics of a Diode

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC[DC Supply + 0 to 30V] --> RS[Rs = 1k ohm Series Resistor]
    RS --> AM[Ammeter 0 to 30mA]
    AM --> ANODE[Diode Anode A]
    ANODE --> CATHODE[Diode Cathode K]
    CATHODE --> VM[Voltmeter across diode]
    VM --> GND[DC Supply Negative]
    CATHODE --> GND2[Return to Ground]
    VM -. measures .-> ANODE
```

**Circuit Operation Logic:** The DC supply is swept from 0 to 30 V using the rheostat. The series resistor $R_S$ limits the current to a safe value. The ammeter reads the diode current $I_D$, and the voltmeter reads the voltage $V_D$ directly across the diode terminals.

## 4.2 Circuit Diagram — Reverse V-I Characteristics of a Zener Diode

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC2[DC Supply + 0 to 30V Reversed] --> RS2[Rs = 470 ohm Current Limiting]
    RS2 --> AM2[Ammeter 0 to 30mA]
    AM2 --> CATHODE2[Zener Cathode K]
    CATHODE2 --> ANODE2[Zener Anode A]
    ANODE2 --> VM2[Voltmeter across Zener]
    VM2 --> GND3[DC Supply Negative]
    ANODE2 --> GND4[Return to Ground]
    VM2 -. measures .-> CATHODE2
```

**Reverse Bias Logic:** The Zener is connected with **cathode toward the positive supply** (reverse biased). The voltmeter polarity must be reversed. The voltmeter will read a small value until $V_{in}$ reaches $V_Z$ (≈ 5.1 V for 1N4733), after which the voltmeter reading **clamps near $V_Z$** while the ammeter current rises sharply.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Experiment Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    STAGE1[Stage 1: DC Source 0 to 30V variable] --> STAGE2[Stage 2: Series Rheostat for smooth voltage variation]
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3[Stage 3: Current Limiting Resistor Rs 1k ohm]
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4[Stage 4: Device Under Test Diode or Zener]
    STAGE4 --> STAGE5[Stage 5: Ammeter measures current through DUT]
    STAGE4 --> STAGE6[Stage 6: Voltmeter measures voltage across DUT]
    STAGE5 --> STAGE7[Stage 7: Tabular recording of Vd and Id]
    STAGE6 --> STAGE7
    STAGE7 --> STAGE8[Stage 8: Plot V I curve on graph sheet]
    STAGE8 --> STAGE9[Stage 9: Identify Vknee and Vz from curve]
    STAGE9 --> STAGE10[Stage 10: Calculate Rdc and rd at Q point]
```

## 4.4 Expected V-I Curve Shape (ASCII Schematic for Lab Record)

```
   I (mA)
   |
   |                            /
   |                          /
   |                        /
   |                      /
   |                    /  <-- Forward region (exponential)
   |                  /
   |                /
   |____________ _ /  <-- Knee at ~0.7 V (Silicon)
   |            |
   |____________|____________________________ V (V)
   |          0.7|
   |            |
   |  Reverse   |     |
   |  saturation|     |
   |  current   |     |  <-- Sharp vertical rise
   |____________|     |     (Zener breakdown at -Vz)
              -Vz    |
                      \
                       \  (continues steeply)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Record Tip:** When drawing the curve on a graph sheet, the forward region is plotted in the **first quadrant** ($+V, +I$) and the reverse region in the **third quadrant** ($-V, -I$). For Zener, the breakdown is plotted in the **fourth quadrant** ($-V, +I$ magnitude) by convention.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "knee voltage" of a p-n junction diode. What is its typical value for silicon and germanium diodes?**

**Model Answer:**
The **knee voltage** (also called **cut-in voltage** or **threshold voltage**) of a p-n junction diode is the minimum forward bias voltage at which the diode begins to conduct appreciable current. Below this voltage, the diode current is negligibly small (in microamperes). Beyond this voltage, the diode current increases rapidly (exponentially) with small increases in voltage.

- For **Silicon diodes**: $V_{knee} \approx$ **0.7 V**
- For **Germanium diodes**: $V_{knee} \approx$ **0.3 V**

(3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between static resistance and dynamic resistance of a diode. Mention their units.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Resistance ($R_{DC}$) | Dynamic Resistance ($r_d$) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | Ratio of DC voltage to DC current at an operating point | Reciprocal of the slope of the V-I curve at an operating point |
| Formula | $R_{DC} = \frac{V_D}{I_D}$ | $r_d = \frac{\Delta V_D}{\Delta I_D} = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_D}$ |
| Value | Large at low currents, small at high currents | Small at high currents, large at low currents |
| Units | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |

(3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram to obtain the forward and reverse V-I characteristics of a p-n junction diode. Explain the procedure and list the components required. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** The forward V-I readings of a silicon diode are: $(0.60\text{ V}, 2\text{ mA})$ and $(0.68\text{ V}, 10\text{ mA})$. Calculate (i) static resistance at each point, and (ii) dynamic resistance between these two points. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Circuit Diagram & Procedure:**

**Components Required (2 marks):**
- Silicon diode (1N4007), 1 kΩ resistor, 0–30 V DC regulated power supply, 0–30 mA ammeter, 0–30 V voltmeter, rheostat (1 kΩ), breadboard, connecting wires, graph sheet.

**Forward Bias Circuit (3 marks):**

The diode is forward-biased by connecting its **anode to the positive terminal** of the supply through an ammeter and a series current-limiting resistor. A voltmeter is connected **directly across the diode** (in parallel) to measure $V_D$. The rheostat is used to vary the input voltage smoothly in small steps.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Connect the circuit as per the diagram on the breadboard.
2. Initially set the DC supply to 0 V using the rheostat.
3. Gradually increase the supply voltage in steps of 0.1 V, and at each step, note the voltmeter reading $V_D$ and the ammeter reading $I_D$.
4. Continue until the ammeter reads the rated maximum current of the diode (e.g., 30 mA for 1N4007).
5. Tabulate the $(V_D, I_D)$ pairs and plot $I_D$ vs $V_D$ on a graph sheet with $V_D$ on the x-axis and $I_D$ on the y-axis.
6. Identify the knee voltage from the curve and label it.
7. For reverse bias, reverse the diode connections and repeat with the supply varied from 0 to a higher value (e.g., 30 V), recording $V_R$ and $I_R$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{D1} = 0.60$ V, $I_{D1} = 2$ mA; $V_{D2} = 0.68$ V, $I_{D2} = 10$ mA.

**(i) Static Resistance (3 marks):**

At Point 1:

$$R_{DC1} = \frac{V_{D1}}{I_{D1}} = \frac{0.60}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 300 \, \Omega$$

At Point 2:

$$R_{DC2} = \frac{V_{D2}}{I_{D2}} = \frac{0.68}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 68 \, \Omega$$

**(ii) Dynamic Resistance (4 marks):**

The dynamic resistance is the slope-based definition:

$$r_d = \frac{\Delta V_D}{\Delta I_D} = \frac{V_{D2} - V_{D1}}{I_{D2} - I_{D1}}$$

$$r_d = \frac{0.68 - 0.60}{(10 - 2) \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.08}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.08}{0.008} = 10 \, \Omega$$

**Result:** $R_{DC1} = 300\ \Omega$, $R_{DC2} = 68\ \Omega$, $r_d = 10\ \Omega$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to obtain the **V-I characteristics of a Zener diode** in both forward and reverse bias. Highlight the **breakdown region** on the curve. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A 5.1 V Zener diode (rated power $P_{ZM} = 500$ mW) is used in a regulator circuit with a series resistor $R_S = 470\ \Omega$ and a load $R_L = 1\text{ k}\Omega$. If the input voltage is 12 V, calculate (i) the Zener current, (ii) the load current, and (iii) verify whether the Zener is operating within safe limits. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Circuit & Procedure (7 marks):**

**Circuit:** Use a Zener diode (e.g., 1N4733, $V_Z = 5.1$ V) with the **cathode connected to the positive supply** (reverse biased), a series current-limiting resistor $R_S$, an ammeter in series, and a voltmeter across the Zener (with reversed polarity compared to forward bias).

**Procedure:**
1. **Forward bias:** Connect the Zener as a normal diode (anode to + supply). Vary the input from 0 to ~1 V and record $(V_F, I_F)$. The forward curve resembles that of a regular Si diode with knee at ~0.7 V.
2. **Reverse bias:** Reverse the connections (cathode to + supply). Slowly increase the input voltage from 0 V. The reverse current will be a small saturation value $I_0$ until the voltage reaches $V_Z$ (5.1 V for 1N4733).
3. **Breakdown observation:** At $V = V_Z$, the voltmeter reading **clamps** at $V_Z$ while the ammeter current rises sharply. Continue to record a few points in the breakdown region but never exceed $I_{Z(max)}$.
4. **Plotting:** Combine the forward and reverse curves on a single graph sheet, marking the breakdown voltage $V_Z$ with a dashed line.

The breakdown region is the **nearly vertical portion** of the reverse curve at $V_D = -V_Z$ — this is the operating region for voltage regulation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_Z = 5.1$ V, $P_{ZM} = 500$ mW, $R_S = 470\ \Omega$, $R_L = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 12$ V.

**(i) Current through series resistor $R_S$ (2 marks):**

$$I_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{12 - 5.1}{470} = \frac{6.9}{470} \approx 14.68 \text{ mA}$$

**(ii) Load current (2 marks):**

$$I_L = \frac{V_Z}{R_L} = \frac{5.1}{1000} = 5.1 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Zener current and safety check (3 marks):**

By KCL at the regulator node:

$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 14.68 - 5.1 = 9.58 \text{ mA}$$

Maximum safe Zener current from power rating:

$$I_{Z(max)} = \frac{P_{ZM}}{V_Z} = \frac{500 \text{ mW}}{5.1 \text{ V}} \approx 98.04 \text{ mA}$$

Since $I_Z = 9.58\text{ mA} \ll I_{Z(max)} = 98.04\text{ mA}$, the Zener is **operating well within safe limits**. ✓

**Power dissipated in Zener:**

$$P_Z = V_Z \times I_Z = 5.1 \times 9.58 \approx 48.86 \text{ mW} \ll 500 \text{ mW}$$

**Result:** $I_S = 14.68$ mA, $I_L = 5.1$ mA, $I_Z = 9.58$ mA — Safe operation confirmed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Plotting the axes backwards:** Always plot $V$ on the **x-axis** and $I$ on the **y-axis**. Reversing this is an automatic 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting units on the axes:** Every axis must have a label (e.g., "Forward Voltage $V_F$ (V)") and a numerical scale.
> 3. **Mixing up static and dynamic resistance formulas:** $R_{DC} = V_D / I_D$ is the **line-to-origin** slope. $r_d = \Delta V_D / \Delta I_D$ is the **tangent slope**. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Not labeling the knee voltage and Zener voltage** on the plotted curve in the lab record.
> 5. **Reversing the Zener diode** in the regulator circuit — a single reversed component yields zero current in the load and full input voltage across the Zener, destroying it.
> 6. **Not including the reverse saturation current** when discussing the reverse characteristics of a regular diode.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **V-I Characteristics** = Graph of diode current $I_D$ vs applied voltage $V_D$ for both forward and reverse bias.
- ✅ **Knee Voltage** = $V_{knee} \approx$ **0.7 V (Si)** / **0.3 V (Ge)** — minimum forward voltage for significant conduction.
- ✅ **Shockley Equation** = $I_D = I_0 \left(e^{V_D/\eta V_T} - 1\right)$; the origin of the exponential forward curve.
- ✅ **Thermal Voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx$ **25 mV at 300 K** — appearing in every AC analysis of diodes.
- ✅ **Static Resistance** $R_{DC} = V_D / I_D$ — Ohm's-law ratio at the operating point.
- ✅ **Dynamic Resistance** $r_d = \eta V_T / I_D \approx 25/I_D(\text{mA})$ — slope of the V-I curve.
- ✅ **At $I_D = 1$ mA, $r_d \approx 25\ \Omega$** — the most common KTU viva question.
- ✅ **Zener Voltage** $V_Z$ = Reverse breakdown voltage at which the Zener clamps — used for voltage regulation.
- ✅ **Always use a series resistor** with the Zener to limit current and prevent thermal runaway.
- ✅ **Zener Power Check** = $P_Z = V_Z \times I_Z$ must be less than the rated $P_{ZM}$.
- ✅ **Zener is connected in REVERSE bias** for regulator operation (cathode to + supply).
- ✅ **Plot axes correctly:** $V$ on x-axis, $I$ on y-axis. Mark the knee voltage and Zener voltage with dashed lines on the graph.
- ✅ **Tabular recording** of $(V_D, I_D)$ pairs is mandatory in the lab record before plotting.
- ✅ **Units must appear on every axis** label, and a proper scale with consistent divisions must be chosen.
- ✅ The **forward region** of the curve is exponential; the **reverse region** is nearly flat at $I_0$ until breakdown; the **Zener breakdown** is a near-vertical cliff at $V = -V_Z$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series Voltage Regulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Series Voltage Regulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Series Voltage Regulator** is a linear voltage regulation topology in which the **pass element (active control device, typically a BJT or power transistor)** is connected in **series** between the unregulated DC input source and the load. The regulator continuously samples the output voltage, compares it against an internal **reference voltage (V_REF)** generated by a Zener diode, and dynamically adjusts the voltage drop across the series pass transistor to maintain a **constant, ripple-free DC output voltage** irrespective of variations in input line voltage or load current demand.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab syllabus (PCICL308), the Series Voltage Regulator experiment forms the cornerstone experiment of Module 2 — *Regulators and Power Supplies* — and directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Design, build, and analyze electronic circuits using discrete semiconductor devices for signal processing and power supply applications."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The series regulator is the building block of the **78xx/79xx** monolithic three-terminal regulators (e.g., LM7805, LM7812). Mastering discrete design is *mandatory* before tackling IC regulators in the theory course.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Variable Faucet" Model

Imagine a water tank supplying water to your home. The water pressure (analogous to the unregulated input DC voltage) keeps fluctuating — sometimes too high, sometimes too low. To keep your shower flow steady (analogous to a constant DC output), you install a **smart, automatic faucet** that:

- **Senses** the output flow rate (sampling network)
- **Compares** it to a reference (Zener diode)
- **Squeezes or releases** the pipe in real time (series pass transistor)

That smart faucet is your **series pass transistor**. The series regulator works on this identical **closed-loop negative feedback** principle. The pass transistor acts like a *variable resistor* in series, dropping whatever excess voltage is needed to keep V_OUT rock-steady.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In a *shunt* regulator, the control element sits in *parallel* with the load (like a bypass valve dumping excess current). In a *series* regulator, the control element sits in *series* with the load (like a restricting valve). Series regulators are far more efficient and hence dominant in real-world power supplies.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

Every KTU lab report must quote these metrics:

- **Line Regulation (LR)** = (ΔV_OUT / ΔV_IN) × 1000 mV/V or in %/V
- **Load Regulation (LDR)** = (V_NL − V_FL) / V_FL × 100 %
- **Ripple Rejection Ratio (PSRR)** = 20·log₁₀(V_ripple_in / V_ripple_out) in dB
- **Efficiency (η)** = (P_OUT / P_IN) × 100 %
- **Dropout Voltage (V_DO)** ≈ 2 V to 3 V for a simple BJT series pass element
- **Standard Zener Voltages Used:** **5.1 V, 5.6 V, 6.2 V, 9.1 V, 12 V**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Five Building Blocks

A textbook series voltage regulator comprises exactly five functional blocks. Memorize them — they appear verbatim in KTU 10-mark theory questions.

1. **Unregulated DC Input (V_IN):** Derived from a transformer + bridge rectifier + smoothing capacitor. Contains a large AC ripple component (typically ±10 % of DC level).
2. **Bias Resistor (R_S):** Limits the current through the Zener diode. Chosen such that I_Z(min) ≤ I_Z ≤ I_Z(max) over all operating conditions.
3. **Zener Reference Diode (D_Z):** Operates in reverse breakdown at V_Z. Provides a *temperature-stable* reference voltage. The brain of the regulator.
4. **Pass Transistor (Q₁ — typically 2N3055, BD139, or TIP31C):** Connected in **common-collector (emitter-follower)** configuration. The muscle — handles the load current.
5. **Load (R_L):** The actual circuit being powered, drawing current I_L.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why emitter follower?** Because the emitter follower offers **high input impedance, low output impedance, and a voltage gain of ≈ 1**, making it the *ideal* buffer between the reference circuit and the load. The output is taken at the emitter (V_E = V_B − V_BE).

## 2.2 Closed-Loop Negative Feedback — The "Why"

Step-by-step operational logic when disturbances occur:

- **Scenario A — Input voltage increases (V_IN ↑):**  
  V_OUT attempts to rise. But the base of Q₁ is clamped at V_Z (by the Zener). Therefore, V_BE = V_B − V_E = V_Z − V_OUT *decreases*. This reduces the base current I_B, reduces I_C, and Q₁ conducts less, dropping more voltage across itself (V_CE ↑). Net effect: V_OUT is restored to its nominal value.

- **Scenario B — Load current increases (I_L ↑):**  
  V_OUT momentarily droops due to the internal source resistance. V_BE = V_Z − V_OUT *increases*. Q₁ conducts harder, V_CE *decreases*, compensating for the droop. V_OUT is restored.

This continuous, automatic correction is **negative feedback** in its purest form and the foundation of *every* linear regulator, op-amp, and control system in existence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Output Voltage | $V_{OUT} = V_Z + V_{BE}$ | Volts (V) | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ for silicon BJT |
| 2 | Zener Current | $I_Z = \dfrac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S} - I_B$ | A | Must satisfy $I_{Z(\min)} \le I_Z \le I_{Z(\max)}$ |
| 3 | Base Current of Pass Transistor | $I_B = \dfrac{I_L}{h_{FE} + 1} \approx \dfrac{I_L}{h_{FE}}$ | A | Choose $h_{FE} \ge 50$ for power BJTs |
| 4 | Voltage across Pass Transistor | $V_{CE} = V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$ | V | Must be $\le V_{CE(\max)}$ rating of Q₁ |
| 5 | Power Dissipated in Pass Transistor | $P_Q = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ | W | Critical for heatsink design |
| 6 | Line Regulation | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}}$ | mV/V or %/V | *Lower* is better |
| 7 | Load Regulation | $LDR = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\ \%$ | % | *Lower* is better |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OUT} \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot I_{IN}} \times 100\ \%$ | % | Typically **30 % – 60 %** for linear regulators |
| 9 | Ripple Rejection | $PSRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{r,in}}{V_{r,out}}\right)$ | dB | Typical: **40 dB – 60 dB** |
| 10 | Dropout Voltage | $V_{DO} = V_{IN(\min)} - V_{OUT}$ | V | Regulator ceases regulation when $V_{IN} < V_{OUT} + V_{DO}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In every formula, the **vertical bar symbol** (e.g., for absolute value) has been intentionally written as `\vert` in the LaTeX source to avoid breaking KTU's markdown renderer. In your exam paper, simply write `|x|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bench power supplies** in every electronics lab (Tektronix, Keysight, Rigol).
- **Pre-regulator stage** before switching-mode power supplies (SMPS) to reduce ripple.
- **Automotive ECU power rails** (12 V → 5 V or 3.3 V).
- **Battery chargers** for laptops and instrumentation.
- **Aircraft avionics** where clean DC is mission-critical.
- **The conceptual ancestor** of every modern op-amp, the **µA723** IC regulator, and the entire **78xx/79xx** family.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage of a Zener-Referenced Series Regulator

**Given Circuit:** Unregulated V_IN → R_S → node V_B (cathode of Zener D_Z) → base of Q₁ → emitter is V_OUT → load R_L to ground. The Zener anode is grounded.

**Step 1 — Identify the node voltages.**  
The base of Q₁ is tied to the cathode of the Zener, hence:

$$
V_B = V_Z
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the BJT emitter-follower relation.**  
The base-emitter junction is forward-biased, so:

$$
V_B - V_{BE} = V_E
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the emitter is the output node.**  
Since the load is connected between the emitter and ground:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_E
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two relations to obtain the canonical formula.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}\,}
$$

Wait — there is a sign convention subtlety. With a **PNP** pass transistor, V_OUT = V_Z + V_BE. With an **NPN** pass transistor in emitter-follower, V_OUT = V_Z − V_BE. Since the standard KTU experiment uses an **NPN BJT (e.g., 2N3055)**, the final canonical expression used in the KTU lab manual is:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}
$$

For $V_Z = 5.6\ \text{V}$ and $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$:

$$
V_{OUT} = 5.6 - 0.7 = 4.9\ \text{V}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selection of the Bias Resistor R_S

R_S must guarantee Zener current stays within its safe operating range across the *entire* envelope of input voltage and load current variation.

**Step 1 — Identify worst-case conditions.**  
- **Maximum** Zener current occurs at **maximum** V_IN and **minimum** load current (lightest load, even no-load).
- **Minimum** Zener current occurs at **minimum** V_IN and **maximum** load current.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the base node.**  
The current through R_S splits into the Zener current and the base current of Q₁:

$$
I_S = I_Z + I_B
$$

**Step 3 — Express I_S via Ohm's law.**

$$
I_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S}
$$

**Step 4 — Set up the two boundary inequalities.**

$$
I_{Z(\min)} \le \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S} - I_B \le I_{Z(\max)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for R_S in each extreme case.**

For the **minimum** R_S (worst-case heating of Zener):

$$
R_{S(\min)} = \frac{V_{IN(\max)} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\max)} + I_B}
$$

For the **maximum** R_S (worst-case Zener losing regulation):

$$
R_{S(\max)} = \frac{V_{IN(\min)} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\min)} + I_B}
$$

**Step 6 — Pick R_S such that the chosen value lies in the interval [R_S(min), R_S(max)].**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always select the **standard E12 / E24 resistor value closest to the geometric mean** of the computed bounds. A wrong R_S is the single most common reason a series regulator fails in KTU lab viva.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Line Regulation and Load Regulation (Numerical Worked Example)

**Given:**  
- $V_{IN}$ varies from **12 V to 15 V** (DC after rectification and filtering).  
- $R_L = 100\ \Omega$, hence nominal $I_L = V_{OUT}/100$.  
- $V_Z = 5.6\ \text{V}$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $h_{FE} = 50$, $R_S = 470\ \Omega$.  
- $V_{OUT(\text{nominal})} = 4.9\ \text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Compute nominal load current.**

$$
I_{L(\text{nom})} = \frac{4.9}{100} = 49\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute base current required by Q₁.**

$$
I_B = \frac{I_L}{h_{FE}} = \frac{49\ \text{mA}}{50} = 0.98\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Zener current at V_IN = 12 V (minimum).**

$$
I_{Z(\min)} = \frac{12 - 5.6}{470} - 0.98 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
I_{Z(\min)} = \frac{6.4}{470} - 0.00098 = 0.01361 - 0.00098 = 12.63\ \text{mA}
$$

This is well above the typical $I_{Z(\min)} = 5\ \text{mA}$ for a 5.6 V Zener, so regulation is maintained.

**Step 4 — Compute Zener current at V_IN = 15 V (maximum).**

$$
I_{Z(\max)} = \frac{15 - 5.6}{470} - 0.00098 = \frac{9.4}{470} - 0.00098
$$

$$
I_{Z(\max)} = 0.02 - 0.00098 \approx 19.02\ \text{mA}
$$

This is below $I_{Z(\max)} = 20\ \text{mA}$ for a standard 0.5 W Zener, so the diode is safe.

**Step 5 — Measure V_OUT at the two extremes** (assume negligible β dependence, emitter-follower gain ≈ 1):

$$
V_{OUT} \approx V_Z - V_{BE} = 4.9\ \text{V}
$$

(approximately constant in both extremes due to feedback).

**Step 6 — Compute Line Regulation.**

$$
LR = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \frac{\vert V_{OUT@15V} - V_{OUT@12V} \vert}{\vert 15 - 12 \vert}
$$

Assume measured $V_{OUT@12V} = 4.89\ \text{V}$ and $V_{OUT@15V} = 4.91\ \text{V}$:

$$
LR = \frac{\vert 4.91 - 4.89 \vert}{3} = \frac{0.02}{3} \approx 6.67\ \text{mV/V}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Load Regulation.**  
Measure $V_{OUT}$ at no-load (I_L ≈ 0) and full-load (I_L = 49 mA).

Let $V_{NL} = 4.95\ \text{V}$ and $V_{FL} = 4.90\ \text{V}$:

$$
LDR = \frac{\vert 4.95 - 4.90 \vert}{4.90} \times 100 \% \approx 1.02\ \%
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Dissipation and Heatsink Requirement

**Step 1 — Power dissipated in pass transistor Q₁.**

$$
P_Q = V_{CE} \cdot I_L = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L
$$

**Step 2 — Worst case (maximum V_IN, maximum I_L).**

$$
P_{Q(\max)} = (15 - 4.9) \cdot 0.049 = 10.1 \cdot 0.049 \approx 0.495\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Q₁'s free-air rating.**  
For a 2N3055, $P_D \approx 115\ \text{W}$ *with infinite heatsink*, but only **1–2 W in free air**. Since 0.495 W < 2 W, no external heatsink is mandatory, but **a clip-on heatsink is best practice** and will fetch full marks in the lab record.

**Step 4 — Thermal resistance calculation (for KTU viva).**  
If the junction temperature must not exceed $T_{J(\max)} = 150\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and ambient is $T_A = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
\theta_{JA(\max)} = \frac{T_{J(\max)} - T_A}{P_{Q(\max)}} = \frac{150 - 40}{0.495} \approx 222\ ^\circ\text{C/W}
$$

For 2N3055, $\theta_{JA} \approx 50\ ^\circ\text{C/W}$ with a small clip-on heatsink, so the design is thermally safe.

## 3.5 Design Checklist Matrix

| Step | Design Action | Governing Equation | Failure Mode if Ignored |
|------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Choose V_Z to set V_OUT | $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$ | Wrong output voltage |
| 2 | Select R_S from bounds | $R_{S(\min)},\ R_{S(\max)}$ | Zener dies or loses regulation |
| 3 | Choose Q₁ with $h_{FE} \ge I_L / I_B$ | $I_B = I_L / h_{FE}$ | Pass transistor saturates |
| 4 | Verify $V_{CE} \le V_{CE(\max)}$ | $V_{CE} = V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$ | Transistor breakdown |
| 5 | Verify $P_D \le P_{Q(\text{rated})}$ | $P_Q = (V_{IN}-V_{OUT}) I_L$ | Thermal runaway |
| 6 | Add heatsink if $P_Q > 1$ W | $\theta_{JA}$ calculation | Device burns out |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Series Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["UNREGULATED DC INPUT V_IN"] -->|DC + Ripple| B["BIAS RESISTOR R_S"]
    B -->|Current I_S| C["ZENER REFERENCE DIODE D_Z"]
    C -->|V_Z = Reference| D["BASE OF PASS TRANSISTOR Q1"]
    D -->|Drives Q1| E["PASS TRANSISTOR Q1 SERIES ELEMENT"]
    E -->|V_OUT Stabilized| F["LOAD R_L"]
    F -->|I_L returns| G["COMMON GROUND"]
    E -.->|Feedback| H["ERROR SENSING NODE V_OUT"]
    H -.->|V_BE Comparison| D
    C -.->|V_Z provides reference| D
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph InputStage["INPUT POWER STAGE"]
        nodeA["Transformer 230V to 12V AC"] --> nodeB["Bridge Rectifier"]
        nodeB --> nodeC["Smoothing Capacitor C_in"]
        nodeC --> nodeD["Unregulated DC V_IN 12V to 15V"]
    end

    subgraph ReferenceStage["REFERENCE VOLTAGE STAGE"]
        nodeD --> nodeE["Bias Resistor R_S 470 ohm"]
        nodeE --> nodeF["Zener Diode D_Z 5.6V"]
        nodeF --> nodeG["Ground"]
    end

    subgraph ControlStage["SERIES PASS STAGE"]
        nodeF --> nodeH["Base of Q1 NPN BJT"]
        nodeD --> nodeI["Collector of Q1"]
        nodeH --> nodeJ["Q1 Emitter Follower"]
        nodeI --> nodeJ
        nodeJ --> nodeK["Output Node V_OUT"]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage["LOAD STAGE"]
        nodeK --> nodeL["Load Resistor R_L"]
        nodeK --> nodeM["Output Capacitor C_out for Ripple Filtering"]
        nodeL --> nodeG
        nodeM --> nodeG
    end

    nodeJ -.Feedback Line.-> nodeF
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Diagram of Feedback Action

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["Disturbance Occurs V_IN rises OR I_L drops"] --> stepB["V_OUT attempts to rise"]
    stepB --> stepC["V_BE of Q1 decreases because V_B is fixed at V_Z"]
    stepC --> stepD["Base current I_B decreases"]
    stepD --> stepE["Collector current I_C decreases"]
    stepE --> stepF["V_CE across Q1 increases"]
    stepF --> stepG["V_OUT pulled back to nominal value"]
    stepG --> stepH["Regulation Restored Negative Feedback Complete"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** When asked to draw the circuit on the record, always mark the three critical voltages explicitly: V_IN (at collector), V_B = V_Z (at base), V_OUT (at emitter), and the relationship V_OUT = V_Z − V_BE. Examiners award 2 marks for this labeling alone.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define a series voltage regulator. Why is a Zener diode used at the base of the series pass transistor?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A series voltage regulator is a linear regulator in which the **pass element (BJT)** is connected in **series** between the unregulated input and the load, maintaining a **constant output voltage** by automatically adjusting its V_CE drop. A Zener diode is used at the base of the pass transistor because it provides a **stable, temperature-independent reference voltage V_Z**. This reference is **independent of input voltage variations** (within the bias limits) and serves as the comparison standard against which V_OUT is regulated. Without the Zener, the base voltage would float with V_IN, defeating the purpose of regulation. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between line regulation and load regulation in a voltage regulator."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Line Regulation | Load Regulation |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Definition | Ability to keep V_OUT constant when V_IN varies | Ability to keep V_OUT constant when I_L varies |
| Formula | $LR = \Delta V_{OUT} / \Delta V_{IN}$ (mV/V) | $LDR = (V_{NL} - V_{FL}) / V_{FL} \times 100\ \%$ |
| Measured by | Varying V_IN at constant I_L | Varying I_L at constant V_IN |
| Ideal value | Zero | Zero |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Zener-diode-based **series voltage regulator**. Derive the expression for the output voltage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a series regulator with $V_Z = 6.2\ \text{V}$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_{IN} = 12\ \text{V}$ (DC), $R_S = 330\ \Omega$, $I_L = 100\ \text{mA}$, and $h_{FE} = 50$, calculate: (i) Output voltage V_OUT, (ii) Base current I_B, (iii) Zener current I_Z, (iv) Power dissipated in the pass transistor.

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Working and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**  
The circuit consists of an unregulated DC input V_IN feeding a bias resistor R_S connected to a Zener diode D_Z (cathode up). The base of an NPN pass transistor Q₁ is tied to the Zener cathode. The collector of Q₁ is tied to V_IN, and the emitter is the V_OUT node feeding the load R_L. (A neat, labeled circuit diagram must accompany — same as the Mermaid flow above in schematic spirit.)

**Working (3 marks):**  
- When V_IN increases, V_OUT tends to rise. But V_B is clamped at V_Z by the Zener.  
- Therefore, V_BE = V_Z − V_OUT *decreases*.  
- This reduces I_B and I_C of Q₁. Q₁ conducts less, V_CE increases, and the excess voltage is dropped across Q₁, restoring V_OUT.  
- The reverse sequence occurs if V_OUT tries to fall.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
V_B = V_Z \quad \text{(Zener in breakdown)}
$$

$$
V_E = V_B - V_{BE} = V_{OUT}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE} = 6.2 - 0.7 = 5.5\ \text{V} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Base Current:**

$$
I_B = \frac{I_L}{h_{FE}} = \frac{100\ \text{mA}}{50} = 2\ \text{mA} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Zener Current:**

$$
I_Z = \frac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S} - I_B
$$

$$
I_Z = \frac{12 - 6.2}{330} - 0.002 = \frac{5.8}{330} - 0.002
$$

$$
I_Z = 0.01758 - 0.002 = 15.58\ \text{mA} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iv) Power Dissipated in Q₁:**

$$
P_Q = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L = (12 - 5.5) \cdot 0.1
$$

$$
P_Q = 6.5 \cdot 0.1 = 0.65\ \text{W} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Conclusion (free 0 mark):** Since $P_Q = 0.65\ \text{W} < 2\ \text{W}$, a small clip-on heatsink is recommended but not strictly mandatory.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **line regulation** and **load regulation** for a DC voltage regulator. How are they measured experimentally in the lab?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A series voltage regulator uses a Zener diode of $V_Z = 5.1\ \text{V}$ and an NPN pass transistor with $V_{BE} = 0.65\ \text{V}$. The unregulated input varies between **10 V and 14 V**. The load current varies from **20 mA to 80 mA**. If $h_{FE(\min)} = 40$, design a suitable **bias resistor R_S** such that $I_{Z(\min)} = 5\ \text{mA}$ and $I_{Z(\max)} = 30\ \text{mA}$. Also calculate the **worst-case power dissipation** in the pass transistor.

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Concept of Line & Load Regulation (7 marks):**

**Line Regulation (3 marks):**  
Line regulation is a measure of the regulator's ability to maintain a **constant output voltage despite variations in the unregulated input (line) voltage**. It is defined as the ratio of the change in output voltage to the change in input voltage, expressed in **mV/V** or **%/V**. Experimentally, V_IN is varied in steps (e.g., 10 V, 12 V, 14 V) using a variable DC source, and V_OUT is measured with a digital multimeter at *constant* I_L (kept fixed by a load resistor). The line regulation is computed as:

$$
LR = \frac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} \quad \text{(mV/V)}
$$

**Load Regulation (3 marks):**  
Load regulation quantifies the change in V_OUT when the **load current** is varied, with V_IN held constant. It is expressed as a **percentage**. Experimentally, the load is varied (e.g., by switching load resistors or using a rheostat), and V_OUT is noted at no-load (V_NL) and full-load (V_FL). Load regulation is:

$$
LDR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\ \%
$$

**Lab Setup Note (1 mark):** A regulated bench DC source supplies V_IN, a digital multimeter measures V_OUT, and an ammeter in series with the load measures I_L.

**Part (b) — Design of R_S and Power Dissipation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute nominal V_OUT and I_B(max):**

$$
V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE} = 5.1 - 0.65 = 4.45\ \text{V}
$$

$$
I_{B(\max)} = \frac{I_{L(\max)}}{h_{FE(\min)}} = \frac{80\ \text{mA}}{40} = 2\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute R_S(min) using V_IN(max) and I_Z(max):**

$$
R_{S(\min)} = \frac{V_{IN(\max)} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\max)} + I_{B(\max)}} = \frac{14 - 5.1}{(30 + 2) \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{8.9}{0.032}
$$

$$
R_{S(\min)} = 278.1\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute R_S(max) using V_IN(min) and I_Z(min):**

$$
R_{S(\max)} = \frac{V_{IN(\min)} - V_Z}{I_{Z(\min)} + I_{B(\max)}} = \frac{10 - 5.1}{(5 + 2) \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4.9}{0.007}
$$

$$
R_{S(\max)} = 700\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose standard E12 value.**  
The valid range is $278.1\ \Omega \le R_S \le 700\ \Omega$. The closest E12 value is **330 Ω** (or 470 Ω). Choose **R_S = 470 Ω** for safety margin. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Worst-case power dissipation in Q₁:**  
Worst case is **maximum V_IN** and **maximum I_L**:

$$
P_{Q(\max)} = (V_{IN(\max)} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{L(\max)}
$$

$$
P_{Q(\max)} = (14 - 4.45) \cdot 0.080 = 9.55 \cdot 0.080 = 0.764\ \text{W}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** A small aluminum clip-on heatsink (thermal resistance ≤ 50 °C/W) is recommended to keep the junction temperature within safe limits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the sign in V_OUT = V_Z ± V_BE.** Always check whether the pass transistor is NPN (use minus) or PNP (use plus). Wrong sign = 0 marks for derivation.
> 2. **Forgetting the I_B term in the Zener current calculation.** Many students write I_Z = (V_IN − V_Z)/R_S alone, ignoring the base current drawn by Q₁. This loses 2 marks in the numerical.
> 3. **Not justifying the choice of R_S from the E12/E24 standard series.** Picking an arbitrary value like 1000 Ω without checking the bounds is a *very common* error.
> 4. **Omitting units** (V, mA, mV/V, %) in the final answer. KTU examiners *strictly* deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Drawing the Zener with anode up** in the circuit. The Zener must be **reverse-biased**, i.e., cathode towards R_S and anode to ground. Reversed Zener = regulator fails in practice = 1 mark lost for wrong diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Series Regulator Definition:** Pass element (BJT) is in **series** with the load; output is taken at the **emitter** (emitter-follower configuration).
- **Canonical Output Equation:** $V_{OUT} = V_Z - V_{BE}$ for NPN; $V_{OUT} = V_Z + V_{BE}$ for PNP. (For KTU, default to NPN → minus sign.)
- **Bias Resistor R_S:** Must satisfy $R_{S(\min)} \le R_S \le R_{S(\max)}$; compute using $I_{Z(\min)}$ and $I_{Z(\max)}$ boundaries; choose nearest **E12/E24** standard value.
- **Zener Current Formula:** $I_Z = (V_{IN} - V_Z)/R_S - I_B$ — *never* forget the $I_B$ term.
- **Line Regulation:** $LR = \Delta V_{OUT} / \Delta V_{IN}$ in mV/V — measured by varying V_IN at constant I_L.
- **Load Regulation:** $LDR = (V_{NL} - V_{FL})/V_{FL} \times 100\ \%$ — measured by varying I_L at constant V_IN.
- **Power in Pass Transistor:** $P_Q = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ — heatsink mandatory if $P_Q > 1\ \text{W}$.
- **Efficiency:** Always less than **60 %** for a simple linear regulator; degrades further as the headroom $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT})$ grows.
- **Dropout Voltage:** Typically **2 V to 3 V** for a BJT emitter follower — the minimum $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT})$ below which regulation fails.
- **Common KTU Lab Components:** 2N3055 / BD139 / TIP31C (pass transistor), 5.6 V / 6.2 V / 9.1 V Zener, 1N4007 rectifier, electrolytic filter caps.
- **Ripple Rejection:** Improved by adding an output filter capacitor C_OUT (typically 100 µF to 470 µF) — typical PSRR: **40 dB to 60 dB**.
- **Stability Caveat:** Adding C_OUT can cause oscillations due to Q₁'s transit capacitance — for IC regulators, the manufacturer datasheet specifies the safe C_OUT range. In discrete designs, use C_OUT ≤ 100 µF and place a small series resistor (≈ 1 Ω) if needed.
- **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, excellent line/load regulation, low ripple, fast transient response.
- **Disadvantages:** Low efficiency (heat), bulky heatsink required at high current, dropout voltage limits minimum V_IN, no short-circuit protection (must add a foldback or current-limit transistor externally).
- **Modern Evolution:** The discrete series regulator is the *direct ancestor* of three-terminal IC regulators **LM78xx, LM317, LM1117**, and low-dropout (LDO) regulators like **LP2985, MIC5205**. Mastering this experiment gives you a 30-year conceptual head-start in power electronics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectifier Circuits: Half Wave, Full Wave and Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Rectifier Circuits: Half Wave, Full Wave and Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rectifier** is an electronic circuit that converts an alternating quantity (AC voltage or current) into a unidirectional (DC) quantity. The process of conversion is known as **Rectification**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab syllabus, rectifier circuits are realized using **uncontrolled solid-state switching elements** — namely the **p-n junction diode** — which conduct only during the positive half-cycle of the input AC signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCICL308 - Module 2):**
> *Rectification is the process of converting bidirectional AC voltage into unidirectional pulsating DC voltage using the unidirectional conduction property of p-n junction diodes.*

The three standard topologies examined under Module Experiment 2 are:

| # | Rectifier Type | Diode Count | Transformer Requirement |
|---|----------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR)** | 1 | Step-down transformer with single secondary |
| 2 | **Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier (FWCT)** | 2 | Step-down transformer with **center-tapped** secondary |
| 3 | **Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (FWB)** | 4 | Step-down transformer with single secondary |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Flow Check Valve"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine a water pipeline carrying water that sloshes back and forth (alternating direction). A **check valve** allows water to flow in only one direction. Similarly, a **diode** in a rectifier allows current to flow in only one direction, "stripping off" the unwanted reverse-flowing half of the AC waveform.

- **Half-wave rectifier** = single check valve that lets only the forward half of the water pass — wasteful, but simple.
- **Full-wave center-tapped** = two valves operating alternately on a **split pipeline**, both pushing water into a common tank.
- **Bridge rectifier** = four valves arranged in a **diamond bridge** that redirect both halves of the sloshing water into the tank — no pipeline split needed, most efficient.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following **figure-of-merit parameters** are universally assessed in the KTU lab viva and examination:

- **V_m** = Peak (maximum) value of the AC input voltage (**Volts**)
- **V_dc / I_dc** = Average (DC) value of output voltage/current
- **V_rms / I_rms** = Root-Mean-Square value of output voltage/current
- **Ripple Factor (γ or Γ)** = Measure of the remaining AC content in the output
- **Rectification Efficiency (η)** = Ratio of DC output power to AC input power
- **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)** = Maximum reverse voltage a diode must withstand
- **Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF)** = Effectiveness of transformer usage
- **f_r** = Ripple frequency (fundamental frequency of output ripple)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR) — Step-by-Step Operation

The half-wave rectifier uses **a single diode** in series with the load resistor **R_L** across the transformer secondary.

**During the Positive Half-Cycle (0 to π):**
- The anode of the diode is positive with respect to its cathode.
- The diode is **forward biased** → it conducts → current flows through R_L.
- Output voltage v_o = v_s − V_D (where V_D ≈ 0.7 V for silicon).

**During the Negative Half-Cycle (π to 2π):**
- The anode becomes negative relative to the cathode.
- The diode is **reverse biased** → it does **NOT** conduct → output is zero.
- The **entire transformer secondary voltage appears across the diode** as reverse bias → this defines the PIV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Output Frequency:** The output ripple frequency equals the input AC frequency, i.e., **f_r = f_in**. This is the lowest possible ripple frequency and results in the **highest ripple content** (worst DC purity).

## 2.2 Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier (FWCT) — Step-by-Step Operation

The full-wave center-tapped rectifier uses **two identical diodes (D1 and D2)** and a transformer with a **center tap** on the secondary winding. The load R_L is connected between the **center tap** and the **common cathode junction** of the two diodes.

**During the Positive Half-Cycle:**
- The upper half of the secondary winding is positive → **D1 is forward biased** and conducts.
- The lower half is negative → **D2 is reverse biased** and is OFF.
- Current flows through the upper half → through D1 → through R_L → back to center tap.
- Output voltage v_o = v_s − V_D.

**During the Negative Half-Cycle:**
- The lower half of the secondary becomes positive → **D2 is forward biased** and conducts.
- The upper half is negative → **D1 is reverse biased** and is OFF.
- Current flows through the lower half → through D2 → through R_L → back to center tap.
- Output voltage v_o = v_s − V_D (same polarity as before).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output Frequency:** The output ripple frequency is **DOUBLE** the input AC frequency: **f_r = 2 × f_in = 100 Hz** (for 50 Hz mains). This is why full-wave rectifiers are far superior — the ripple is easier to filter out.

## 2.3 Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (FWB) — Step-by-Step Operation

The bridge rectifier uses **four diodes (D1, D2, D3, D4)** arranged in a **bridge (Graetz) configuration**. **No center tap is required** on the transformer secondary — this is its biggest practical advantage.

**During the Positive Half-Cycle:**
- Terminal A of the secondary is positive; terminal B is negative.
- Current path: **A → D1 → R_L → D3 → B**.
- Diodes **D1 and D3 conduct**; D2 and D4 are reverse biased.

**During the Negative Half-Cycle:**
- Terminal A becomes negative; terminal B becomes positive.
- Current path: **B → D2 → R_L → D4 → A**.
- Diodes **D2 and D4 conduct**; D1 and D3 are reverse biased.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Lab Viva:** In each conducting half-cycle, current passes through **TWO diodes in series**, so the total diode drop is **2 × V_D (≈ 1.4 V)**. This is a small but real efficiency loss compared to the center-tapped design (which uses only one diode per half-cycle).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-ready summary — memorize before every lab exam and viva.**

| **Parameter** | **Half-Wave Rectifier** | **Full-Wave (Center-Tap)** | **Full-Wave (Bridge)** |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Number of Diodes** | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| **V_dc (Average Output Voltage)** | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ |
| **V_rms (RMS Output Voltage)** | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| **I_dc = V_dc / R_L** | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi R_L}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi R_L}$ |
| **I_rms = V_rms / R_L** | $\dfrac{V_m}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}\, R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}\, R_L}$ |
| **Ripple Factor γ** | $1.21$ | $0.482$ | $0.482$ |
| **Ripple Frequency f_r** | $f_{in}$ | $2 f_{in}$ | $2 f_{in}$ |
| **Rectification Efficiency η** | $40.6\,\%$ | $81.2\,\%$ | $81.2\,\%$ |
| **PIV (per diode)** | $V_m$ | $2 V_m$ | $V_m$ |
| **Diode drop per conduction** | $V_D$ | $V_D$ | $2 V_D$ |
| **TUF (DC)** | $0.286$ | $0.692$ (CT secondary) / $0.810$ (primary) | $0.810$ |
| **Transformer Required** | Standard | **Center-tapped** secondary | Standard (no CT) |

**Boundary Conditions / Engineering Assumptions:**

- Assume **ideal diodes** (V_D = 0) unless explicitly told to include silicon drop (V_D = 0.7 V).
- V_m = √2 × V_rms(secondary) = √2 × (V_secondary, rated).
- For silicon diodes, **V_D ≈ 0.7 V** is the standard threshold.
- **PIV calculation:** For bridge rectifiers, even though two diodes conduct, **each individual diode still sees only V_m** as reverse voltage when the other pair conducts.
- **Ripple factor formula (universal):** $\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{I_{rms}}{I_{dc}}\right)^{2} - 1}$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Rectifier circuits form the **front-end of every linear DC power supply** — the building block of:
> - Mobile phone chargers, laptop adapters (SMPS primary stage)
> - Battery charging circuits (e-bikes, inverters)
> - DC motor drives in industrial automation
> - Signal demodulation in AM radio receivers (envelope detector)
> - Laboratory bench power supplies used in every KTU electronics lab

The **bridge rectifier** is the **industry default** because it requires no center-tapped transformer — meaning the transformer is cheaper, smaller, and lighter, while the PIV stress on each diode is **halved** compared to the center-tapped design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of V_dc for Half-Wave Rectifier

The instantaneous output voltage during one complete period (0 to 2π) of a half-wave rectifier is:

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t) & \text{for } 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ 0 & \text{for } \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Definition of Average (DC) Value**

The average value of any periodic waveform over one period T is defined as:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the piecewise waveform**

Since the output is zero during the negative half-cycle, the integral reduces to the positive half-cycle only:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta$$

(We used T = 2π and substituted θ = ωt, so dθ = ω·dt, giving dt = dθ/ω, and the factor 1/T = 1/(2π) when expressed in terms of θ.)

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[-\cos(\theta)\Big]_{0}^{\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Apply limits**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[-\cos(\pi) - \big(-\cos(0)\big)\Big]$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[-(-1) - (-1)\Big] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{V_m}{\pi}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318\, V_m}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of V_dc for Full-Wave Rectifier

For both full-wave topologies (center-tapped and bridge), the output is the **absolute value** of the input sinusoid — both half-cycles contribute equally:

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t) & \text{for } 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ -V_m \sin(\omega t) & \text{for } \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Average value over one full period**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \vert v_o(t) \vert\, d\theta$$

**Step 2 — Use symmetry (both half-cycles are identical mirror images)**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos(\theta)\Big]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.636\, V_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The full-wave rectifier produces **exactly twice** the average DC voltage of the half-wave rectifier, because both halves of the AC cycle contribute to the output.

## 3.3 Derivation of V_rms for Half-Wave Rectifier

**Step 1 — RMS Definition**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o^2(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\theta)\, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity** $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}\, d\theta = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \Big[\theta - \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{2}\Big]_{0}^{\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate limits (sin(2π) = sin(0) = 0)**

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \cdot \pi = \frac{V_m^2}{4}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of V_rms for Full-Wave Rectifier

By symmetry (both half-cycles contribute), the squaring doubles the integral:

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\theta)\, d\theta = \frac{V_m^2}{2}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Ripple Factor (γ)

**Universal formula (applies to all rectifiers):**

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac,\,rms}}{V_{dc}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^{2} - 1}$$

where $V_{ac,rms} = \sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2}$ is the RMS value of the **AC ripple component alone**.

**For Half-Wave Rectifier:**

$$\gamma_{HWR} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_m / 2}{V_m / \pi}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{4} - 1}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{HWR} \approx 1.21 \;(\text{or } 121\%)}$$

**For Full-Wave Rectifier (both topologies):**

$$\gamma_{FW} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_m / \sqrt{2}}{2 V_m / \pi}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{FW} \approx 0.482 \;(\text{or } 48.2\%)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lower ripple factor = smoother DC output.** The full-wave rectifier has **less than half** the ripple content of a half-wave rectifier — this is a major reason for its universal adoption.

## 3.6 Derivation of Rectification Efficiency (η)

$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc}\, I_{dc}}{V_{rms}\, I_{rms}} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R_L}{V_{rms}^2 / R_L} = \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{2}$$

**For Half-Wave:**

$$\eta_{HWR} = \left(\frac{V_m/\pi}{V_m/2}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{\pi^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{HWR} \approx 0.4056 = 40.6\%}$$

**For Full-Wave:**

$$\eta_{FW} = \left(\frac{2 V_m / \pi}{V_m / \sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FW} \approx 0.8118 = 81.2\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **theoretical maximum** efficiency of any rectifier is **81.2 %** for full-wave and **40.6 %** for half-wave. Real circuits achieve slightly less due to diode drops, transformer losses, and source impedance.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Lab Style)

**Problem Statement:** A **bridge rectifier** is supplied by a transformer with secondary RMS voltage of **12 V** and is connected to a load resistor **R_L = 100 Ω**. The diodes are silicon (V_D = 0.7 V). Calculate:
(a) Peak voltage V_m
(b) DC output voltage V_dc
(c) RMS output voltage V_rms
(d) Ripple factor γ
(e) DC load current I_dc
(f) PIV rating of each diode

**Solution:**

**(a) Peak Voltage**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times V_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \times 12 = 16.97\, V$$

**(b) DC Output Voltage** (accounting for two diode drops in series during each half-cycle)

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2(V_m - 2V_D)}{\pi} = \frac{2(16.97 - 1.4)}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 15.57}{\pi} = 9.91\, V$$

**(c) RMS Output Voltage**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m - 2V_D}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{15.57}{\sqrt{2}} = 11.01\, V$$

**(d) Ripple Factor**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{11.01}{9.91}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{1.234 - 1} = \sqrt{0.234} \approx 0.484$$

**(e) DC Load Current**

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L} = \frac{9.91}{100} = 99.1\, mA$$

**(f) PIV per Diode**

$$\text{PIV} = V_m = 16.97\, V \;(\text{safe rating: choose a diode with PIV} \geq 25\, V)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Half-Wave Rectifier — Functional Architecture

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards applied: alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no internal pipes. -->

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACsrc["AC SOURCE 230V 50Hz"] --> TR1["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER"]
    TR1 --> SEC["SECONDARY Vrms"]
    SEC --> NODE_A["NODE A"]
    NODE_A --> DIODE_H["DIODE D ANODE"]
    DIODE_H --> NODE_C["NODE C CATHODE"]
    NODE_C --> RL_H["LOAD RESISTOR RL"]
    RL_H --> CT_H["CENTER TAP / GROUND"]
    CT_H --> SEC
    NODE_C --> OUT_H["DC OUTPUT Vdc PULSATING"]
    NODE_A --> PIV_H["REVERSE BIAS PIV EQUALS Vm DURING NEG HALF"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** AC mains → transformer steps down to low voltage → diode passes only the positive half → load sees pulsating DC. During the negative half, the diode blocks and the full V_m appears across it as reverse voltage (PIV).

## 4.2 Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACsrc2["AC SOURCE 230V 50Hz"] --> TR2["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER WITH CENTER TAP"]
    TR2 --> TOP["UPPER HALF SECONDARY"]
    TR2 --> BOT["LOWER HALF SECONDARY"]
    TR2 --> CT2["CENTER TAP NODE GROUND REFERENCE"]
    TOP --> D1_FW["DIODE D1 ANODE"]
    D1_FW --> COM_CATH["COMMON CATHODE NODE"]
    BOT --> D2_FW["DIODE D2 ANODE"]
    COM_CATH --> RL_FW["LOAD RESISTOR RL"]
    RL_FW --> CT2
    COM_CATH --> VDC_OUT["DC OUTPUT Vdc SMOOTHER THAN HWR"]
    PIV_NOTE["PIV PER DIODE EQUALS 2 Vm"]
    D1_FW -.-> PIV_NOTE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The center-tap splits the secondary into two equal halves. D1 fires during positive half-cycle (using upper half), D2 fires during negative half-cycle (using lower half). Both deliver current to R_L in the same direction. PIV per diode is **2 V_m** because when one diode conducts, the OTHER diode sees the voltage of BOTH halves in reverse.

## 4.3 Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACsrc3["AC SOURCE 230V 50Hz"] --> TR3["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER NO CENTER TAP"]
    TR3 --> TERM_A["TERMINAL A"]
    TR3 --> TERM_B["TERMINAL B"]
    TERM_A --> D1B["DIODE D1"]
    D1B --> PNODE["POSITIVE DC BUS"]
    TERM_B --> D3B["DIODE D3"]
    D3B --> PNODE
    TERM_A --> D2B["DIODE D2"]
    TERM_B --> D4B["DIODE D4"]
    D2B --> NNODER["NEGATIVE DC BUS"]
    D4B --> NNODER
    PNODE --> RL_B["LOAD RESISTOR RL"]
    RL_B --> NNODER
    RL_B --> VDC_B["DC OUTPUT Vdc"]
    PIV_B["PIV PER DIODE EQUALS Vm ONLY"]
    D1B -.-> PIV_B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** No center tap needed. During positive half-cycle, current flows A → D1 → Load → D3 → B. During negative half-cycle, current flows B → D2 → Load → D4 → A. Each diode sees only **V_m** as reverse bias (much lower PIV stress than the center-tapped design).

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — All Three Topologies

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["RECTIFIER CIRCUIT SELECTION DECISION"]
    START --> Q1["IS INPUT AC MAINS AVAILABLE"]
    Q1 --> YES["YES USE TRANSFORMER"]
    Q1 --> NO["NO USE DIODE DIRECTLY LOW VOLTAGE"]
    YES --> Q2["IS CENTER TAP AVAILABLE ON TRANSFORMER"]
    Q2 --> YCT["YES USE FWCT RECTIFIER 2 DIODES"]
    Q2 --> NCT["NO USE BRIDGE RECTIFIER 4 DIODES"]
    NO --> HWR["USE HALF WAVE RECTIFIER 1 DIODE"]
    YCT --> OUT1["OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS 2Vm BY PI"]
    NCT --> OUT2["OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS 2Vm BY PI LESS 2 VD DROP"]
    HWR --> OUT3["OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS Vm BY PI HALF POWER WASTED"]
```

## 4.5 Output Waveform Comparison (ASCII Visualisation)

```
HALF-WAVE OUTPUT (only +ve half appears)
   v_o
    |   ___                         ___
    |  /   \                       /   \
    | /     \                     /     \
    |/       \                   /       \
    +---------\-----------------/---------+---> t
    0         \                 /
              |    ___         |   ___
              |   /   \        |  /   \
              |  /     \       | /     \
              | /       \      |/       \
              +----------\-----+----------+---> 0
              π         2π    3π        4π

FULL-WAVE OUTPUT (both halves appear, flipped up)
   v_o
    |   ___       ___       ___       ___
    |  /   \     /   \     /   \     /   \
    | /     \   /     \   /     \   /     \
    |/       \_/       \_/       \_/       \_
    +---------+---------+---------+---------+---> t
    0        π/2       π       3π/2      2π
```
