# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define rectification efficiency and ripple factor. Why is the full-wave bridge rectifier preferred over the half-wave rectifier in DC power supply design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Rectification Efficiency (η)** is the ratio of DC output power delivered to the load to the AC input power supplied: $\eta = P_{dc}/P_{ac} = (V_{dc}/V_{rms})^2$. **(1 mark)**
- **Ripple Factor (γ)** is the ratio of RMS value of the AC ripple component to the DC component: $\gamma = V_{ac,rms}/V_{dc} = \sqrt{(V_{rms}/V_{dc})^2 - 1}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Comparison:** Half-wave gives η = 40.6 %, γ = 1.21. Full-wave bridge gives η = 81.2 %, γ = 0.482 — exactly **double the efficiency** and **less than half the ripple**, with no need for a center-tapped transformer. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)? Compare the PIV rating required for diodes in a center-tapped full-wave rectifier versus a bridge rectifier for the same secondary voltage V_m.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **PIV** is the maximum reverse voltage that a diode can safely withstand without breaking down. **(1 mark)**
- **Center-tapped rectifier:** When one diode conducts (say D1), the other diode (D2) is reverse biased and sees the voltage of BOTH halves of the secondary in series. Hence **PIV = 2 V_m**. **(1 mark)**
- **Bridge rectifier:** Each diode when reverse biased sees only the secondary voltage across it. Hence **PIV = V_m**. **(1 mark)**
- **Conclusion:** Bridge rectifier requires diodes with **half the PIV rating**, making it cheaper and safer.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to subtract the diode drops** (V_D = 0.7 V) in numerical problems → loses 1–2 marks.
> - For **bridge rectifiers**, remember the **2 V_D drop** per half-cycle (because two diodes conduct in series) — common mistake to use only 1 V_D.
> - Always **state the formulas first** before substituting values — KTU examiners award marks even if the final arithmetic slips.
> - In waveform sketches, **label both axes (voltage and time), the peak V_m, the period T, and clearly mark the conduction intervals** for each diode.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **full-wave bridge rectifier** with a sinusoidal input. Sketch the input and output waveforms and indicate the conduction intervals of each diode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **bridge rectifier** is supplied from a 230 V, 50 Hz AC mains through a step-down transformer with turns ratio 10:1. The load resistance is **R_L = 1 kΩ**. Assuming ideal silicon diodes (V_D = 0.7 V), calculate:
(i) Peak secondary voltage V_m
(ii) DC output voltage V_dc
(iii) DC load current I_dc
(iv) RMS ripple voltage V_ac,rms
(v) Ripple factor γ
(vi) PIV rating required for each diode. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Circuit Operation and Waveforms (7 marks):**

The bridge rectifier consists of **four diodes D1, D2, D3, D4** connected in a closed-loop (bridge) configuration with the **transformer secondary connected across one diagonal** and the **load resistor R_L connected across the other diagonal**.

**Positive Half-Cycle (0 to π):**
- Terminal A is +, Terminal B is −.
- D1 is forward biased (anode at A, cathode at top of load).
- D3 is forward biased (anode at bottom of load, cathode at B).
- D2 and D4 are reverse biased.
- **Current path:** A → D1 → R_L → D3 → B.
- Output v_o is positive across R_L.

**Negative Half-Cycle (π to 2π):**
- Terminal A is −, Terminal B is +.
- D2 is forward biased (anode at B, cathode at top of load).
- D4 is forward biased (anode at bottom of load, cathode at A).
- D1 and D3 are reverse biased.
- **Current path:** B → D2 → R_L → D4 → A.
- Output v_o is still positive across R_L (same polarity).

**Waveform:** Both halves appear across R_L as positive humps → **pulsating DC** with frequency **2 f_in = 100 Hz**. **(Full marks for circuit + 2 sets of waveforms + labelled axes = 7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** V_primary = 230 V, turns ratio n = 10:1, f = 50 Hz, R_L = 1 kΩ = 1000 Ω, V_D = 0.7 V (silicon).

**(i) Peak Secondary Voltage V_m**

First compute secondary RMS voltage:

$$V_{sec,rms} = \frac{V_{primary}}{n} = \frac{230}{10} = 23\, V$$

Then the peak:

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times V_{sec,rms} = \sqrt{2} \times 23 = 32.53\, V$$

**(ii) DC Output Voltage V_dc**

For a bridge rectifier, two diodes conduct in series per half-cycle, so the effective peak across the load is (V_m − 2V_D):

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2(V_m - 2V_D)}{\pi} = \frac{2(32.53 - 1.4)}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 31.13}{3.1416} = \frac{62.26}{3.1416} = 19.82\, V$$

**(iii) DC Load Current I_dc**

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L} = \frac{19.82}{1000} = 19.82\, mA$$

**(iv) RMS Ripple Voltage V_ac,rms**

First compute the RMS load voltage:

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m - 2V_D}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{31.13}{\sqrt{2}} = 22.01\, V$$

Then the ripple voltage:

$$V_{ac,rms} = \sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2} = \sqrt{(22.01)^2 - (19.82)^2} = \sqrt{484.44 - 392.83} = \sqrt{91.61} = 9.57\, V$$

**(v) Ripple Factor γ**

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{dc}} = \frac{9.57}{19.82} = 0.483 \;(\approx 48.3\,\%)$$

**(vi) PIV per Diode**

$$\text{PIV} = V_m = 32.53\, V$$

So select diodes with PIV rating **≥ 50 V** for safety margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **half-wave rectifier** with a capacitor filter. Explain its operation and show with a neat sketch how the capacitor reduces ripple. Compare the filtered output with and without the capacitor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **full-wave center-tapped rectifier** uses a transformer with secondary RMS voltage of **50 V (total secondary, end-to-end)**. The load is **R_L = 500 Ω** and silicon diodes are used (V_D = 0.7 V). Calculate:
(i) RMS voltage of each half of the secondary
(ii) V_m per half
(iii) DC output voltage V_dc
(iv) DC power delivered to load P_dc
(v) AC power input to the secondary P_ac
(vi) Rectification efficiency η. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Half-Wave Rectifier with Capacitor Filter (7 marks):**

**Circuit:** A diode D in series with R_L, with a **filter capacitor C** connected in parallel across R_L.

**Operation:**
- During the positive half-cycle, when the diode conducts, the capacitor charges up to nearly the peak voltage V_m (minus V_D).
- When the input falls below V_C, the diode becomes reverse biased and **disconnects the source from the load**.
- The capacitor now **discharges slowly through R_L**, maintaining the output voltage close to V_m.
- During the next positive peak (next cycle), the diode re-conducts briefly to **replenish the capacitor charge lost during discharge**.

**Waveform:** Output is a **slowly decaying sawtooth** with small ripple excursions between peaks, instead of a full sine-pulse without the filter.

**Comparison:**
- Without capacitor: output is pure pulsating DC with ripple factor 1.21.
- With capacitor: ripple factor becomes very small (γ = 1 / (4√3 f R_L C) for full-wave with C-filter) — smoother DC.
- Trade-off: Larger C → less ripple but bigger physical size, higher inrush current through diode at each peak.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** Total secondary RMS = 50 V (end-to-end across both halves), R_L = 500 Ω, V_D = 0.7 V.

**(i) RMS voltage of each half of the secondary**

Since the secondary is center-tapped, the total 50 V is shared between the two equal halves:

$$V_{half,rms} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\, V$$

**(ii) Peak voltage V_m per half**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times V_{half,rms} = \sqrt{2} \times 25 = 35.36\, V$$

**(iii) DC Output Voltage V_dc**

Only one diode conducts per half-cycle, so subtract only one V_D:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2(V_m - V_D)}{\pi} = \frac{2(35.36 - 0.7)}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 34.66}{3.1416} = \frac{69.32}{3.1416} = 22.06\, V$$

**(iv) DC Power Delivered to Load P_dc**

$$P_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{R_L} = \frac{(22.06)^2}{500} = \frac{486.6}{500} = 0.973\, W = 973\, mW$$

**(v) AC Power Input to Secondary P_ac**

First compute I_dc and I_rms:

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L} = \frac{22.06}{500} = 44.12\, mA$$

$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_m - V_D}{\sqrt{2}\, R_L} = \frac{34.66}{\sqrt{2} \times 500} = \frac{34.66}{707.1} = 49.02\, mA$$

Then P_ac:

$$P_{ac} = I_{rms}^2 \times R_L = (49.02 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 500 = 2.4028 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 = 1.201\, W$$

**(vi) Rectification Efficiency η**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.973}{1.201} \times 100\% = 81.0\,\%$$

(This matches the theoretical 81.2 % closely — small discrepancy due to V_D inclusion.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — must know before every KTU lab exam / viva:**

- ☐ A rectifier **converts AC to pulsating DC** using the unidirectional property of p-n junction diodes.
- ☐ **Half-wave** uses **1 diode**, output frequency = **f_in**, efficiency = **40.6 %**, ripple factor = **1.21**, PIV = **V_m**.
- ☐ **Full-wave center-tapped** uses **2 diodes**, needs **center-tapped transformer**, output frequency = **2 f_in**, efficiency = **81.2 %**, ripple factor = **0.482**, PIV per diode = **2 V_m**, one V_D drop per cycle.
- ☐ **Full-wave bridge** uses **4 diodes**, no center tap needed, output frequency = **2 f_in**, efficiency = **81.2 %**, ripple factor = **0.482**, PIV per diode = **V_m**, **2 V_D drops per cycle**.
- ☐ Master formulas: $V_{dc} = V_m/\pi$ (HWR) and $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$ (full-wave), $V_{rms} = V_m/2$ (HWR) and $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ (FW).
- ☐ Bridge rectifier is the **industry standard** for DC power supplies due to (a) lower PIV per diode, (b) no center tap required (cheaper transformer), (c) full utilization of transformer secondary → higher TUF ≈ 0.81.
- ☐ In lab experiments, **always include the silicon diode drop V_D = 0.7 V** unless explicitly told "ideal diodes".
- ☐ **TUF (Transformer Utilization Factor)** = DC power delivered / (VA rating of transformer). HWR TUF = 0.286, FWCT TUF = 0.692, Bridge TUF = 0.810.
- ☐ For waveform sketches: **always label axes**, mark V_m, π, 2π, conduction intervals, and zero-output regions for off-diodes.
- ☐ Universal ripple factor formula: $\gamma = \sqrt{(V_{rms}/V_{dc})^2 - 1}$.
- ☐ Universal efficiency formula: $\eta = (V_{dc}/V_{rms})^2$.
- ☐ **Filter capacitor C** dramatically reduces ripple; ripple voltage ≈ $V_{ripple} \approx I_{dc} / (f_r C)$ — larger C and higher f_r mean smaller ripple.
- ☐ Ripple frequency f_r is **the most reliable way to distinguish** a half-wave (50 Hz) from a full-wave (100 Hz) output on an oscilloscope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filters: RC Low Pass and High Pass Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Filters: RC Low Pass and High Pass Filter ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) An **RC Filter** is a fundamental first-order linear passive filter network composed of a single resistor (**R**) and a single capacitor (**C**) arranged in either a series or shunt configuration. It selectively allows signals of certain frequency ranges to pass through while attenuating (weakening) others, governed by the frequency-dependent impedance of the capacitor. Two basic variants exist: - **RC Low Pass Filter (RC-LPF):** A filter that passes signals with frequencies **lower** than its cutoff frequency ($f_c$) and attenuates signals with frequencies **higher** than $f_c$. The output is taken across the **capacitor**. - **RC High Pass Filter (RC-HPF):** A filter that passes signals with frequencies **higher** than its cutoff frequency ($f_c$) and attenuates signals with frequencies **lower** than $f_c$. The output is taken across the **resistor**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCICL308 - Module 2):** Students must experimentally verify the frequency response of both the **RC Low Pass Filter** and **RC High Pass Filter** by measuring the gain (in dB) and phase shift at multiple input frequencies using a function generator and CRO/DSO. The cutoff frequency must be verified to occur at the **−3 dB point** of the magnitude response. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition Imagine a **garden hose with a small mesh strainer** at the end: - A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** behaves like a **strainer that blocks large debris (high-frequency noise/spikes)** but lets the smooth, gentle flow of water (low-frequency base signal) pass through. The capacitor in an RC-LPF acts like a "smoothing bucket" — it stores charge and resists rapid voltage changes, effectively "straining out" fast fluctuations. - A **High Pass Filter (HPF)** behaves like a **speed bump that only lets fast-moving cars pass**, while slow, heavy trucks are blocked. The capacitor in an RC-HPF blocks slow DC or low-frequency components (acts as an open circuit at DC) but freely passes high-frequency AC signals. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Cutoff frequency | $f_c$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | | Angular cutoff frequency | $\omega_c$ | **Radians/second (rad/s)** | | Filter gain at cutoff | $A_{fc}$ | **−3 dB (0.707 of input)** | | Time constant | $\tau = RC$ | **Seconds (s)** | | Capacitive reactance | $X_C$ | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** | > [!NOTE] > The **−3 dB point** is also called the **half-power point** because the output power drops to exactly **50%** of the maximum (mid-band) power. This occurs at $f = f_c$ where the magnitude response equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown #### **A. RC Low Pass Filter (LPF) — First-Order Passive** **Circuit Topology:** Input $V_{in}$ applied across the series combination of **R** (resistor) and **C** (capacitor). Output $V_{out}$ is taken **across the capacitor**. **Physical "Why" Behind the Behavior:** 1. **At very low frequencies (DC, $f \to 0$):** The capacitor's reactance $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ becomes very large (approaches an **open circuit**). Almost the entire input voltage drops across the capacitor → $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$. Signal **passes**. 2. **At very high frequencies ($f \to \infty$):** The capacitor's reactance $X_C$ becomes very small (approaches a **short circuit**). Almost the entire input voltage drops across the resistor → $V_{out} \approx 0$. Signal is **attenuated**. 3. **At the cutoff frequency ($f = f_c$):** The reactance $X_C = R$. The output magnitude equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of the input, and the phase lag is **45°**. #### **B. RC High Pass Filter (HPF) — First-Order Passive** **Circuit Topology:** Input $V_{in}$ applied across the series combination of **C** (capacitor) and **R** (resistor). Output $V_{out}$ is taken **across the resistor**. **Physical "Why" Behind the Behavior:** 1. **At very low frequencies (DC, $f \to 0$):** The capacitor's reactance $X_C$ becomes very large (acts as **open circuit**). No current flows → no voltage drop across R → $V_{out} \approx 0$. Signal is **blocked**. 2. **At very high frequencies ($f \to \infty$):** The capacitor's reactance $X_C$ becomes very small (acts as **short circuit**). The input is applied directly across the resistor → $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$. Signal **passes**. 3. **At the cutoff frequency ($f = f_c$):** The reactance $X_C = R$. The output magnitude equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of the input, and the phase lead is **45°**. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Parameter | RC Low Pass Filter (LPF) | RC High Pass Filter (HPF) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Transfer Function** $H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | | 2 | **Magnitude Response** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | | 3 | **Phase Response** $\angle H(j\omega)$ | $\phi(\omega) = -\arctan(\omega RC)$ | $\phi(\omega) = 90^\circ - \arctan(\omega RC)$ | | 4 | **Cutoff Frequency** $f_c$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | | 5 | **Angular Cutoff** $\omega_c$ | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | | 6 | **Time Constant** $\tau$ | $\tau = RC$ (seconds) | $\tau = RC$ (seconds) | | 7 | **Gain in dB at $f_c$** | $-3 \text{ dB}$ | $-3 \text{ dB}$ | | 8 | **Passband** ($f \ll f_c$) | $0 < f < f_c$ | $f_c < f < \infty$ | | 9 | **Stopband** ($f \gg f_c$) | $f > f_c$ | $0 < f < f_c$ | | 10 | **Roll-off Rate** | $-20 \text{ dB/decade}$ | $+20 \text{ dB/decade}$ | | 11 | **Output is across** | Capacitor (C) | Resistor (R) | | 12 | **DC Behavior** | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (passes DC) | $V_{out} = 0$ (blocks DC) | --- ### 2.3 Bode Plot — Quick Reference **Gain in Decibels (dB):** $$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \vert H(j\omega) \vert \right)$$ - **LPF:** $A_{dB} \approx 0$ dB for $f \ll f_c$; $A_{dB} \approx -3$ dB at $f = f_c$; $A_{dB}$ decreases at **−20 dB/decade** for $f \gg f_c$. - **HPF:** $A_{dB}$ increases at **+20 dB/decade** for $f \ll f_c$; $A_{dB} \approx -3$ dB at $f = f_c$; $A_{dB} \approx 0$ dB for $f \gg f_c$. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Filter Type Used | |---|---| | Audio subwoofer crossover (block highs) | LPF | | Anti-aliasing in ADC inputs (before sampling) | LPF | | AC coupling between amplifier stages (block DC offset) | HPF | | Hum removal (60 Hz/50 Hz power line noise rejection) | Notch (related to LPF) | | RF receiver preselector (block low-frequency interference) | HPF | | Smoothing rectified DC in power supplies | LPF (smoothing capacitor) | | Audio tweeter crossover (block lows) | HPF | | DC blocking capacitor at input of an amplifier | HPF | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of RC Low Pass Filter **Starting Circuit:** Series combination of R and C, with $V_{in}$ applied across the series pair and $V_{out}$ measured across C. **Step 1 — Write the impedances in the frequency domain (phasor/s-domain):** $$Z_R = R, \quad Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule (VDR):** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the impedances:** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega RC + 1}$$ **Step 5 — Hence the transfer function:** $$H_{LPF}(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 6 — Magnitude squared:** $$\vert H_{LPF} \vert^2 = \frac{1}{(1)^2 + (\omega RC)^2} = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$$ **Step 7 — Take the square root to obtain magnitude:** $$\vert H_{LPF}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ **Step 8 — Phase angle:** $$\phi_{LPF}(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega RC}{1}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$ **Step 9 — Cutoff frequency derivation:** The cutoff frequency is defined as the frequency at which the magnitude drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its passband (maximum) value. Passband magnitude is 1 (at $f = 0$): $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2 \implies (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1 \implies \omega_c RC = 1$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{and} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Transfer Function of RC High Pass Filter **Starting Circuit:** Series combination of C and R, with $V_{in}$ applied across the series pair and $V_{out}$ measured across R. **Step 1 — Apply VDR with $V_{out}$ across the resistor:** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_R}{Z_R + Z_C} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 3 — Transfer function:** $$H_{HPF}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 4 — Magnitude:** $$\vert H_{HPF}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ **Step 5 — Phase angle:** $$\phi_{HPF}(\omega) = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$ **Step 6 — Cutoff frequency derivation:** At $f = f_c$, $\vert H_{HPF} \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$\frac{\omega_c RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ Squaring: $$\frac{(\omega_c RC)^2}{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$2(\omega_c RC)^2 = 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 \implies (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1$$ $$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{and} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The cutoff frequency formula is **identical** for both LPF and HPF — only the output node differs. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Style) **Given:** $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ (typical lab values) **Step A — Compute the cutoff frequency:** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} = \frac{10000}{6.2832} \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step B — Tabulate expected gain and phase for LPF at multiple frequencies:** | $f$ (Hz) | $\omega RC$ | $\vert H_{LPF} \vert$ | Gain (dB) | Phase $\phi$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 100 | 0.0628 | 0.998 | −0.034 dB | −3.6° | | 500 | 0.3142 | 0.954 | −0.41 dB | −17.4° | | 1000 | 0.6283 | 0.847 | −1.44 dB | −32.1° | | 1591.55 ($f_c$) | 1.0000 | **0.7071** | **−3.00 dB** | **−45.0°** | | 3000 | 1.885 | 0.469 | −6.58 dB | −62.0° | | 10000 | 6.283 | 0.157 | −16.06 dB | −81.0° | | 50000 | 31.42 | 0.0318 | −29.95 dB | −88.2° | **Step C — Verification of the −3 dB point:** At $f = f_c = 1591.55$ Hz, $\vert H \vert = 0.7071$, so $$A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(0.7071) = 20 \times (-0.1505) = -3.01 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step D — Verification of the roll-off rate:** From $f_c$ to $10 f_c$ (one decade up): $$\Delta A_{dB} = -16.06 - (-3.00) = -13.06 \text{ dB/decade (theoretical: } -20 \text{ dB/decade)}$$ (The deviation from −20 dB/decade near $f_c$ is expected; the asymptotic slope is approached only well above $f_c$.) --- ### 3.4 Phase Calculation Sample (LPF at $f = 2 f_c$) $$\omega RC = 2 \implies \phi = -\tan^{-1}(2) = -63.43^\circ$$ This means the **output lags the input by 63.43°** at twice the cutoff frequency. The output voltage reaches its peak **after** the input has already started falling. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Circuit Schematic — RC Low Pass Filter (Mermaid Block Diagram) > [!NOTE] > Mermaid is used here to render a **functional block-level schematic** that clearly maps the signal flow, component interactions, and output node. This avoids the limitation of drawing literal resistor/capacitor symbols. ```mermaid graph LR nodeVin["Vin: AC Signal Source
Function Generator"] nodeR["R: 1 kOhm Resistor
Series Element"] nodeC["C: 0.1 uF Capacitor
Shunt to Ground"] nodeGND["GND: Ground Reference
0 Volt Node"] nodeVout["Vout: Output Terminal
Across Capacitor"] nodeCRO["CRO or DSO
Measure Vout"] nodeVin -- "Series connection" --> nodeR nodeR -- "Node X" --> nodeVout nodeVout -- "Across Capacitor" --> nodeC nodeC -- "Connected to" --> nodeGND nodeVout -- "Probed Channel 2" --> nodeCRO style nodeVin fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000 style nodeR fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000000 style nodeC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#0000CD,color:#000000 style nodeGND fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 style nodeVout fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000000 style nodeCRO fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000000 ``` **Reading the schematic:** - The **input node** is on the left (function generator). - **R** sits in series between the input and the output node. - **C** is connected from the output node to **ground**. - The **output** is measured across the capacitor. - For a **standard low-pass behavior**, as $f$ rises, $X_C$ falls, and the capacitor shunts more of the signal to ground → $V_{out}$ drops. --- ### 4.2 Circuit Schematic — RC High Pass Filter ```mermaid graph LR nodeVin2["Vin: AC Signal Source
Function Generator"] nodeC2["C: 0.1 uF Capacitor
Series Element"] nodeR2["R: 1 kOhm Resistor
Shunt to Ground"] nodeGND2["GND: Ground Reference
0 Volt Node"] nodeVout2["Vout: Output Terminal
Across Resistor"] nodeCRO2["CRO or DSO
Measure Vout"] nodeVin2 -- "Series connection" --> nodeC2 nodeC2 -- "Node Y" --> nodeVout2 nodeVout2 -- "Across Resistor" --> nodeR2 nodeR2 -- "Connected to" --> nodeGND2 nodeVout2 -- "Probed Channel 2" --> nodeCRO2 style nodeVin2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000 style nodeC2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#0000CD,color:#000000 style nodeR2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000000 style nodeGND2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 style nodeVout2 fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000000 style nodeCRO2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000000 ``` **Reading the schematic:** - The **capacitor C** is now in series with the input signal. - The **resistor R** is the shunt element (output is taken across it). - At DC and low frequencies, C blocks current → $V_{out} \approx 0$. - At high frequencies, C acts as a wire → $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$. --- ### 4.3 Frequency Response — Bode Plot Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph LPF_RESPONSE["RC LOW PASS FILTER RESPONSE"] axisA["Frequency Axis f (log scale, Hz)"] fcLPF["fc = 1591 Hz
The -3 dB Corner"] passLPF["Passband Region
f less than fc
Gain approx 0 dB"] stopLPF["Stopband Region
f greater than fc
Slope = -20 dB/decade"] axisA --> passLPF axisA --> fcLPF axisA --> stopLPF end subgraph HPF_RESPONSE["RC HIGH PASS FILTER RESPONSE"] axisB["Frequency Axis f (log scale, Hz)"] fcHPF["fc = 1591 Hz
The -3 dB Corner"] stopHPF["Stopband Region
f less than fc
Slope = +20 dB/decade"] passHPF["Passband Region
f greater than fc
Gain approx 0 dB"] axisB --> stopHPF axisB --> fcHPF axisB --> passHPF end style LPF_RESPONSE fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#B8860B style HPF_RESPONSE fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4 style fcLPF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00 style fcHPF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00 ``` --- ### 4.4 Experimental Test Setup Flow (Lab Procedure Mapping) ```mermaid graph TB step1["STEP 1: Assemble RC circuit on breadboard
R = 1 kOhm, C = 0.1 uF"] step2["STEP 2: Connect function generator to Vin
Set Vin amplitude to 5 V peak-to-peak"] step3["STEP 3: Connect CRO Channel 1 to Vin
CRO Channel 2 to Vout"] step4["STEP 4: Vary input frequency from 100 Hz to 100 kHz
Record Vout amplitude and phase shift"] step5["STEP 5: Tabulate f, Vout, Gain in dB, Phase in degrees"] step6["STEP 6: Calculate theoretical fc using fc = 1 over 2 pi RC"] step7["STEP 7: Plot Gain vs log f
Verify -3 dB at fc and -20 dB/dec slope"] step8["STEP 8: Repeat for HPF configuration
Swap component positions"] step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 style step1 fill:#98FB98 style step2 fill:#98FB98 style step3 fill:#98FB98 style step4 fill:#FFD700 style step5 fill:#FFD700 style step6 fill:#FFA07A style step7 fill:#FFA07A style step8 fill:#87CEFA ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define an RC Low Pass Filter. State the expression for its cutoff frequency.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **RC Low Pass Filter (RC-LPF)** is a first-order passive filter consisting of a resistor R and a capacitor C, where the output is taken across the capacitor. It allows signals with frequencies **below the cutoff frequency** to pass with minimal attenuation and attenuates signals with frequencies **above the cutoff frequency**. **Cutoff frequency expression:** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ The cutoff frequency is defined as the frequency at which the output power drops to **50%** of the maximum, or equivalently, the output voltage magnitude drops to **$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$** (≈ 0.707) of the input — corresponding to a gain of **−3 dB**. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for −3 dB explanation)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Distinguish between the output node and the phase response of RC-LPF and RC-HPF.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | RC-LPF | RC-HPF | |---|---|---| | Output taken across | **Capacitor (C)** | **Resistor (R)** | | Phase at $f_c$ | **−45° (output lags input)** | **+45° (output leads input)** | | DC behavior | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (passes DC) | $V_{out} = 0$ (blocks DC) | | Phase equation | $\phi = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | $\phi = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | **(3 marks: 1 mark each for output node, phase at $f_c$, and DC behavior — tabular comparison)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the **transfer function** and **cutoff frequency** of an RC Low Pass Filter. Plot its frequency response (Bode magnitude plot). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Circuit Description (1 mark):** A series combination of R and C with input $V_{in}$ applied across the pair and output $V_{out}$ taken across the capacitor C. **Step 2 — Voltage Divider Application (2 marks):** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$ **Step 3 — Simplification (2 marks):** $$H_{LPF}(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ $$\vert H_{LPF}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ **Step 4 — Cutoff Frequency Derivation (1 mark):** At $f = f_c$, $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \implies \omega_c RC = 1$$ $$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ **Step 5 — Frequency Response Plot (1 mark):** - 0 dB gain (flat) for $f \ll f_c$ - −3 dB at $f = f_c$ - Slope of **−20 dB/decade** for $f \gg f_c$ --- **Q. (b)** For an RC-LPF with $R = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 10 \text{ nF}$, calculate the **cutoff frequency** and the **output voltage** when an input of $5 \text{ V}_{pp}$ at $f = 2 f_c$ is applied. Also find the **phase shift**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Cutoff Frequency (2 marks):** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4700 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{2.953 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3386.3 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Input Frequency (1 mark):** $$f = 2 f_c = 2 \times 3386.3 = 6772.6 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 3 — Magnitude at $2 f_c$ (2 marks):** At $f = 2f_c$, $\omega RC = 2$, so: $$\vert H_{LPF} \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{1}{2.2361} \approx 0.4472$$ **Step 4 — Output Voltage (1 mark):** $$V_{out,pp} = \vert H \vert \times V_{in,pp} = 0.4472 \times 5 = 2.236 \text{ V}_{pp}$$ **Step 5 — Phase Shift (1 mark):** $$\phi = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC) = -\tan^{-1}(2) = -63.43^\circ$$ **Final Answer:** $f_c \approx 3.39 \text{ kHz}$, $V_{out} \approx 2.236 \text{ V}_{pp}$, Phase = **−63.43°** (output lags input). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the **transfer function** and **cutoff frequency** of an RC High Pass Filter. Sketch its **Bode magnitude and phase plot**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Circuit Description (1 mark):** Series combination of C and R; input $V_{in}$ across the series pair; output $V_{out}$ across the resistor R. **Step 2 — Voltage Divider Rule (2 marks):** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_R}{Z_R + Z_C} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$ **Step 3 — Simplification (2 marks):** $$H_{HPF}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ $$\vert H_{HPF}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ **Step 4 — Cutoff Frequency Derivation (1 mark):** Setting $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ at $f = f_c$: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ **Step 5 — Bode Plot Sketch (1 mark):** - **Magnitude:** Slope of **+20 dB/decade** for $f \ll f_c$, flattens at 0 dB for $f \gg f_c$, equals −3 dB at $f_c$. - **Phase:** Starts near +90° at low $f$, equals +45° at $f_c$, approaches 0° at high $f$. --- **Q. (b)** Design an RC High Pass Filter with a cutoff frequency of **$2 \text{ kHz}$** using a capacitor of value **$0.05 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$**. Then compute the **gain in dB** and **phase** at an input frequency of **$500 \text{ Hz}$**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate Required R (2 marks):** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \implies R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.55 \text{ }\Omega \approx 1.59 \text{ k}\Omega$$ (Standard closest E12 value: **1.5 kΩ** or **1.6 kΩ**) **Step 2 — Compute $\omega RC$ at $f = 500$ Hz (1 mark):** $$\omega RC = \frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{500}{2000} = 0.25$$ (Alternatively: $\omega RC = 2\pi \times 500 \times 1591.55 \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6} = 0.25$) **Step 3 — Magnitude Calculation (1 mark):** $$\vert H_{HPF} \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{1 + 0.0625}} = \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{1.0625}}$$ $$\vert H_{HPF} \vert = \frac{0.25}{1.0308} = 0.2425$$ **Step 4 — Gain in dB (1 mark):** $$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.2425) = 20 \times (-0.6154) \approx -12.31 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5 — Phase Calculation (2 marks):** $$\phi = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC) = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.25)$$ $$\tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04^\circ$$ $$\phi = 90^\circ - 14.04^\circ = 75.96^\circ \approx +76^\circ$$ **Final Answer:** $R \approx 1.59 \text{ k}\Omega$, Gain $\approx -12.31$ dB, Phase $\approx +76°$ (output leads input). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks — KTU Board Examiner Insights:** 1. **Confusing the Output Node (Loss: 1–2 marks):** Students frequently **swap the output** — they take output across the resistor in an LPF or across the capacitor in an HPF. The rule is simple: **LPF = output across C; HPF = output across R**. Remember: the **capacitor's impedance decreases with frequency**, so it **shunts high frequencies to ground** (LPF behavior) and **blocks low frequencies** (HPF behavior). 2. **Incorrect Phase Sign Convention (Loss: 1 mark):** In the LPF, the output **lags** the input (negative phase). In the HPF, the output **leads** the input (positive phase). Examiners specifically check for the **sign** and the **±45° at $f_c$** value. 3. **Forgetting the −3 dB Definition (Loss: 1 mark):** The cutoff frequency is **NOT** defined as "where output equals input." It is **explicitly** defined as the frequency at which the **output voltage magnitude drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (≈ 0.707) of the passband**, equivalent to a gain of **−3 dB**, equivalent to **half-power** point. 4. **Missing the 20 dB/decade Roll-off (Loss: 1–2 marks):** A first-order RC filter rolls off at **exactly −20 dB/decade** (or +20 dB/decade for HPF) beyond the cutoff. Students often write "steep slope" without specifying this number — examiners expect the **exact value**. 5. **Dimensional Errors in $f_c$ (Loss: 1 mark):** When substituting R and C values, students forget to convert kΩ → Ω and µF → F. Always work in **base SI units** (ohms and farads). 6. **No Phase Plot in Bode Sketch (Loss: 1 mark):** When asked for a "Bode plot," examiners expect **both magnitude and phase curves**. A magnitude-only plot is incomplete. 7. **Lab Report Errors:** In the lab record, students often write $f_c$ values in kHz without units, or mix up input and output amplitudes. Always tabulate with **units** and **label both Vin and Vout** clearly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Must-Know Points for KTU Exam:** - ✅ **LPF:** Series R, Shunt C, **Output across C**. Passes lows, blocks highs. - ✅ **HPF:** Series C, Shunt R, **Output across R**. Passes highs, blocks lows. - ✅ **Cutoff frequency (both filters):** $\boxed{f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}}$ - ✅ **−3 dB at $f_c$** = $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ magnitude = **half-power point**. - ✅ **LPF phase at $f_c$:** **−45°** (output lags). HPF phase at $f_c$: **+45°** (output leads). - ✅ **Roll-off slope:** First-order filter = **±20 dB/decade** beyond $f_c$. - ✅ **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ determines how fast the capacitor charges/discharges. - ✅ **DC behavior:** LPF passes DC ($V_{out} = V_{in}$); HPF blocks DC ($V_{out} = 0$). - ✅ **Capacitive reactance:** $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ — decreases with frequency. - ✅ **Gain in dB formula:** $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$. - ✅ **Lab verification tip:** Set $V_{in}$ to a fixed amplitude (e.g., 5 Vpp) and sweep $f$. The frequency at which $V_{out} = 0.707 \times V_{in}$ is the **experimental $f_c$** — verify against theoretical $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. - ✅ **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$; $1 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-6} \text{ F}$; $1 \text{ nF} = 10^{-9} \text{ F}$. - ✅ **KTU exam format:** Part A = 3 marks (definition/distinction); Part B = 14 marks (derivation + numerical). - ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"C-Output = Low-Pass"** — the capacitor's slow charging rejects fast changes → LPF behavior. **"R-Output = High-Pass"** — the resistor sees current only when the capacitor allows it (high frequencies) → HPF behavior.

topics.title: Integrator and Differentiator Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Integrator and Differentiator Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Integrator and Differentiator Circuits?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCICL308 - Electronic Devices and Circuits Lab**, Module Experiment 2 deals with the design, hardware realization, and waveform analysis of two fundamental **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** based signal processing circuits:

- **Op-Amp Integrator:** A circuit whose output voltage is the **time integral** of the input voltage, scaled by a gain factor.
- **Op-Amp Differentiator:** A circuit whose output voltage is the **time derivative** of the input voltage, scaled by a gain factor.

These two circuits are mathematically **duals** of each other — swapping the positions of the resistor and capacitor in the feedback network converts one circuit into the other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment focuses on the practical verification of the **ideal mathematical operations** (integration and differentiation) using the **IC 741 Op-Amp**, and the observation of the circuit's response to standard periodic waveforms such as **sine, square, and triangular waves**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car and watching your **speedometer**:

- The **speedometer** shows your *instantaneous rate of change of position* — it **differentiates** your position signal with respect to time.
- The **odometer** in your car shows the *total distance traveled* — it **integrates** your speed signal over time.

In electronics, we perform these same operations on **voltage signals**:

- A **Differentiator** answers: *"How fast is the signal changing RIGHT NOW?"*
- An **Integrator** answers: *"How much has the signal accumulated OVER TIME?"*

For example, if you feed a **square wave** into a differentiator, it produces sharp **spikes** at every transition (because the rate of change is sudden). Conversely, a square wave fed into an integrator produces a **triangular wave** (because the integral of a constant is a ramp).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering:** Integrators are the heart of every analog computer, every analog-to-digital converter's charge-balancing stage, every PID controller's I-term, and audio filter design. Differentiators are used in edge-detection circuits, FM demodulators, and high-frequency noise-shaping networks.

## 1.3 Core Building Block: The Op-Amp in Negative Feedback

Both circuits rely on the **ideal op-amp assumptions** that govern their behavior:

- **Infinite open-loop gain** ($A_{OL} \to \infty$)
- **Infinite input impedance** ($Z_{in} \to \infty$) → **Zero input current** at the inverting ($V^-$) and non-inverting ($V^+$) terminals.
- **Zero output impedance**
- **Virtual short:** $V^+ = V^-$ (because of infinite gain with negative feedback)
- **Virtual ground:** When the non-inverting terminal is grounded, the inverting terminal is also held at **0 V** without being physically connected to ground.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Examiners frequently test whether students can correctly identify the **virtual ground concept** as the key reason current can flow through the capacitor in an integrator (since no current can flow *into* the op-amp's input terminals).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Op-Amp Integrator — Circuit Topology

The ideal op-amp integrator consists of:
- An **input resistor** $R$ connected between the input signal $V_{in}$ and the **inverting terminal** ($V^-$).
- A **feedback capacitor** $C$ connected between the **output** $V_{out}$ and the inverting terminal.
- The **non-inverting terminal** ($V^+$) is grounded.

The output node is taken from the op-amp output, while the inverting input is the "summing junction" where input and feedback currents meet.

## 2.2 Op-Amp Integrator — Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Apply KCL at the inverting node (Virtual Ground):** Since $V^- = 0$ V, the current through the input resistor is:
$$I_R = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

2. **Kirchhoff's Current Law at the Inverting Node:** All current flowing through $R$ must flow through $C$ because no current enters the op-amp:
$$I_C = I_R = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

3. **Capacitor i-v Relationship:** The current through the feedback capacitor is proportional to the rate of change of voltage across it:
$$I_C = C \frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt} = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

4. **Equate and Integrate:** Equating the two expressions for $I_C$ and integrating both sides with respect to time:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

Integrating with respect to $t$ from $0$ to $t$ (assuming zero initial charge on $C$):
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** The output is the **negative integral** of the input, scaled by the factor $\dfrac{1}{RC}$.

## 2.3 Op-Amp Differentiator — Circuit Topology

The differentiator is obtained by **swapping** the positions of $R$ and $C$:
- An **input capacitor** $C$ is connected between $V_{in}$ and the inverting terminal.
- A **feedback resistor** $R$ is connected between $V_{out}$ and the inverting terminal.

## 2.4 Op-Amp Differentiator — Working Principle

1. **Current Through Input Capacitor:**
$$I_C = C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

2. **KCL at Virtual Ground:** All of this current flows through the feedback resistor:
$$I_R = -\frac{V_{out}}{R}$$

3. **Equate and Solve:**
$$C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R}$$

$$V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The output is the **negative derivative** of the input, scaled by the factor $RC$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Integrator Formula** | **Differentiator Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt$ | $V_{out}(t) = -RC\,\dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | Volts (V) |
| Scale Factor (Time Constant) | $T = RC$ | $\tau_d = RC$ | Seconds (s) |
| Cut-off Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Frequency Response Type | **Low-Pass Filter** (-20 dB/decade roll-off above $f_c$) | **High-Pass Filter** (+20 dB/decade rise below $f_c$) | — |
| Practical Component Ratio | $R_1 C \gg R C$ (to limit DC gain) | $R_2 C_2 \ll R_1 C_1$ (to limit high-freq gain) | — |
| Phase Shift at Midband | $-90^\circ$ (output lags input) | $+90^\circ$ (output leads input) | Degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Integrators form the **active filter** core in biomedical instrumentation (e.g., EEG/ECG baseline drift removal), audio crossovers, and 555-timer waveform generation. Differentiators are used in **analog PID controllers** (the D-term), radar pulse-shaping, and **FM demodulation**.

## 2.6 Practical Limitations of the Ideal Circuits

The **ideal** integrator and differentiator have problems when implemented with real components:

- **Pure Integrator Drawback:** The DC gain is theoretically infinite, meaning any tiny DC offset at the input causes the output to **saturate** to the supply rails ($\pm V_{sat}$). This is the "**integrator drift**" or "**integrator wind-up**" problem.
- **Pure Differentiator Drawback:** The high-frequency gain increases without bound, amplifying **high-frequency noise** and causing instability (oscillation).

To overcome these, the **practical circuits** use additional components:
- **Practical Integrator:** Adds a **feedback resistor $R_f$** in parallel with $C$ to limit DC gain to $-R_f/R_1$ and define a low-frequency pole.
- **Practical Differentiator:** Adds an **input resistor $R_1$** in series with $C$ and a **small feedback capacitor $C_f$** in parallel with $R$ to limit high-frequency gain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Op-Amp Integrator Output Equation

We start with the basic op-amp integrator topology and rigorously derive the output voltage expression.

**Given:**
- Input signal: $V_{in}(t)$ connected through resistor $R$ to the inverting terminal.
- Feedback element: Capacitor $C$ from output to inverting terminal.
- Non-inverting terminal: Grounded ($V^+ = 0$).
- Initial condition: $V_{out}(0) = 0$ (uncharged capacitor at $t = 0$).

**Step 1 — Apply the Virtual Short Concept:**

Since the op-amp is in negative feedback with infinite open-loop gain, the differential input voltage is zero:

$$V^+ - V^- = 0$$

With $V^+ = 0$ (non-inverting terminal is grounded):

$$V^- = 0 \text{ V (Virtual Ground)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Current Through the Input Resistor:**

Using Ohm's law across resistor $R$, with one end at $V_{in}$ and the other at the virtual ground ($0$ V):

$$I_R = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node:**

The sum of currents entering the inverting node must equal zero. Since no current flows *into* the op-amp's ideal input ($I_{op-amp} = 0$), all current flowing in through $R$ must exit through $C$:

$$I_R + I_C = 0$$

$$I_C = -I_R = -\frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** The current $I_C$ is defined as flowing from the inverting node toward the output node. The negative sign indicates that when $V_{in}$ is positive, current actually flows in the *opposite* direction (from output through $C$ to the inverting node).

**Step 4 — Express $I_C$ Using the Capacitor Voltage-Current Relationship:**

The voltage across the capacitor is $V_C = V^- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$.

Using the fundamental capacitor equation:

$$I_C = C \frac{dV_C}{dt} = C \frac{d(-V_{out})}{dt} = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the Two Expressions for $I_C$:**

$$-C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}(t)}{RC}$$

Integrating both sides with respect to $t$ from $0$ to $t$:

$$V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = \frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Initial Condition and Finalize:**

Assuming the capacitor is uncharged at $t = 0$, so $V_{out}(0) = 0$:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Integrator Equation:**
> $$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}(t) \, dt$$
> The negative sign confirms that the **inverting integrator** produces an output that is **$180^\circ$ out of phase** with the integral of the input.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Op-Amp Differentiator Output Equation

**Given:**
- Input signal: $V_{in}(t)$ connected through capacitor $C$ to the inverting terminal.
- Feedback element: Resistor $R$ from output to inverting terminal.
- Non-inverting terminal: Grounded ($V^+ = 0$).

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**

$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Current Through the Input Capacitor:**

The voltage across the capacitor is $V_C = V_{in} - V^- = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$.

Using the capacitor i-v relationship:

$$I_C = C \frac{dV_C}{dt} = C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node:**

The current entering through $C$ must exit through the feedback resistor $R$ (since none enters the op-amp):

$$I_C + I_R = 0$$

$$I_R = -I_C = -C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Express $I_R$ Using Ohm's Law Across $R$:**

The current through the feedback resistor, with one end at $V^- = 0$ and the other at $V_{out}$, is:

$$I_R = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the Two Expressions for $I_R$:**

$$-\frac{V_{out}}{R} = -C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R} = C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Differentiator Equation:**
> $$V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$
> Again, the negative sign indicates the **$180^\circ$ phase inversion** characteristic of inverting op-amp configurations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Response to Standard Input Waveforms

This is the **highest-yield KTU table** — it is almost always asked in viva and frequently appears in the ESE paper.

| **Input Waveform** | **Integrator Output $V_{out}$** | **Differentiator Output $V_{out}$** | **Visual Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Square Wave** | **Triangular Wave** (linear ramps up and down) | **Spike Train** (sharp positive & negative spikes at edges) | Integrator smooths; Differentiator highlights edges |
| **Triangular Wave** | **Parabolic Wave** (piecewise quadratic segments) | **Square Wave** (alternating + and - levels) | Integrator curves; Differentiator extracts slope |
| **Sine Wave** $V_p \sin(\omega t)$ | $-\dfrac{V_p}{\omega RC}\cos(\omega t)$ (Cosine, $-90^\circ$ shifted, scaled) | $-RC \cdot \omega V_p \cos(\omega t)$ (Cosine, $-90^\circ$ shifted, scaled) | Both shift by 90° but with opposite gain dependence |
| **DC Voltage** | **Ramp (linearly increasing/decreasing)** until saturation | **Zero (no change → no derivative)** | Differentiator rejects DC; Integrator drifts |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A practical op-amp integrator is built with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F}$. A square wave of amplitude $\pm 5$ V at $1$ kHz is applied. Find the peak-to-peak output voltage.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the time constant:

$$RC = (10 \times 10^3) \times (0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2:** The half-period of a 1 kHz square wave is:

$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{1}{2f} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1000} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3:** During the positive half-cycle ($V_{in} = +5$ V for $0.5$ ms), the output ramps negatively:

$$\Delta V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{T/2} V_{in} \, dt = -\frac{1}{10^{-3}} \times (5) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** By symmetry, the negative half-cycle gives $+2.5$ V swing.

**Step 5:** Total peak-to-peak output:

$$V_{out,pp} = 2 \times 2.5 = 5 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Since the time constant $RC = 1$ ms equals the full period of 1 ms, the integrator operates in a regime where the output is a clean, undistorted triangular wave — exactly what we expect from an ideal integrator.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Op-Amp Integrator — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin Signal Source] -->|through Resistor R| B[Inverting Node Virtual Ground]
    C[Non Inverting Terminal] -->|Connected to Ground| D[Ground Reference 0V]
    B -->|Input Current IR = Vin divide by R| E[Op Amp IC 741]
    F[Feedback Capacitor C] -->|Integrates Current into Voltage| B
    E -->|Scaled and Inverted Output| G[Vout Negative Integral of Vin]
    D --> E
    F -.->|Feedback Path| E
```

## 4.2 Op-Amp Differentiator — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin Signal Source] -->|through Capacitor C| B[Inverting Node Virtual Ground]
    C[Non Inverting Terminal] -->|Connected to Ground| D[Ground Reference 0V]
    B -->|Derivative of Vin as Current IC| E[Op Amp IC 741]
    F[Feedback Resistor R] -->|Converts Current to Voltage| B
    E -->|Scaled and Inverted Derivative| G[Vout Negative RC dVindt]
    D --> E
    F -.->|Feedback Path| E
```

## 4.3 Waveform Transformation Flow — Integrator Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal Classification] --> B{Is Input Constant DC}
    A --> C{Is Input Square Wave}
    A --> D{Is Input Triangular Wave}
    A --> E{Is Input Sinusoidal}
    B -->|Yes| F[Output Linear Ramp toward Saturation]
    C -->|Yes| G[Output Triangular Wave with 180 degree Phase Inversion]
    D -->|Yes| H[Output Parabolic Curves Piecewise Quadratic]
    E -->|Yes| I[Output Cosine Wave Scaled by 1 over omega RC]
```

## 4.4 Waveform Transformation Flow — Differentiator Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal Classification] --> B{Is Input Constant DC}
    A --> C{Is Input Square Wave}
    A --> D{Is Input Triangular Wave}
    A --> E{Is Input Sinusoidal}
    B -->|Yes| F[Output Zero No Variation No Derivative]
    C -->|Yes| G[Output Sharp Spikes Positive on Rising Edge Negative on Falling Edge]
    D -->|Yes| H[Output Square Wave Alternating Levels Proportional to Slope]
    E -->|Yes> I[Output Cosine Wave Scaled by omega RC High Frequency Amplified]
```

## 4.5 Practical Integrator — Modified Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin Source] -->|R1 Input Resistor| B[Summing Junction]
    B -->|to Inverting Input| C[Op Amp 741]
    D[Ground] -->|to Non Inverting| C
    C -->|Output Node| E[Vout]
    F[Feedback Capacitor Cf in Parallel with Rf] -->|Stability Network| B
    F -.->|Feedback Path| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The parallel combination of $C_f$ and $R_f$ in the feedback path defines a **low-frequency pole** at $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ and limits the DC gain to $-R_f/R_1$. This prevents the output from drifting to saturation due to input offset voltage.

## 4.6 Practical Differentiator — Modified Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin Source] -->|Series R1 Limits HF Gain| B[Input Capacitor C1]
    B -->|to Inverting Node| C[Op Amp 741]
    D[Ground] -->|to Non Inverting| C
    C -->|Output Node| E[Vout]
    F[Feedback R in Parallel with Small Cf] -->|Roll Off HF Noise| B
    F -.->|Feedback Path| E
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define an ideal op-amp integrator and write its output voltage equation. Mention the role of the time constant $RC$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An ideal op-amp integrator is an active circuit using an op-amp with a resistor $R$ at the input and a capacitor $C$ in the feedback path. It produces an output proportional to the time integral of the input voltage. **(1 mark)**
The output equation is $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt$. **(1 mark)**
The time constant $RC$ acts as the scaling factor — a larger $RC$ produces a smaller magnitude output, and it also defines the cutoff frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ of the integrator's low-pass behavior. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Why is a practical differentiator preferred over an ideal differentiator in real applications?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An ideal differentiator has a high-frequency gain that increases linearly with frequency (at +20 dB/decade), which causes it to **amplify high-frequency noise** and become **unstable** (prone to oscillation). **(2 marks)**
A practical differentiator adds a **series input resistor $R_1$** (to limit high-frequency gain) and a **small feedback capacitor $C_f$** (to roll off gain above the desired frequency band), ensuring stable operation and noise immunity. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**(a)** Derive the output voltage expression of an op-amp integrator using the virtual ground concept. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: Op-amp with input resistor $R$, feedback capacitor $C$, non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Step 1:** Apply virtual short: $V^- = V^+ = 0$ V. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Current through input resistor:
$$I_R = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** By KCL at inverting node (no current into op-amp):
$$I_C = I_R = \frac{V_{in}}{R} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Capacitor current equation:
$$I_C = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Equate and rearrange:
$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6:** Integrate both sides:
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

With $V_{out}(0) = 0$ (ideal initial condition):

$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt} \quad \textbf{Final Result}$$

---

**(b)** An op-amp integrator is designed with $R = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.05 \text{ \mu F}$. A square wave of amplitude $\pm 4$ V at $500$ Hz is applied. Calculate the peak-to-peak output voltage and sketch the input-output waveforms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute time constant:
$$RC = (20 \times 10^3)(0.05 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute half-period:
$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{1}{2f} = \frac{1}{2 \times 500} = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** Output ramp magnitude per half-cycle:
$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{V_{in} \times (T/2)}{RC} = \frac{4 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Total peak-to-peak output:
$$V_{out,pp} = 2 \times \Delta V_{out} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Sketch — Input is a $\pm 4$ V square wave at 500 Hz; Output is an **inverted triangular wave** swinging between $-4$ V and $+4$ V (peak-to-peak = 8 V), with 180° phase shift. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget the **negative sign** in the integrator equation, causing the phase of the output triangular wave to be drawn **inverted** relative to the input square wave. A correctly drawn waveform should show the triangular wave **falling** during the **positive** half of the square wave input (inverting configuration). Losing 1–2 marks here is extremely common.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an op-amp differentiator. Derive its output voltage equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Circuit description: A differentiator has the input signal connected through a **capacitor $C$** to the inverting terminal, with a **feedback resistor $R$** from output to inverting terminal. The non-inverting terminal is grounded. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply virtual short: $V^- = 0$ V. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Current through input capacitor:
$$I_C = C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4:** KCL at inverting node: $I_C = -I_R$ where $I_R = -V_{out}/R$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Equate:
$$C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $V_{out}$:
$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

**(b)** A sine wave of $V_{in}(t) = 2 \sin(2000\pi t)$ V is applied to a differentiator with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F}$. Determine the output waveform equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters:
- Amplitude $V_p = 2$ V
- Angular frequency $\omega = 2000\pi$ rad/s
- Time constant $\tau = RC = (10^4)(0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}$ s. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply the differentiator equation:
$$V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{d}{dt}\left[2\sin(2000\pi t)\right] \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the derivative:
$$\frac{d}{dt}[2\sin(2000\pi t)] = 2 \times 2000\pi \cos(2000\pi t) = 4000\pi \cos(2000\pi t) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $-RC$:
$$V_{out}(t) = -(10^{-3})(4000\pi)\cos(2000\pi t) = -4\pi \cos(2000\pi t) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5:** Final waveform:
$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -4\pi \cos(2000\pi t) \approx -12.57 \cos(2000\pi t) \text{ V}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When computing differentiator output for a sine input, students often forget that the **gain is proportional to frequency**. Hence, at high frequencies, the output amplitude can easily exceed the op-amp's saturation voltage ($\pm V_{sat}$), causing **clipping** of the output waveform — a fact that should be commented upon for full marks. Also, watch for the sign: derivative of sine is cosine, and the inverting amplifier introduces an additional $180^\circ$ shift.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equations to Memorize:**
  - Integrator: $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt$
  - Differentiator: $V_{out}(t) = -RC\,\dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$

- **Topology Rule:** The component connected to the **input** and the component in the **feedback path** are always **swapped** between integrator and differentiator.

- **Phase Relationship:** Both circuits produce an output that is **$180^\circ$ out of phase** (inverted) compared to the mathematically expected result, because of the **inverting configuration** of the op-amp.

- **Virtual Ground is Sacred:** Always invoke $V^- = 0$ V first — it is the **starting point** for every derivation.

- **Square Wave → Triangular Wave:** Integrator converts step changes to linear ramps. Use this for quick waveform identification in exams.

- **Triangular Wave → Square Wave:** Differentiator extracts the constant slopes of a triangular wave as flat levels.

- **Differentiator Rejects DC:** The derivative of a constant is zero, so DC inputs produce no output. This is a common viva question.

- **Integrator Drifts on DC:** Without a feedback resistor, the integrator's output will **saturate** for any DC input due to its infinite DC gain — the **practical integrator** with $R_f \parallel C$ solves this.

- **Cutoff Frequency:** Both circuits share the same formula $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, but the integrator is a **low-pass** filter and the differentiator is a **high-pass** filter.

- **Time Constant Selection Rule:** For an integrator to produce a clean triangular output from a square wave, the period $T$ of the input should be **greater than $2RC$**. If $T \ll 2RC$, the output will be a heavily attenuated, rounded waveform.

- **Component Choice for IC 741 Lab:** Standard KTU lab values are $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F}$ to $0.1 \text{ \mu F}$, giving $RC$ in the millisecond range suitable for audio-frequency waveform observations.

- **Power Supply:** Always power the IC 741 with **dual supplies** ($\pm 12$ V or $\pm 15$ V) for the integrator/differentiator experiments; single-supply operation requires biasing networks not covered in this experiment.

- **Viva Favorites:** "Why is the output of an integrator triangular when input is square?" / "What is the limitation of an ideal differentiator?" / "Why is the practical integrator modified with a feedback resistor?" — prepare crisp 2–3 sentence answers for these.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V-I Characteristics of BJT and MOSFET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** V-I Characteristics of BJT and MOSFET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — V-I Characteristics of a BJT

The **V-I (Voltage–Current) characteristics of a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** are a family of static graphical plots that describe the steady-state electrical response of the device under DC bias. They are plotted in two families:

1. **Input Characteristics** — A plot of the **Base Current $I_B$** versus the **Base–Emitter Voltage $V_{BE}$**, parameterized by a fixed **Collector–Emitter Voltage $V_{CE}$**.
2. **Output Characteristics** — A plot of the **Collector Current $I_C$** versus the **Collector–Emitter Voltage $V_{CE}$**, parameterized by fixed values of the **Base Current $I_B$**.

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Manual (PCICL308), the **Common-Emitter (CE) configuration** is the standard topology used in the laboratory to plot BJT V-I curves, because it simultaneously displays both current gain and voltage gain behavior.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definition — V-I Characteristics of a MOSFET

The **V-I characteristics of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** are graphical plots that describe the drain current response of the device to applied terminal voltages. For an **n-channel enhancement MOSFET**, the two principal plots are:

1. **Transfer (or Transconductance) Characteristics** — A plot of the **Drain Current $I_D$** versus the **Gate–Source Voltage $V_{GS}$**, with $V_{DS}$ held constant.
2. **Output Characteristics** — A plot of the **Drain Current $I_D$** versus the **Drain–Source Voltage $V_{DS}$**, with $V_{GS}$ held constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled** device — its conduction is regulated by an electric field induced by $V_{GS}$, and **the gate draws essentially zero DC current** (leakage on the order of pA to nA). This is the fundamental physical distinction from the BJT, which is a **current-controlled** device.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Picture

#### The BJT as a "Two-Handled Water Tap"

Imagine a tap where the **base terminal is a small auxiliary handle** that you must push to control the main flow. A tiny push on this small handle (a small $I_B$) releases a torrent of water (a large $I_C$) through the main pipe. The "V-I characteristic" is essentially a chart that tells you: *for every small push on the small handle, how much water comes out of the big pipe, and how does that flow change if downstream pressure (analogous to $V_{CE}$) varies?*

#### The MOSFET as a "Capacitive Pressure Plate"

Now imagine a different tap where the handle does **not move water mechanically** but instead changes the **electric field across a pressure-sensitive membrane** (the oxide–silicon interface). You apply a voltage $V_{GS}$, the membrane deflects, and a conducting channel forms under it — allowing drain current $I_D$ to flow. The harder you press (higher $V_{GS}$), the wider the channel opens, the more current flows. Crucially, **no water touches the handle** — hence the negligible gate current.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and standard values are universally used in the lab and must be memorized:
>
> - **Thermal Voltage** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85 \ mV}$ at $T = 300 \ K$ (room temperature)
> - **Boltzmann Constant** $k = \mathbf{1.380 \times 10^{-23} \ J/K}$
> - **Electronic Charge** $q = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ C}$
> - **Silicon Bandgap Energy** $E_g = \mathbf{1.12 \ eV}$
> - **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration of Si** $n_i = \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10} \ cm^{-3}}$ at $300 \ K$
> - **Typical NPN BJT** (e.g., BC547, 2N3904): $\beta_{DC} \approx 100 - 800$, $V_{BE(on)} \approx \mathbf{0.7 \ V}$
> - **Typical Enhancement NMOS** (e.g., IRF540, CD4007): $V_{th} \approx \mathbf{1 - 3 \ V}$

---

### 1.5 BJT vs. MOSFET — A Side-by-Side Snapshot

| Parameter | BJT (NPN, CE) | MOSFET (NMOS, Enhancement) |
|---|---|---|
| Control variable | Current $I_B$ | Voltage $V_{GS}$ |
| Controlling terminal impedance | Low ($\sim k\Omega$) | Very high ($\sim 10^{12} \ \Omega$) |
| Key DC equation | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ |
| Turn-on parameter | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ |
| Output current type | Exponential in $V_{BE}$ | Quadratic in $V_{GS}$ (saturation) |

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative table is a **favourite KTU viva question** — be ready to articulate at least three differences and three similarities between the BJT and MOSFET characteristics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BJT — Theoretical Foundation (Common-Emitter Configuration)

The BJT in CE mode is biased so that the **Base–Emitter junction is forward-biased** ($V_{BE} > 0$) and the **Base–Collector junction is reverse-biased** ($V_{BC} < 0$). This is the **active region**, the principal operating regime for amplification.

#### 2.1.1 Input Characteristics ($I_B$ vs. $V_{BE}$)

- For a fixed $V_{CE}$, as $V_{BE}$ increases, the BE junction's forward bias grows, exponentially increasing the base current.
- The shape is **diode-like** because the BE junction is essentially a forward-biased PN diode.
- The curve shifts **slightly to the right** as $V_{CE}$ increases (because of the **Early effect** — the effective base width shrinks, slightly reducing recombination, so for the same $V_{BE}$ a smaller $I_B$ flows).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **input characteristic is NOT a pure diode curve** — the shift due to $V_{CE}$ is precisely what gives the BJT its amplifying (transistor) action, distinguishing it from an ordinary diode.

#### 2.1.2 Output Characteristics ($I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$)

The output characteristic has **four distinct regions**:

1. **Cutoff Region** — Both junctions reverse-biased. $I_C \approx I_{CEO}$ (leakage), practically zero.
2. **Saturation Region** — Both junctions forward-biased. $V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V. $I_C$ rises steeply with $V_{CE}$.
3. **Active (Forward Active) Region** — BE forward, BC reverse. $I_C \approx \beta I_B$, **almost independent** of $V_{CE}$ (a small upward slope exists due to the Early effect).
4. **Breakdown Region** — $V_{CE} > BV_{CEO}$. The collector junction avalanches; $I_C$ rises catastrophically. **Avoid in lab!**

#### 2.1.3 The Early Effect

The **Early voltage $V_A$** quantifies the slight dependence of $I_C$ on $V_{CE}$ in the active region:

$$I_C = I_{CO} \left( 1 + \frac{V_{CE}}{V_A} \right)$$

where $I_{CO}$ is the collector current extrapolated to $V_{CE} = 0$, and the **output resistance** of the transistor is:

$$r_o = \frac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C} \approx \frac{V_A}{I_C}$$

---

### 2.2 MOSFET — Theoretical Foundation (NMOS, Enhancement Mode)

The n-channel enhancement MOSFET is the most commonly used device in modern CMOS VLSI. Its operation is governed by the **induced channel** that forms in the p-substrate when $V_{GS} > V_{th}$.

#### 2.2.1 Operating Regions

| Region | Condition | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff (Subthreshold)** | $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | No channel; $I_D = 0$ (ideally) |
| **Triode (Linear / Ohmic)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Channel exists, drain end still pinched-on |
| **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Channel pinched off at drain; $I_D$ nearly constant |
| **Breakdown** | $V_{DS} > BV_{DSS}$ | Avalanche at drain junction — destructive |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **triode region**, the MOSFET behaves as a **voltage-controlled resistor** $R_{DS(on)}$, whose value is:
> $$R_{DS(on)} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})}$$
> This is why MOSFETs are widely used as analog switches and digital pass transistors.

#### 2.2.2 Threshold Voltage Concept

The **threshold voltage $V_{th}$** is the minimum $V_{GS}$ required to invert the p-substrate surface into an n-type conducting channel. It is determined by the fabrication process through:

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

where $\gamma$ is the **body-effect coefficient** and $\phi_F$ is the **Fermi potential** of the substrate.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master formula cheat sheet** for Module Experiment 2. Every formula listed has been asked in past KTU University Examinations at least once.

| # | Device | Formula | Region / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BJT | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | All regions | A |
| 2 | BJT | $I_C = \beta_{DC} I_B$ | Forward Active | A |
| 3 | BJT | $I_C = I_S \exp\!\left( \dfrac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) \left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A} \right)$ | Forward Active (with Early effect) | A |
| 4 | BJT | $I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B$ | Forward Active | A |
| 5 | BJT | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$ | Forward Active | dimensionless |
| 6 | BJT | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Small-signal (active) | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | BJT | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Small-signal (active) | S (Siemens) |
| 8 | MOSFET | $I_D = 0$ | Cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$) | A |
| 9 | MOSFET | $I_D = K_n \left[ 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2 \right]$ | Triode ($V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$) | A |
| 10 | MOSFET | $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \left(1 + \lambda V_{DS}\right)$ | Saturation (with channel-length modulation) | A |
| 11 | MOSFET | $K_n = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L}$ | Process / geometry constant | A / V$^2$ |
| 12 | MOSFET | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | "Overdrive" or "effective" voltage | V |
| 13 | MOSFET | $g_m = \sqrt{2 K_n \dfrac{W}{L} I_D} = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | Small-signal (saturation) | S |
| 14 | MOSFET | $R_{DS(on)} = \dfrac{1}{K_n \dfrac{2}{V_{OV}} \cdot V_{DS}}$ simplified to $\dfrac{1}{2 K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})}$ | Triode, small $V_{DS}$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Do **NOT** confuse $K_n$ (the KTU / Sedra-Smith convention) with $k_n'$ (= $\mu_n C_{ox}$, the Neamen convention). They differ by a factor of $\dfrac{W}{2L}$. In KTU exam problems, **always check which convention the question uses**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BJT characteristics** underpin the design of **linear amplifiers, switching regulators, current mirrors, and high-frequency RF front-ends** in legacy and precision analog ICs.
- **MOSFET characteristics** are the bedrock of **digital CMOS logic, power electronics (SMPS, motor drives, inverters), RF power amplifiers, and every modern microprocessor** — billions of MOSFETs share a single silicon die in today's CPUs/GPUs.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU lab, the **V-I curve of a power MOSFET (e.g., IRF540)** is the basis for designing **PWM inverter drives** for induction motors — a frequent application-oriented viva question.
