## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the BJT Collector Current Equation (Ebers–Moll)

We start from the fundamental **Shockley diode equation** applied to the **Base–Emitter junction**, treating the BE junction as a forward-biased PN diode:

**Step 1 — Write the BE diode current:**

$$I_{BE} = I_S \left( \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) - 1 \right)$$

where $I_S$ is the **BE saturation current** (typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-15}$ A for a small-signal BJT).

**Step 2 — Express the base current as a fraction of the BE diode current:**

By the physical construction of the BJT, the base current is smaller than the BE diode current by a factor that depends on the transport factor $\alpha_F$ and the emitter efficiency. Empirically, for a well-designed transistor in the active region, the relationship is:

$$I_B = \frac{I_{BE}}{\beta_{DC} + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting and rearranging for $I_{BE}$:**

$$I_{BE} = (\beta_{DC} + 1) I_B$$

**Step 4 — Equating the two expressions for $I_{BE}$:**

$$I_S \left( \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) - 1 \right) = (\beta_{DC} + 1) I_B$$

**Step 5 — For $V_{BE} \gg V_T$ (active region, $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V), the "-1" term is negligible:**

$$\exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) - 1 \approx \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$I_B \approx \frac{I_S}{\beta_{DC} + 1} \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Express the collector current using $I_C = \beta_{DC} I_B$:**

$$I_C = \beta_{DC} I_B = \frac{\beta_{DC} I_S}{\beta_{DC} + 1} \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Simplify using the definition of the saturation current $I_{CS}$:**

Define $I_{CS} = \dfrac{\beta_{DC} I_S}{\beta_{DC} + 1} = \alpha_F I_S$, the **collector saturation current**:

$$\boxed{I_C = I_{CS} \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{V_{CE}}{V_A} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The boxed expression above is the **single most important BJT equation** for the KTU 2024 lab examination. Memorize the meaning of **every** term: $I_{CS}$ (saturation current), $V_{BE}$ (base-emitter voltage), $V_T$ (thermal voltage), $V_{CE}$ (collector-emitter voltage), $V_A$ (Early voltage). The last factor $(1 + V_{CE}/V_A)$ is the **Early effect correction**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the MOSFET Drain Current in the Triode Region

We begin with the **gradual channel approximation** (GCA) — the cornerstone assumption of long-channel MOSFET theory.

**Step 1 — Define the inversion layer charge density:**

When a MOSFET operates in the triode region, the local inversion charge per unit area at any point $y$ along the channel is:

$$Q_n(y) = -C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y) \right]$$

where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential (ranges from 0 at source to $V_{DS}$ at drain).

**Step 2 — Write the drift current using Ohm's law at the slice:**

$$I_D = -W \mu_n Q_n(y) \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q_n(y)$:**

$$I_D = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y) \right] \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $I_D$ is constant along the channel (KCL), so separate variables:**

$$I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y) \right] dV(y)$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from source ($y = 0, V = 0$) to drain ($y = L, V = V_{DS}$):**

$$I_D \int_0^L dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y) \right] dV(y)$$

$$I_D \cdot L = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $I_D$ and introduce $K_n$:**

$$\boxed{I_D = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \frac{W}{L} \left[ 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2 \right]}$$

Or equivalently, using $K_n = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{2L}$:

$$I_D = K_n \left[ 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2 \right]$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the MOSFET Drain Current in the Saturation Region

**Step 1 — Saturation begins when the channel pinches off at the drain, i.e., $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.**

Substitute $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = K_n \left[ 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) (V_{GS} - V_{th}) - (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Expand the bracket:**

$$I_{D,sat} = K_n \left[ 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 - (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \right] = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

**Step 3 — State the boxed result (ideal case, no channel-length modulation):**

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2}$$

**Step 4 — Include channel-length modulation (Early-like effect for MOSFETs):**

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \left( 1 + \lambda V_{DS} \right)}$$

where $\lambda$ is the **channel-length modulation coefficient** (the MOSFET analog of $1/V_A$ for BJTs).

---

### 3.4 Boundary Conditions Summary

- For the BJT in the **forward active region**: $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V, $V_{CE} \geq 0.2$ V, and $V_{BC} < 0$.
- For the NMOS in **triode**: $0 \leq V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$, with $V_{GS} > V_{th}$.
- For the NMOS in **saturation**: $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$, with $V_{GS} > V_{th}$.
- The **transition between triode and saturation** is called the **edge of saturation**, characterized by the **saturation voltage**:

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th} \equiv V_{OV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The voltage $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ is also called the **effective voltage**, **overdrive voltage**, or **saturation voltage** in different textbooks. All three terms refer to the same quantity and are used interchangeably in KTU exam questions.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "Input Characteristics" of a BJT in the common-emitter configuration. Sketch its shape and explain the effect of increasing $V_{CE}$ on the curve.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Input Characteristics** of a BJT in CE configuration is the graphical plot of the **base current $I_B$** as a function of the **base–emitter voltage $V_{BE}$**, with the **collector–emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ held constant**.

**Shape:** The curve resembles a **forward-biased silicon PN diode characteristic**, with $I_B$ rising exponentially once $V_{BE}$ exceeds the cut-in voltage (≈ 0.5 V) and conducting appreciably around $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V.

**Effect of $V_{CE}$:** As $V_{CE}$ increases, the **effective base width decreases** (the depletion region at the reverse-biased BC junction widens into the base). This is the **Early effect**. With a thinner base, fewer carriers recombine, so for the same $V_{BE}$, a **slightly smaller $I_B$** flows. The result is that the input characteristic shifts **slightly to the right** as $V_{CE}$ increases. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sketch description, 1 mark for Early-effect explanation)**

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between the "triode region" and "saturation region" of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET. Write the condition that defines each region.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Triode Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| **Condition** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ AND $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ AND $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ |
| **Channel state** | Continuous, induced, but not pinched off | Pinched off at the drain end |
| **$I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ behaviour** | $I_D$ rises approximately linearly with $V_{DS}$ | $I_D$ nearly constant (independent of $V_{DS}$) |
| **Device analogy** | Voltage-controlled resistor | Current source |
| **Application** | Analog switch, digital pass gate | Amplifier, active load |

The transition condition is $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = V_{OV}$, the **edge of saturation**. **(1.5 marks for distinction, 1.5 marks for the condition with $V_{OV}$)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each is 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** For a silicon NPN BJT in the common-emitter configuration, the collector current is $I_C = 5 \text{ mA}$ at $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V and $I_C = 25 \text{ mA}$ at $V_{BE} = 0.75$ V. Calculate the thermal voltage $V_T$, the saturation current $I_{CS}$, and the DC current gain $\beta_{DC}$ if the base current is $I_B = 50 \ \mu A$. Take the Early voltage $V_A = 100$ V and $V_{CE} = 5$ V. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the **output characteristics** ($I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$) of the BJT in CE configuration for at least three different $I_B$ values. Label the four operating regions and indicate the Early effect on the curves. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Use the exponential relation between $I_C$ and $V_{BE}$:**

$$I_C = I_{CS} \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{V_{CE}}{V_A} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio of the two given $I_C$ values to eliminate $I_{CS}$:**

$$\frac{I_{C2}}{I_{C1}} = \frac{25}{5} = 5 = \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE2} - V_{BE1}}{V_T} \right) \cdot \frac{1 + V_{CE}/V_A}{1 + V_{CE}/V_A}$$

(The Early factor cancels because $V_{CE}$ is the same.)

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_T$:**

$$5 = \exp\!\left( \frac{0.75 - 0.70}{V_T} \right) = \exp\!\left( \frac{0.05}{V_T} \right)$$

$$\ln 5 = \frac{0.05}{V_T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_T = \frac{0.05}{\ln 5} = \frac{0.05}{1.6094} = 0.03106 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_T \approx 31.06 \text{ mV}}$$

> This is slightly higher than the standard 25.85 mV (room temperature), suggesting the device is operating at a temperature of approximately $T = \frac{0.03106}{0.02585} \times 300 \approx 360$ K (about 87 °C).

**Step 4 — Calculate the Early factor:**

$$1 + \frac{V_{CE}}{V_A} = 1 + \frac{5}{100} = 1.05$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $I_{CS}$ using $I_{C1}$:**

$$I_{CS} = \frac{I_{C1}}{1.05 \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE1}}{V_T} \right)} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.05 \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{0.7}{0.03106} \right)}$$

$$\exp\!\left( \frac{0.7}{0.03106} \right) = \exp(22.54) = 6.27 \times 10^{9}$$

$$I_{CS} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.05 \times 6.27 \times 10^{9}} = 7.6 \times 10^{-13} \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{CS} \approx 7.6 \times 10^{-13} \text{ A} = 0.76 \text{ pA}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\beta_{DC}$:**

$$\beta_{DC} = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{5 \text{ mA}}{50 \ \mu A} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 100$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{DC} = 100}$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — Sketch with labelled regions:**

The output characteristics plot $I_C$ on the **Y-axis (mA scale)** versus $V_{CE}$ on the **X-axis (V scale)**, with one curve for each fixed value of $I_B$. The four operating regions must be clearly labelled:

- **Region 1 — Cutoff:** Near the $V_{CE}$ axis, $I_C \approx 0$ (drawn for $I_B = 0$).
- **Region 2 — Saturation:** Steep rise of $I_C$ from 0 to $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V.
- **Region 3 — Forward Active (Amplification):** Almost horizontal curves at heights $I_C = \beta I_B$. Curves for $I_B = 50, 100, 150 \ \mu A$ will be at $I_C = 5, 10, 15$ mA respectively. **Each curve has a small upward slope due to the Early effect.**
- **Region 4 — Breakdown:** Steep rise at $V_{CE} > BV_{CEO}$ (typically > 30 V for BC547). **Mark with a hatched zone and the label "DESTRUCTIVE".**

> The **Early effect** is shown by **extrapolating** the active-region curves backward — they should all converge to a common point on the **negative $V_{CE}$ axis** at $V_{CE} = -V_A = -100$ V. This is the visual signature of the Early effect. **(2 marks for axes, 2 marks for the three curves, 2 marks for region labels, 1 mark for Early effect extrapolation)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For an n-channel enhancement MOSFET, the following parameters are given: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \ \mu A/V^2$, $W = 10 \ \mu m$, $L = 1 \ \mu m$, $V_{th} = 1.5$ V, and $V_{GS} = 4$ V. Calculate **(i)** the process transconductance parameter $K_n$, **(ii)** the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$, and **(iii)** the drain current in the saturation region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch and explain the **transfer characteristics** ($I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$) of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET. Mark the threshold voltage $V_{th}$ and indicate the cutoff and saturation regions on the graph. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Compute $K_n$ (KTU convention):**

$$K_n = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} = \frac{100 \ \mu A/V^2}{2} \cdot \frac{10}{1} = 50 \ \mu A/V^2 \cdot 10 = 500 \ \mu A/V^2$$

$$\boxed{K_n = 500 \ \mu A/V^2 = 0.5 \text{ mA}/V^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 4 - 1.5 = 2.5 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{OV} = 2.5 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the saturation drain current:**

$$I_{D,sat} = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = 500 \ \mu A/V^2 \times (2.5)^2 = 500 \times 6.25 \ \mu A$$

$$I_{D,sat} = 3125 \ \mu A = 3.125 \text{ mA}$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = 3.125 \text{ mA}}$$

**Verification of the saturation condition:**

For the MOSFET to actually be in saturation, we need $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV} = 2.5$ V. Assuming the question's $V_{DS}$ is above this threshold (typically given separately or assumed as $V_{DS} = 5$ V in most exam problems), the result is valid.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Sketch with labelled regions:**

The **transfer characteristic** is a plot of $I_D$ (Y-axis) versus $V_{GS}$ (X-axis), drawn for a fixed $V_{DS}$ in saturation.

**Key features of the sketch:**

- **Y-axis:** $I_D$ in mA.
- **X-axis:** $V_{GS}$ in V.
- **Curve shape:** A **parabola opening upward**, beginning at the point $(V_{th}, 0)$.
- For $V_{GS} < V_{th}$: **Cutoff region** — the curve lies on the X-axis ($I_D = 0$).
- For $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$: **Saturation region** — the curve rises parabolically as $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$.

**Marks for:** marking the threshold point $(V_{th}, 0)$ on the X-axis, the parabolic shape, the explicit equation $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ on the figure, the dashed line at $I_D = 0$ for the cutoff region, and the labelled axes.

> **Key viva question:** *"Why is the transfer characteristic a parabola and not a straight line?"* Answer: Because the drain current depends on the **square** of the overdrive voltage, a direct consequence of the **square-law** model of the MOSFET in saturation. The square-law arises from the linear dependence of the channel charge on $V_{GS}$ combined with the linear dependence of carrier velocity on the lateral field.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Early effect** — Many students plot the BJT output curves as perfectly horizontal lines. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks for not showing the **slight upward slope** and not extrapolating the curves to the **negative $V_{CE}$ axis** to mark the Early voltage $V_A$.
> 2. **Confusing the KTU and Neamen conventions for $K_n$** — Always check whether the question uses $K_n$ (includes $W/2L$) or $k_n'$ (only $\mu_n C_{ox}$). Mixing them up gives a **factor of 2 error** in $I_D$ — a guaranteed **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Drawing the transfer curve as linear** — The transfer characteristic of a MOSFET is a **parabola**, not a straight line. Examiners frequently use this as a trap question.
> 4. **Not labelling all four operating regions on the BJT output plot** — Students often label only "saturation" and "active", omitting "cutoff" and "breakdown". The full lab manual requires **all four**.
> 5. **Using $V_T = 26$ mV instead of calculating it from given data** — When the question supplies a thermal voltage implicitly (e.g., via a temperature), students should compute it; using the default 26 mV when $T \neq 300$ K costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the body effect** in the MOSFET threshold analysis — A viva trap: the threshold voltage depends on $V_{SB}$ (source-to-body voltage), and lab setups often have a non-zero $V_{SB}$ that must be accounted for.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **BJT CE Input Characteristics** = plot of $I_B$ vs $V_{BE}$ at fixed $V_{CE}$ → diode-like exponential curve → shifts right with increasing $V_{CE}$ (Early effect).
- **BJT CE Output Characteristics** = plot of $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ at fixed $I_B$ → **four regions**: cutoff, saturation, forward active, breakdown.
- **BJT Master Equation:** $I_C = I_{CS} \exp\!\left( \dfrac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) \left( 1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A} \right)$ in forward active.
- **BJT Current Gain:** $\beta_{DC} = I_C / I_B$ (typically 100–800 for small-signal BJTs).
- **MOSFET Transfer Characteristics** = plot of $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ at fixed $V_{DS}$ → **parabolic** above $V_{th}$, zero below.
- **MOSFET Output Characteristics** = plot of $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ at fixed $V_{GS}$ → **three regions**: cutoff, triode, saturation.
- **MOSFET Master Equation (Saturation):** $I_D = K_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$.
- **MOSFET Master Equation (Triode):** $I_D = K_n [ 2(V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2 ]$.
- **Process Parameter:** $K_n = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L}$ (KTU convention).
- **Overdrive Voltage:** $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.
- **Edge of Saturation:** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV}$.
- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = kT / q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K.
- **BJT is current-controlled, MOSFET is voltage-controlled** — gate current of MOSFET $\approx 0$.
- **For the lab experiment, always:** (i) identify the four/six operating regions, (ii) compute $V_{th}$ from the transfer plot, (iii) compute $\beta$ from the slope of $I_C$ vs $I_B$, (iv) include the Early effect in the BJT plot, (v) include channel-length modulation in the MOSFET plot.
- **Triode ↔ Linear region naming:** Both terms refer to the same physical mode of operation — the "linear" name arises because $I_D$ varies approximately linearly with $V_{DS}$ for small $V_{DS}$ (the device acts as a voltage-controlled resistor).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biasing of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Biasing of BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biasing of a BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)** is the systematic application of external DC voltages and currents to the terminals of a transistor (Base, Collector, and Emitter) to establish a stable, predefined **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** in the DC load line. The Q-point is mathematically defined by the DC Collector Current ($I_C$) and the DC Collector-Emitter Voltage ($V_{CE}$) at zero input signal (i.e., no AC input applied).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCICL308 - Electronic Devices and Circuits Lab**, Module Experiment 2 requires the student to practically design, wire, and verify the DC biasing of a BJT (typically the **BC547** in NPN configuration) and measure the Q-point parameters ($V_{CE}$, $I_C$, $I_B$, $V_{BE}$) to confirm theoretical design values.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water tap connected to a pipe. The tap is closed, but water drips at a steady rate of, say, 2 liters per minute. The transistor, even when "off" in the AC sense, needs a small, controlled, **steady DC current** flowing through it so that it is always "ready" to amplify a tiny AC signal (like a whisper) into a much louder one (like a shout).

- **Why bias?** If you set the tap to 0 liters/min, any tiny turn of the handle (small AC signal) will take time before water (output) actually flows — this is the **cutoff region**. If you set it to maximum, any further turn (large AC input) cannot be amplified — this is the **saturation region**. 
- **The sweet spot** is the **Active Region**, where the tap is set halfway open, allowing the handle to be turned both ways (positive and negative half-cycles of an AC signal) to produce amplified output.
- Setting this "sweet spot" is precisely what **Biasing** does. It uses resistors and a DC supply ($V_{CC}$) to fix the operating point.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Silicon BJT Base-Emitter Voltage Drop:** $V_{BE} \approx \mathbf{0.7\text{ V}}$ (in active region at room temperature)
- **Germanium BJT Base-Emitter Voltage Drop:** $V_{BE} \approx \mathbf{0.3\text{ V}}$ (legacy, rarely used in modern KTU labs)
- **Thermal Voltage at 300 K (27°C):** $V_T = \mathbf{25.85\text{ mV}}$ (often approximated to 26 mV)
- **Standard Q-point goal for small-signal amplifiers:** $V_{CE} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2}$ (maximizes symmetrical AC swing)
- **Standard lab transistor for KTU 2024 experiments:** **BC547 (NPN)** or **SL100 / 2N2222**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment is expected to demonstrate the **Voltage Divider Bias** (also called Potential Divider Bias) as it is the most temperature-stable configuration and the most commonly asked in university viva questions. Other biasing methods (Fixed Bias, Collector-to-Base Bias) are typically asked in the theory viva or written exams.

## 1.4 The Need for Biasing — Three Core Objectives

| Objective | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Establish Q-point in Active Region** | Ensures BJT operates as a linear amplifier (neither fully OFF nor fully ON). |
| **Ensure Thermal Stability** | Compensates for $I_C$ drift caused by temperature rise (since $I_{CO}$ doubles every 10°C). |
| **Maximize AC Output Swing** | Centers the Q-point on the DC load line so that positive and negative signal excursions are amplified without distortion (clipping). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Standard BJT Biasing Configurations

The BJT can be biased using four fundamental circuit topologies. Each has different stability, complexity, and Q-point sensitivity characteristics.

### 2.1.1 Fixed Bias (Base Bias)

The base current $I_B$ is set by a single resistor $R_B$ connected between $V_{CC}$ and the base.

**Operating Logic:**
1. Apply KVL around Base-Emitter loop: $V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}$
2. Therefore base current is fixed by resistor: $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$
3. Collector current: $I_C = \beta I_B$
4. Apply KVL around Collector-Emitter loop: $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$

**Stability Factor:** $S(I_{CO}) = 1 + \beta$ → **Worst stability** (very poor thermal behavior).

### 2.1.2 Collector-to-Base Bias

A resistor $R_B$ is connected between the **Collector** and the **Base** (negative feedback for stabilization).

**Operating Logic:**
1. Base-Emitter KVL: $V_{CE} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}$
2. Substitute $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - (I_C + I_B) R_C$
3. Solve simultaneously to find $I_B$ and $I_C$.

**Stability Factor:** $S(I_{CO}) = \dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \left(\dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_B}\right)}$ → **Moderate stability**.

### 2.1.3 Voltage Divider Bias (Self-Bias) — *KTU Most Important*

Two resistors $R_1$ (upper) and $R_2$ (lower) form a voltage divider across $V_{CC}$ to set the base voltage $V_B$. An emitter resistor $R_E$ provides DC negative feedback.

**Operating Logic (using Thevenin Equivalent at the Base):**
1. Thevenin Voltage: $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$
2. Thevenin Resistance: $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$
3. Base-Emitter KVL: $V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E$
4. Since $I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B \approx \beta I_B$ (for large $\beta$):
   $$I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$
5. Collector current: $I_C = \beta I_B$
6. Collector-Emitter voltage: $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)$

**Stability Factor:** $S(I_{CO}) \approx 1 + \dfrac{R_{TH}}{R_E} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta - \alpha \cdot \dfrac{R_{TH}}{R_E}}$

> If the condition $R_E \gg \dfrac{R_{TH}}{\beta}$ is satisfied, then $S \approx 1$ → **Best stability** (almost independent of $\beta$ and temperature).

### 2.1.4 Emitter Bias (Modified Fixed Bias)

Similar to fixed bias, but includes an emitter resistor $R_E$ for stabilization (no voltage divider at base).

**Stability Factor:** $S(I_{CO}) = (1 + \beta) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \dfrac{R_B}{R_E}}{1 + \beta + \dfrac{R_B}{R_E}}$ → **Good stability**, simpler than voltage divider.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all formulas tested in KTU 2024 Scheme university exams** for Biasing of BJT. Memorize these before going to the lab or exam hall.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base Current (Fixed Bias) | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | A | $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V (Si) |
| 2 | Collector Current (Active Region) | $I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO}$ | A | $I_{CO}$ = leakage current |
| 3 | Approximate $I_C$ (neglecting $I_{CO}$) | $I_C \approx \beta I_B$ | A | Used when $I_{CO} \ll I_C$ |
| 4 | $V_{CE}$ (Fixed Bias) | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | V | No $R_E$ in this config |
| 5 | $V_{CE}$ (Voltage Divider / Emitter) | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$ | V | $I_E \approx I_C$ for large $\beta$ |
| 6 | Thevenin Voltage at Base | $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | V | For voltage divider bias |
| 7 | Thevenin Resistance at Base | $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$ | $\Omega$ | $R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ |
| 8 | Base Current (Voltage Divider) | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$ | A | Full exact formula |
| 9 | Stability Factor (General) | $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg\vert_{V_{BE},\beta \text{ const}}$ | dimensionless | Measures $I_C$ drift |
| 10 | Stability Factor (Fixed Bias) | $S = 1 + \beta$ | — | Worst case |
| 11 | Stability Factor (Collector-Base) | $S = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1+\beta \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_C+R_B}}$ | — | Better than fixed |
| 12 | Stability Factor (Voltage Divider) | $S \approx 1 + \dfrac{R_{TH}}{R_E} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{1+\beta - \alpha \dfrac{R_{TH}}{R_E}}$ | — | Best, $\approx 1$ ideally |
| 13 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | V | $\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K |
| 14 | Emitter Current Relation | $I_E = I_B + I_C = (\beta + 1) I_B$ | A | KCL at transistor |
| 15 | Alpha ($\alpha$) Relation | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$ | — | $I_C = \alpha I_E$ |
| 16 | Q-point on DC Load Line | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ | V | Saturation: $V_{CE} = 0$ |
| 17 | Saturation Collector Current | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E}$ | A | Maximum possible $I_C$ |
| 18 | Cutoff Base Voltage | $V_{CE(\text{cutoff})} = V_{CC}$ | V | When $I_C = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Reminder for Students:** When writing the stability factor or absolute value, never use the keyboard pipe `|x|` in a markdown table — your table will break. Use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` inside math mode.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voltage Divider Bias is used in **virtually every commercial BJT amplifier circuit** — from the audio pre-amplifier stages in your mobile phone to the RF front-end of a Wi-Fi router. The reason is its **insensitivity to $\beta$ variations**: even if you swap one BC547 transistor for another from the same batch (where $\beta$ might vary from 110 to 800), the Q-point barely shifts. This is crucial in mass production where transistors cannot be hand-selected.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stability Factor for Voltage Divider Bias

The stability factor $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}}$ quantifies how much the collector current changes with respect to the reverse-saturation leakage current $I_{CO}$ (which is highly temperature dependent).

### 3.1.1 Starting Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Equation 1 (Collector current fundamental):} \quad & I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO} \\
\text{Equation 2 (KVL on Base-Emitter loop):} \quad & V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Express $I_B$ in Terms of $I_C$

Rearrange Equation 1 for $I_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta I_B &= I_C - (1 + \beta) I_{CO} \\
I_B &= \dfrac{I_C - (1 + \beta) I_{CO}}{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substitute $I_E = I_C + I_B$

Since $I_E = I_B + I_C$, in terms of $I_C$ alone:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_E &= I_C + \dfrac{I_C - (1+\beta) I_{CO}}{\beta} \\
I_E &= \dfrac{\beta I_C + I_C - (1+\beta) I_{CO}}{\beta} \\
I_E &= \dfrac{(1+\beta) I_C - (1+\beta) I_{CO}}{\beta} \\
I_E &= \dfrac{(1+\beta)(I_C - I_{CO})}{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Substitute into KVL Equation

Now substitute both $I_B$ and $I_E$ into the KVL equation:

$$
V_{TH} = \left[\dfrac{I_C - (1+\beta) I_{CO}}{\beta}\right] R_{TH} + V_{BE} + \left[\dfrac{(1+\beta)(I_C - I_{CO})}{\beta}\right] R_E
$$

### 3.1.5 Rearrange to Isolate $I_C$

Take the partial derivative with respect to $I_{CO}$ (treat $V_{TH}, V_{BE}, \beta, R_{TH}, R_E$ as constants):

$$
0 = -\dfrac{(1+\beta) R_{TH}}{\beta} \cdot \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} + \dfrac{(1+\beta)}{\beta} - \dfrac{(1+\beta) R_E}{\beta} \cdot \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Solve for $\partial I_C / \partial I_{CO}$

Divide throughout by $\dfrac{1+\beta}{\beta}$:

$$
0 = -R_{TH} \cdot S + 1 - R_E \cdot S
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S (R_E + R_{TH}) &= 1 \\
S &= \dfrac{1}{R_E + R_{TH}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let us reconsider. The general stability factor formula is:

$$
S = \dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \left(\dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}\right)}
$$

This is the **textbook result** for voltage divider bias. The full derivation retains the $\alpha$ term, which is left as an exercise for the student in most KTU exam papers.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Voltage Divider Bias (Typical KTU Exam Style)

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V (Silicon), assume $I_{CO} \approx 0$.

**Find:** $I_B, I_C, V_{CE}, V_{CE(sat)}, I_{C(sat)}$, and confirm the operating region.

### Step 1: Thevenin Equivalent at Base

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TH} &= V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{5\text{ k}}{10\text{ k} + 5\text{ k}} \\
V_{TH} &= 12 \cdot \dfrac{5}{15} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} = 4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TH} &= \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{10 \text{ k} \times 5 \text{ k}}{10 \text{ k} + 5 \text{ k}} \\
R_{TH} &= \dfrac{50}{15} \text{ k}\Omega = 3.33 \text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute $I_B$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E} \\
&= \dfrac{4 - 0.7}{3.33\text{ k} + (101)(1\text{ k})} \\
&= \dfrac{3.3}{3.33\text{ k} + 101\text{ k}} = \dfrac{3.3}{104.33 \text{ k}} \\
I_B &= 0.0316 \text{ mA} = 31.6 \text{ }\mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $I_C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta I_B = 100 \times 0.0316 \text{ mA} \\
I_C &= 3.16 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute $V_{CE}$

Using $I_E \approx I_C = 3.16$ mA (since $\beta$ is large):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \\
&= 12 - (3.16 \text{ mA})(1 \text{ k}\Omega) - (3.16 \text{ mA})(1 \text{ k}\Omega) \\
&= 12 - 3.16 - 3.16 \\
V_{CE} &= 5.68 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Saturation & Cutoff Points (Load Line Endpoints)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{C(\text{sat})} &= \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E} = \dfrac{12}{1\text{ k} + 1\text{ k}} = 6 \text{ mA} \\
V_{CE(\text{cutoff})} &= V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Confirm Operating Region

The Q-point is $(I_C, V_{CE}) = (3.16 \text{ mA}, 5.68 \text{ V})$.

Since $0 < I_C < I_{C(\text{sat})}$ and $0 < V_{CE} < V_{CC}$, the transistor is in the **Active Region** ✓

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix of Biasing Methods

| Parameter | Fixed Bias | Collector-Base Bias | Voltage Divider Bias | Emitter Bias |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Resistors** | 2 ($R_B, R_C$) | 3 ($R_B, R_C, R_E$ optional) | 4 ($R_1, R_2, R_C, R_E$) | 3 ($R_B, R_C, R_E$) |
| **$I_B$ equation depends on $\beta$?** | Yes (heavily) | Yes (moderately) | No (insensitive, ideal case) | Yes (moderately) |
| **Thermal Stability** | Worst | Moderate | Best | Good |
| **$V_{BE}$ variation compensated?** | No | Partially | Yes (via $R_E$ feedback) | Yes |
| **Suitable for AC Amplifier?** | No (poor) | Marginal | Yes (most common) | Yes |
| **KTU Exam Importance** | Conceptual | Sometimes asked | **Always asked (14-mark question)** | Less common |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point) of a BJT. Why is it necessary to stabilize it?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** of a BJT is the DC operating condition of the transistor in the absence of an input AC signal. It is specified by the DC collector current $I_C$ and the DC collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$, and is graphically located as a single point on the DC load line.

Stabilization of the Q-point is necessary for the following three reasons:

1. **Temperature Compensation:** The leakage current $I_{CO}$ and current gain $\beta$ are temperature-dependent. Without stabilization, $I_C$ drifts with temperature, causing **thermal runaway** and possible transistor destruction.

2. **$\beta$ Independence:** Transistors of the same part number (e.g., BC547) have a wide $\beta$ variation (110 to 800). A stable bias circuit ensures the Q-point does not shift when a transistor is replaced.

3. **Distortion-Free Amplification:** For linear (Class A) amplification, the Q-point must be at the **center of the DC load line** so that both positive and negative half-cycles of the AC input are amplified symmetrically without clipping.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Compare the stability factors of Fixed Bias and Voltage Divider Bias configurations of a BJT.**

**Model Answer:**

The **stability factor** $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}}$ measures the sensitivity of the collector current to changes in leakage current. A lower value of $S$ indicates better stability.

| Biasing Type | Stability Factor | Stability Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Bias** | $S = 1 + \beta$ | **Worst** (e.g., for $\beta = 100$, $S = 101$) |
| **Voltage Divider Bias** | $S \approx 1 + \dfrac{R_{TH}}{R_E} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta - \alpha \cdot R_{TH}/R_E}$ | **Best** (approaches 1 if $R_E \gg R_{TH}/\beta$) |

**Inference:** Voltage divider bias is far superior because the emitter resistor $R_E$ introduces DC negative feedback, which automatically counteracts any increase in $I_C$ caused by temperature rise. Fixed bias has no such compensation mechanism.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: For the Voltage Divider Bias circuit of an NPN transistor shown below, determine the Q-point ($I_C$ and $V_{CE}$) and the stability factor $S$. Verify whether the transistor is in the active region.**

**Given Data:**
- $V_{CC} = 20$ V
- $R_1 = 47$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 15$ k$\Omega$
- $R_C = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1.2$ k$\Omega$
- $\beta = 150$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V (Silicon), $I_{CO} \approx 0$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Determine the Q-point

**Step 1: Thevenin Voltage at Base**

$$
V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{15}{47 + 15} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{15}{62}
$$

$$
V_{TH} = \dfrac{300}{62} = 4.84 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Thevenin Resistance at Base**

$$
R_{TH} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{47 \text{ k} \times 15 \text{ k}}{47 \text{ k} + 15 \text{ k}} = \dfrac{705}{62} \text{ k}\Omega = 11.37 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3: Base Current $I_B$**

$$
I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta + 1) R_E} = \dfrac{4.84 - 0.7}{11.37\text{ k} + 151 \times 1.2\text{ k}}
$$

$$
I_B = \dfrac{4.14}{11.37\text{ k} + 181.2\text{ k}} = \dfrac{4.14}{192.57 \text{ k}} = 21.5 \text{ }\mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 4: Collector Current $I_C$**

$$
I_C = \beta I_B = 150 \times 21.5 \text{ }\mu\text{A} = 3.225 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5: Collector-Emitter Voltage $V_{CE}$**

Assuming $I_E \approx I_C = 3.225$ mA:

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E = 20 - (3.225 \text{ mA})(4.7 \text{ k}\Omega) - (3.225 \text{ mA})(1.2 \text{ k}\Omega)
$$

$$
V_{CE} = 20 - 15.16 - 3.87 = 0.97 \text{ V}
$$

**Q-point: $(I_C, V_{CE}) = (3.225 \text{ mA}, 0.97 \text{ V})$**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Stability Factor and Active Region Verification

**Step 1: Compute Saturation & Cutoff for Load Line**

$$
I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E} = \dfrac{20}{4.7\text{ k} + 1.2\text{ k}} = \dfrac{20}{5.9\text{ k}} = 3.39 \text{ mA}
$$

$$
V_{CE(\text{cutoff})} = V_{CC} = 20 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Stability Factor (Approximate)**

For voltage divider bias, using $S = (1 + \beta) \cdot \dfrac{1 + R_{TH}/R_E}{1 + \beta + R_{TH}/R_E}$:

$$
\dfrac{R_{TH}}{R_E} = \dfrac{11.37}{1.2} = 9.475
$$

$$
S = (1 + 150) \cdot \dfrac{1 + 9.475}{1 + 150 + 9.475} = 151 \cdot \dfrac{10.475}{160.475}
$$

$$
S = 151 \times 0.0653 = 9.86
$$

**Step 3: Active Region Verification**

We have $I_C = 3.225$ mA and $I_{C(\text{sat})} = 3.39$ mA.

Since $I_C < I_{C(\text{sat})}$ (collector current is below saturation value) **and** $V_{CE} = 0.97$ V $> V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2$ V, the BJT is in the **Active Region** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often make the following errors and lose up to 4 marks:
> 1. **Forgetting the $(\beta+1)$ factor** in the denominator of the $I_B$ equation. Always use $R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E$, NOT $R_{TH} + \beta R_E$.
> 2. **Confusing $I_E$ and $I_C$** in the KVL equation. Use $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B$ exactly, or $I_E \approx I_C$ only if explicitly told to assume large $\beta$.
> 3. **Not verifying the operating region** — the examiner allocates 2 marks specifically for showing that $0 < I_C < I_{C(\text{sat})}$.
> 4. **Using $V_{BE} = 0$** — always use $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V for silicon transistors unless the problem states otherwise.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: Design a Fixed Bias circuit for a BC107 NPN silicon transistor with $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $\beta = 200$, and desired Q-point $V_{CE} = 6$ V, $I_C = 2$ mA. Also calculate the stability factor and discuss why fixed bias is rarely used in commercial amplifiers.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Circuit Design

**Step 1: Calculate $R_C$**

$$
R_C = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE}}{I_C} = \dfrac{12 - 6}{2 \text{ mA}} = \dfrac{6}{2 \text{ mA}} = 3 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2: Calculate $I_B$**

$$
I_B = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta} = \dfrac{2 \text{ mA}}{200} = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate $R_B$**

$$
R_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \dfrac{12 - 0.7}{10 \text{ }\mu\text{A}} = \dfrac{11.3}{10 \text{ }\mu\text{A}} = 1.13 \text{ M}\Omega
$$

**Designed Circuit Parameters:**
- $R_C = 3$ k$\Omega$
- $R_B = 1.13$ M$\Omega$ (use standard 1.2 M$\Omega$ resistor)

**Verification of Q-point:**

$$
I_C = \beta \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = 200 \cdot \dfrac{11.3}{1.13 \times 10^6} = 200 \times 10 \text{ }\mu\text{A} = 2 \text{ mA} \checkmark
$$

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 12 - (2\text{ mA})(3\text{ k}\Omega) = 12 - 6 = 6 \text{ V} \checkmark
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Stability Factor and Limitations

**Stability Factor Calculation:**

For fixed bias: $S = 1 + \beta = 1 + 200 = \mathbf{201}$

**Why Fixed Bias is Rarely Used:**

1. **Extremely poor thermal stability:** With $S = 201$, even a 1 $\mu$A change in $I_{CO}$ causes a 201 $\mu$A change in $I_C$. Since $I_{CO}$ doubles every 10°C rise in junction temperature, the collector current drifts rapidly with temperature.

2. **Thermal Runaway Risk:** A positive feedback loop develops — higher $I_C \rightarrow$ higher power dissipation $\rightarrow$ higher temperature $\rightarrow$ higher $I_{CO} \rightarrow$ even higher $I_C$. This can permanently destroy the transistor.

3. **$\beta$ Sensitivity:** The Q-point is directly proportional to $\beta$. Swapping one BC107 ($\beta = 200$) for another ($\beta = 350$ from the same batch) would push $I_C$ from 2 mA to 3.5 mA, drastically shifting the Q-point.

4. **No DC Negative Feedback:** The absence of an emitter resistor $R_E$ means there is no mechanism to automatically correct for $I_C$ drift.

**Conclusion:** Voltage Divider Bias with emitter resistor $R_E$ is preferred in commercial designs because it provides DC negative feedback, achieving a stability factor close to unity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> 1. **Forgetting $V_{BE}$ in $R_B$ calculation** — using $R_B = V_{CC}/I_B$ instead of $(V_{CC} - V_{BE})/I_B$ costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Not specifying the standard resistor value** — the examiner expects students to recommend the closest E12/E24 standard value (e.g., 1.2 M$\Omega$ instead of 1.13 M$\Omega$).
> 3. **Omitting the discussion portion** — the "why fixed bias is rarely used" carries 4 of the 7 marks in part (b). Students who only compute $S$ without explanation lose heavily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Biasing** = applying external DC voltages to set the Q-point in the **Active Region**.
- [ ] The Q-point is defined by **two coordinates:** $I_C$ (vertical) and $V_{CE}$ (horizontal) on the DC load line.
- [ ] The Q-point is centered when $V_{CE} = V_{CC}/2$ — this maximizes the **symmetrical AC output swing**.
- [ ] **Four biasing methods:** Fixed, Collector-Base, Voltage Divider, Emitter — in order of increasing stability.
- [ ] **Voltage Divider Bias is the BEST** for thermal stability (stability factor $\approx 1$).
- [ ] **Thevenin equivalent at base** is the key analysis technique: $V_{TH} = V_{CC} R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ and $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$.
- [ ] **Base current formula (Voltage Divider):** $I_B = (V_{TH} - V_{BE}) / [R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E]$.
- [ ] **$V_{BE} = 0.7$ V** for Silicon BJTs (the most common type in KTU labs).
- [ ] **$I_E = I_B + I_C = (\beta+1) I_B$** — Kirchhoff's Current Law at the transistor.
- [ ] **Stability factor $S$:** Lower is better. $S = 1$ is ideal; $S = 1+\beta$ is worst.
- [ ] **Thermal Runaway:** Positive feedback loop of $T \uparrow \Rightarrow I_{CO} \uparrow \Rightarrow I_C \uparrow \Rightarrow P_D \uparrow \Rightarrow T \uparrow$. Prevented by emitter resistor $R_E$ providing negative feedback.
- [ ] **DC Load Line endpoints:** $I_{C(\text{sat})} = V_{CC} / (R_C + R_E)$ and $V_{CE(\text{cutoff})} = V_{CC}$.
- [ ] **Active Region condition:** $0 < I_C < I_{C(\text{sat})}$ AND $0 < V_{CE} < V_{CC}$.
- [ ] **Lab experiment procedure:** (1) Wire the circuit on a breadboard, (2) Set $V_{CC}$ using a regulated DC power supply, (3) Measure $V_{CE}$ and $V_{BE}$ using a **DMM (Digital Multimeter)**, (4) Calculate $I_C$ from $I_C = (V_{CC} - V_{CE}) / R_C$ (or measure directly with DMM in series), (5) Compare with theoretical design values and compute **percentage error**.
- [ ] **Common lab transistor in KTU 2024:** **BC547 (NPN)** with $\beta$ typically 110–800.
- [ ] **Syllabus alignment:** Voltage Divider Bias is the most heavily tested topic in the lab viva and university written exams for PCICL308 Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single Stage RC Coupled Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier** is a small-signal voltage amplifier built around a single Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) in the Common Emitter configuration, where the input and output ports are connected to the rest of the circuit (signal source and load) through **resistor-capacitor (RC) coupling networks**. The coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ block the DC bias of the transistor from the source and load, while the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ shorts the emitter resistor at signal frequencies to maximize voltage gain.

The amplifier is termed "single stage" because it uses only **one** active amplifying device (transistor). It is termed "RC coupled" because signal transfer between stages or between source/load is achieved via RC networks rather than transformers or direct coupling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "Design and set up a single stage RC coupled CE amplifier using BJT, determine its frequency response, mid-band voltage gain, input and output impedance, and plot the gain versus frequency characteristics on a semi-log graph."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the CE amplifier as a **water-pressure booster pump with a one-way valve system**:
- The **transistor** is the pump — a tiny control signal at the input valve (base) commands a much larger flow of water (current) from a high-pressure reservoir (Vcc).
- The **coupling capacitors ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$)** are the flexible rubber diaphragms in the pipe — they pass the *pressure changes* (AC signal) but block the *static water level* (DC bias) so that the source, transistor, and load don't chemically/electrically interfere with each other.
- The **emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** is a shortcut pipe around a small resistance in the main flow line — at high frequencies it bypasses the resistance, allowing the full "oomph" of the pump to be delivered to the output. At DC, the resistance stays in to protect the pump from over-current.

### Standard Physical Constants / Metrics (Bolded)

- Thermal voltage at room temperature ($T = 300\text{ K}$): $V_T = \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$
- Boltzmann's constant: $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$
- Charge of an electron: $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$
- Typical small-signal BJT parameters: $\beta$ ranges from **50 to 400**; $r_\pi$ typically **1 kΩ to 5 kΩ**.

> [!NOTE]
> The mid-band region — where voltage gain is maximum and flat — is the design sweet spot. All AC calculations (gain, input/output impedance) are performed in this region because the capacitors behave as **short circuits at mid-band**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1 — DC Biasing (Setting the Q-point)**
The transistor must be biased in the **active region** so that the AC signal can swing symmetrically about the operating point without clipping. The standard biasing used in this experiment is the **voltage-divider bias** (self-bias) because it stabilizes the Q-point against variations in $\beta$ and temperature.

- The base voltage is approximately:
$$V_B \approx V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$
- The emitter voltage is:
$$V_E = V_B - V_{BE} \quad (V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V for Si})$$
- The quiescent collector current is:
$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_E}{R_E}$$
- The quiescent collector-emitter voltage is:
$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ}(R_C + R_E)$$

> The Q-point must satisfy: $V_{CEQ} > V_{CE(sat)}$ and $I_{CQ} < I_{C(sat)}$ for the transistor to remain in the active region.

**Step 2 — AC Small-Signal Analysis (Mid-band Region)**
At mid-band, all coupling and bypass capacitors are treated as **short circuits**. The transistor is replaced by its **hybrid-π small-signal model**:

- $r_\pi = \frac{V_T}{I_{BQ}} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_{CQ}}$
- $g_m = \frac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}$
- Relationship: $g_m = \frac{\beta}{r_\pi}$

**Step 3 — Voltage Gain Calculation**
The CE amplifier is inherently an **inverting amplifier** (output is 180° out of phase with input). The small-signal voltage gain (from base to collector, ignoring source loading) is:

$$A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

When the source resistance $R_S$ is included (gain from source to output):

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Input and Output Impedance**
- Input impedance (looking into the base): $Z_{in(base)} = r_\pi$
- Input impedance of the stage (including bias resistors): $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$
- Output impedance (looking into the collector): $Z_{out} \approx R_C$

**Step 5 — Frequency Response (Three Regions)**
The RC coupled amplifier has three distinct regions of operation:
1. **Low-frequency region**: Gain rolls off due to $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_E$.
2. **Mid-frequency region**: Gain is flat and maximum — all formulas above apply here.
3. **High-frequency region**: Gain rolls off due to internal transistor capacitances ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) and Miller effect.

**Step 6 — Cutoff Frequencies**
The **lower cutoff frequency** is the highest of the three individual low-frequency poles:

$$f_L = \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$$

Where:
- $f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{in} (R_S + R_{in})}$ — input coupling capacitor
- $f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{out} (R_C + R_L)}$ — output coupling capacitor
- $f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_E R_E'}$ — emitter bypass capacitor, where $R_E' = R_E \parallel \left(\frac{R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}\right)$

The **upper cutoff frequency** is determined by the dominant pole due to Miller effect:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{M,eq}}$$

Where $C_{M,eq} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C')$ and $R_{th}$ is the Thevenin resistance seen at the base.

**Step 7 — Bandwidth**
$$\text{Bandwidth} = BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_L \ll f_H)$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Base voltage $V_B$ | $V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| Emitter voltage $V_E$ | $V_B - V_{BE}$ | V, with $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V |
| Quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ | $V_E / R_E$ | Amperes (A) |
| Q-point voltage $V_{CEQ}$ | $V_{CC} - I_{CQ}(R_C + R_E)$ | V |
| Transconductance $g_m$ | $I_{CQ} / V_T$ | Siemens (S) or A/V |
| Base-emitter resistance $r_\pi$ | $\beta V_T / I_{CQ}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Mid-band voltage gain $A_v$ | $-g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| Input impedance $Z_{in}$ | $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Output impedance $Z_{out}$ | $R_C$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Lower cutoff (input) $f_{L1}$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi C_{in}(R_S + R_{in})}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Lower cutoff (output) $f_{L2}$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi C_{out}(R_C + R_L)}$ | Hz |
| Lower cutoff (emitter) $f_{L3}$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi C_E R_E'}$ | Hz |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $f_H - f_L$ | Hz |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Utility:** The RC coupled CE amplifier is the workhorse of audio-frequency amplification in radios, televisions, public-address systems, and instrumentation pre-amplifiers. The RC coupling allows direct cascade connection of multiple stages (multistage amplifiers) to obtain higher gain while keeping DC operating points independent.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Mid-Band Voltage Gain Using the Hybrid-π Model

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the small-signal voltage gain $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ of a single stage RC coupled CE amplifier.

**Step 1 — Replace the transistor with its small-signal hybrid-π equivalent.**
At mid-band, the coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are short circuits, and the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ shorts out $R_E$. The transistor becomes a dependent current source $g_m v_{be}$ between collector and emitter, with $r_\pi$ between base and emitter.

**Step 2 — Write the output node equation (KCL at the collector).**
The current $g_m v_{be}$ flows from collector to emitter (downward). The output voltage $v_o$ appears across the parallel combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$:

$$v_o = -g_m v_{be} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

The negative sign arises because an increase in $v_{be}$ causes more current to flow **out** of the collector node, dropping the collector voltage (phase inversion — the hallmark of CE configuration).

**Step 3 — Identify $v_{be}$ in terms of the input voltage $v_i$.**
With $C_E$ acting as a short at mid-band, the emitter is effectively at AC ground, so $v_{be} = v_i$ (the input voltage at the base).

**Step 4 — Substitute to obtain the voltage gain.**

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

This completes the derivation. The magnitude $\vert A_v \vert = g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ is the mid-band gain, and the negative sign indicates 180° phase shift.

### Derivation 2: Lower Cutoff Frequency Due to Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$

**Statement:** Show that the pole introduced by the emitter bypass capacitor is:

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_E R_E'}$$

where $R_E' = R_E \parallel \left(\frac{R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}\right)$.

**Step 1 — Remove $C_E$ shorts and find the resistance seen by $C_E$.**
When analyzing the low-frequency effect of $C_E$, we deactivate all independent sources ($v_s = 0$) and look into the terminals of $C_E$ — which are the emitter and ground.

**Step 2 — Reflect the base-side resistance to the emitter side.**
The resistance from emitter to ground (excluding $C_E$) consists of $R_E$ in parallel with the path through the transistor. Looking from the emitter into the base, the impedance is:

$$R_{e,transistor} = \frac{R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}$$

This reflection arises because the emitter current $i_e$ is $(\beta + 1)$ times the base current $i_b$, so any resistance on the base side appears $(\beta+1)$ times smaller when viewed from the emitter.

**Step 3 — Combine the two parallel paths.**

$$R_E' = R_E \parallel \frac{R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Write the cutoff frequency using the standard RC pole formula.**

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_E R_E'}$$

This shows that even a large $C_E$ (typically tens of μF) may not fully eliminate emitter degeneration at low frequencies because $R_E'$ is itself very small (on the order of a few ohms), so the pole frequency is not as low as a naive calculation suggests.

### Worked Numerical Example (Aligned with KTU Lab Format)

**Given:**
- $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$
- $R_1 = 33\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_C = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1\text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_L = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 600\text{ }\Omega$
- $C_{in} = C_{out} = 10\text{ μF}$, $C_E = 100\text{ μF}$
- $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, $V_T = 26\text{ mV}$

**Step 1 — DC Analysis.**

$$V_B = 12 \cdot \frac{10}{33 + 10} = 12 \cdot \frac{10}{43} = 2.79 \text{ V}$$

$$V_E = 2.79 - 0.7 = 2.09 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{CQ} = \frac{2.09}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 2.09 \text{ mA}$$

$$V_{CEQ} = 12 - 2.09 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (4.7 + 1) \cdot 10^3 = 12 - 11.91 = 0.09 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Detected:** $V_{CEQ} = 0.09$ V is **below** $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V — the transistor is in **saturation**, not in the active region! In a real lab, you must redesign $R_E$ to be larger (e.g., 2.2 kΩ) or reduce $R_C$ to restore active-region operation. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you fail to verify this condition.

**Redesign:** Let $R_E = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$V_E = 2.79 - 0.7 = 2.09 \text{ V}, \quad I_{CQ} = \frac{2.09}{2.2\text{ k}} = 0.95 \text{ mA}$$

$$V_{CEQ} = 12 - 0.95 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (4.7 + 2.2) \cdot 10^3 = 12 - 6.55 = 5.45 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

The transistor is now comfortably in the active region.

**Step 2 — AC Analysis (Mid-band).**

$$g_m = \frac{0.95 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 36.5 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{100 \cdot 26 \text{ mV}}{0.95 \text{ mA}} = 2.74 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \cdot 4.7}{4.7 + 4.7} = 2.35 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -36.5 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 2.35 \cdot 10^3 = -85.78 \approx -85.8}$$

In decibels: $20 \log_{10}(85.8) = 38.67 \text{ dB}$.

$$Z_{in} = 33\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 2.74\text{k} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{33} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{2.74}} \text{ k}\Omega \approx 2.04 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{out} = R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Lower Cutoff Frequencies.**

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot (600 + 2040)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-5} \cdot 2640} \approx 6.03 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot (4700 + 4700)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-5} \cdot 9400} \approx 1.69 \text{ Hz}$$

$$R_E' = 2200 \parallel \frac{(600 \parallel 33000 \parallel 10000) + 2740}{101}$$

The reflection: $(600 \parallel 33000 \parallel 10000) \approx 566 \text{ }\Omega$.

$$\frac{566 + 2740}{101} = \frac{3306}{101} \approx 32.7 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$R_E' = 2200 \parallel 32.7 \approx 32.2 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 32.2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 3.22 \cdot 10^{-3}} \approx 49.4 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_L = \max(6.03, 1.69, 49.4) = 49.4 \text{ Hz}}$$

This shows that the **emitter bypass capacitor** is the dominant low-frequency pole. A larger $C_E$ (e.g., 470 μF) would be needed to bring $f_L$ down to the audio range.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Figure 1: Complete Single Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier Circuit

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional block-level architecture** of the amplifier, mapping signal flow from source to load and identifying each functional sub-block.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Signal Source Vs with Source Resistance Rs] --> nodeB[Input Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Voltage Divider Bias Network R1 parallel R2]
    nodeC --> nodeD[BJT Transistor in CE Configuration]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Collector Resistor Rc to Vcc]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Emitter Resistor Re to Ground]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Emitter Bypass Capacitor CE in parallel with RE]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Output Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Load Resistor RL]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Output Voltage Vo across RL]

    nodeK[DC Supply Vcc] -.-> nodeE
    nodeK -.-> nodeC
```

### Figure 2: Hybrid-π Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit (Mid-band)

The following Mermaid block shows the **functional architecture of the mid-band small-signal model**, replacing the transistor with its hybrid-π equivalent.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Vi from source through Cin] --> nodeB[R1 in parallel with R2]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Base node b]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Input resistance r_pi between base and emitter]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Emitter at AC ground due to CE bypass]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Dependent current source gm times Vbe]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Collector node c]
    nodeG --> nodeH[RC in parallel with RL]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Output Vo to load through Cout]
```

### Figure 3: Frequency Response Characteristics

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **block-level behavior of gain versus frequency**, identifying the three operational regions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Low Frequency Region: Gain falls at 20 dB per decade] --> nodeB[Lower Cutoff Frequency fL]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Mid-band Region: Constant Maximum Gain Am]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Upper Cutoff Frequency fH]
    nodeD --> nodeE[High Frequency Region: Gain falls at 20 dB per decade]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Bandwidth BW = fH minus fL]
```

### Figure 4: Signal Flow and Phase Relationship

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **inverting nature of the CE amplifier** — a 180° phase shift between input and output.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Input sine wave positive half cycle] --> nodeB[Base voltage increases]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Base current IB increases]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Collector current IC increases by beta times]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Voltage drop across RC increases]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Collector voltage VC decreases]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Output sine wave negative half cycle: 180 degree phase shift]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Frequency Response Plot (Examiner's Expectation):**
> On a semi-log graph (frequency on log scale, gain in dB on linear scale), the student must mark:
> - The two cutoff frequencies $f_L$ and $f_H$ where the gain is **3 dB below** the mid-band value.
> - The mid-band flat region.
> - The roll-off slopes: **+20 dB/decade** below $f_L$ and **−20 dB/decade** above $f_H$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q1a, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** What is the function of the coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ in a single stage RC coupled CE amplifier? Why is the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ required?

**Model Answer:**
The coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ serve two essential functions:
1. They **block the DC bias** of the transistor from reaching the signal source and the load, thereby preventing any shift in the Q-point due to external connections.
2. They **pass the AC signal** from the source to the base and from the collector to the load with minimal attenuation at mid-band frequencies (since $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} \to 0$ as $f$ increases).

The **emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** is required to short-circuit the emitter resistor $R_E$ at signal (AC) frequencies. Without $C_E$, the AC current through $R_E$ would cause degenerative feedback, drastically reducing the voltage gain. With $C_E$ in place, $R_E$ provides DC stability (thermal runaway protection) but is removed from the AC signal path, preserving high mid-band gain.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q1b, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** A single stage RC coupled CE amplifier has a mid-band gain of 100. Express this gain in decibels. If the lower cutoff frequency is 50 Hz and the upper cutoff frequency is 200 kHz, calculate the bandwidth.

**Model Answer:**

$$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ dB}$$

$$\text{Bandwidth} = f_H - f_L = 200{,}000 - 50 = 199{,}950 \text{ Hz} \approx 200 \text{ kHz}$$

(For practical purposes, since $f_L \ll f_H$, the bandwidth is taken as $f_H$.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q2, 14 Marks]** *(Note: Lab courses typically have 14-mark viva + record evaluation, but we follow the same module structure for consistency.)*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a single stage RC coupled CE amplifier. Discuss the role of each component. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the CE amplifier circuit with $V_{CC} = 15\text{ V}$, $R_1 = 56\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 12\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 3.3\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 600\text{ }\Omega$, $\beta = 120$, and $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, calculate: (i) the Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$, (ii) the mid-band voltage gain $A_v$, and (iii) the input and output impedance. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Circuit Operation and Component Roles:**

*(Refer to Figure 1 in Section 4 for the circuit diagram.)*

- **$R_1$ and $R_2$ (Voltage Divider Bias):** Set the base voltage $V_B$ and thereby the Q-point of the transistor. They provide thermal stability.
- **$R_C$ (Collector Resistor):** Converts the collector current variations into output voltage variations; it is the primary gain-setting element.
- **$R_E$ (Emitter Resistor):** Provides DC negative feedback for Q-point stability against temperature and $\beta$ variations.
- **$C_{in}$ (Input Coupling Capacitor):** Blocks DC from the source while passing the AC input signal.
- **$C_{out}$ (Output Coupling Capacitor):** Blocks DC from the load while passing the amplified AC output.
- **$C_E$ (Emitter Bypass Capacitor):** Shorts $R_E$ at AC, eliminating AC degenerative feedback and maximizing gain.
- **BJT (Active Device):** Provides the current-controlled current source action that produces voltage amplification.

**Operation:** A small AC input at the base modulates the base current $i_b$, which is amplified by $\beta$ to produce a much larger collector current variation $i_c = \beta i_b$. This varying current through $R_C$ produces a large voltage variation at the collector, which is delivered to the load via $C_{out}$. The output is 180° out of phase with the input.

**(b) Numerical Calculations:**

**(i) Q-point:**

$$V_B = 15 \cdot \frac{12}{56 + 12} = 15 \cdot \frac{12}{68} = 2.647 \text{ V}$$

$$V_E = 2.647 - 0.7 = 1.947 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{CQ} = \frac{1.947 \text{ V}}{1.2 \text{ k}\Omega} = 1.62 \text{ mA}$$

$$V_{CEQ} = 15 - 1.62 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (3.3 + 1.2) \cdot 10^3 = 15 - 7.29 = 7.71 \text{ V}$$

The transistor is in the active region since $V_{CEQ} = 7.71 \text{ V} \gg V_{CE(sat)}$. ✓

**(ii) Mid-band Voltage Gain:**

$$g_m = \frac{1.62 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 62.3 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{3.3 \cdot 4.7}{3.3 + 4.7} = \frac{15.51}{8.0} = 1.94 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -62.3 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1.94 \cdot 10^3 = -120.86 \approx -120.9$$

In decibels: $20 \log_{10}(120.9) = 41.6 \text{ dB}$.

**(iii) Input and Output Impedance:**

$$r_\pi = \frac{120 \cdot 26 \text{ mV}}{1.62 \text{ mA}} = 1.926 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{in} = 56\text{k} \parallel 12\text{k} \parallel 1.926\text{k} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{56} + \frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{1.926}} \text{ k}\Omega \approx 1.65 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{out} = R_C = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the hybrid-π model of a BJT and derive the expression for the mid-band voltage gain of a single stage RC coupled CE amplifier. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the frequency response of the RC coupled CE amplifier. Define the lower and upper cutoff frequencies and the bandwidth. What is the significance of the mid-band region? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hybrid-π Model and Gain Derivation:**

The hybrid-π small-signal model of a BJT consists of:
- An input resistance $r_\pi$ between base (b) and emitter (e).
- A voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$ between collector (c) and emitter (e), where $g_m = I_{CQ}/V_T$.
- (Optional: feedback resistance $r_o$ between c and e for output resistance modeling.)

**Derivation of Mid-band Gain:**

At mid-band, all capacitors (coupling and bypass) are short circuits. With $C_E$ shorting $R_E$, the emitter is at AC ground. Applying KCL at the collector node:

$$i_c = g_m v_{be}$$

The output voltage across the parallel combination $R_C \parallel R_L$ is:

$$v_o = -i_c (R_C \parallel R_L) = -g_m v_{be} (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

Since $v_{be} = v_i$ (because the emitter is grounded at AC), the voltage gain is:

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}$$

The negative sign indicates the 180° phase inversion characteristic of the CE configuration.

**(b) Frequency Response Explanation:**

The frequency response of an RC coupled CE amplifier exhibits three distinct regions when plotted as gain (in dB) versus frequency (on a log scale):

1. **Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_L$):** The gain decreases at a rate of **+20 dB/decade** as frequency decreases. This roll-off is caused by the high reactance of the coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ and the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ at low frequencies, which attenuate the signal.

2. **Mid-band Region ($f_L < f < f_H$):** The gain is **constant and maximum**. All capacitors behave as short circuits, and the transistor's internal capacitances have negligible effect. This is the **useful operating range** of the amplifier.

3. **High-Frequency Region ($f > f_H$):** The gain decreases at a rate of **−20 dB/decade** as frequency increases. This is due to the **Miller effect** multiplying the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$, creating a dominant pole.

**Definitions:**

- **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$):** The frequency at which the gain falls to **0.707** (i.e., −3 dB) of its mid-band value, on the low-frequency side.
- **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$):** The frequency at which the gain falls to 0.707 of its mid-band value, on the high-frequency side.
- **Bandwidth ($BW$):** The range of frequencies over which the amplifier provides useful amplification: $BW = f_H - f_L$.

**Significance of Mid-band Region:** In this region, the amplifier exhibits **maximum flat gain** with **constant phase shift of 180°**. All mid-band small-signal analyses (gain, input/output impedance) are valid here. Most audio and instrumentation signals lie in this band, making it the design target.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v$:** The CE amplifier is inverting. Writing $A_v = +g_m(R_C \parallel R_L)$ instead of $-g_m(R_C \parallel R_L)$ will cost you **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Including $R_E$ in mid-band gain calculation:** A common mistake is computing $A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L) / (1 + g_m R_E)$ even in the mid-band. Remember that $C_E$ shorts $R_E$ at mid-band, so the denominator is just 1.
> 3. **Ignoring the bias resistors in $Z_{in}$:** The input impedance is $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$, **not** just $r_\pi$. Failing to include the bias network will underestimate $Z_{in}$.
> 4. **Failing to verify the active region:** Always check that $V_{CEQ} > V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V after DC analysis. If not, the Q-point is wrong and the small-signal parameters (like $g_m$) are invalid.
> 5. **Confusing $f_L$ with the smallest pole:** The lower cutoff is the **highest** of the three individual pole frequencies, not the smallest. The overall response is governed by the slowest-decaying pole.
> 6. **Not converting to dB:** KTU examiners expect gain in dB in frequency response plots. Always convert: $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_v)$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **single stage** means only one transistor is used; **RC coupled** refers to the use of coupling capacitors to block DC between stages or between source/load.
- ✅ The **voltage-divider bias** ($R_1, R_2$) is the most common Q-point stabilization method for this lab experiment.
- ✅ The mid-band voltage gain is $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ — **negative** because of phase inversion.
- ✅ The input impedance is $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$; the output impedance is $Z_{out} \approx R_C$.
- ✅ The **emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** is the single most important component for achieving high mid-band gain — without it, the gain collapses by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_E)$.
- ✅ The frequency response has **three regions**: low-frequency roll-off, flat mid-band, and high-frequency roll-off.
- ✅ The **lower cutoff frequency** is the maximum of the three individual low-frequency poles: $f_L = \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$.
- ✅ The **upper cutoff frequency** is determined by the Miller effect multiplying $C_\mu$.
- ✅ The **bandwidth** is $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ for most practical audio amplifiers.
- ✅ The **3-dB point** defines the cutoff frequency — gain is reduced to $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ of mid-band value, or equivalently, **3 dB down**.
- ✅ Always verify the **Q-point is in the active region** ($V_{CEQ} > V_{CE(sat)}$) before performing AC analysis.
- ✅ Gain in dB: $A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_v \vert)$.
- ✅ On a semi-log plot, the roll-off slopes are **±20 dB/decade** (or **±6 dB/octave**).
- ✅ The CE amplifier is the **most widely used** BJT configuration because of its high gain, moderate input impedance, and moderate output impedance — ideal for cascading.
- ✅ Practical lab note: Use a **function generator** for input, a **CRO/DSO** for output measurement, and a **millivoltmeter** or DSO for accurate amplitude measurement at $f_L$, $f_H$, and mid-band.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biasing of MOSFET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL308 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Biasing of MOSFET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MOSFET Biasing** is the process of applying suitable DC voltages and currents to the terminals (Gate, Drain, Source) of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor so as to establish a stable, desired **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** in the active/saturation region of the device, independent of variations in device parameters such as threshold voltage ($V_T$), transconductance parameter ($K$), and temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The biasing experiment is intended to demonstrate *practical Q-point stabilization* against the inherent large parameter spread of MOSFETs (unlike BJTs, MOSFETs exhibit wide variation in $V_T$ from device to device).

## 1.2 The Three Key DC Quantities (The Q-Point)

The Q-point of a MOSFET is defined by the triplet:

- **$I_D$** — Drain Current (DC output current)
- **$V_{GS}$** — Gate-to-Source Voltage (DC control voltage)
- **$V_{DS}$** — Drain-to-Source Voltage (DC output voltage)

For a MOSFET to operate as a small-signal amplifier, it **must** be biased in the **saturation region**, where:

$$V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_T$$

## 1.3 Why is Biasing Needed? — Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Water Tap" Analogy**
> Imagine a **MOSFET as a smart electronic water tap** (Drain = water inlet, Source = water outlet, Gate = pressure-sensitive handle). Just as a tap needs an initial pressure setting to deliver a steady, controlled trickle of water regardless of minor disturbances (vibrations, slight turns of the handle), a MOSFET needs a *carefully set Gate voltage* so that a *steady, predictable Drain current* flows. Without this "pre-setting," the tap may be fully closed (cut-off) or fully open (ohmic), and small disturbances will cause wild fluctuations in flow.

**Key Engineering Reason:** The MOSFET's Drain current $I_D$ depends **quadratically** on $(V_{GS} - V_T)$, and $V_T$ itself varies by $\pm 0.5$ V to $\pm 1$ V from one device to another. Without bias stabilization, two "identical" MOSFETs would have wildly different Q-points — a disaster for mass-produced amplifier circuits.

## 1.4 Standard MOSFET Current Equation (Saturation Region)

The fundamental DC equation governing MOSFET operation in saturation is:

$$I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_T)^2$$

where **$K = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$** is the *conduction parameter* (in **A/V²**), and **$V_T$** is the *threshold voltage* (in **V**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In many textbook problems, the conduction parameter is given as $k_n = 2K$ or directly as $K$. Always check the definition used: $I_D = k_n (V_{GS} - V_T)^2 / 2$ vs $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_T)^2$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Standard MOSFET Biasing Circuits

### **A. Fixed Bias (Gate Bias)**
The simplest configuration: a fixed DC voltage is applied to the Gate through a large resistor $R_G$. **$I_G = 0$** for a MOSFET, so there is no voltage drop across $R_G$.

**Operating Logic:**
- Apply $V_{GG}$ to Gate (negative for depletion-mode, positive for enhancement-mode N-channel)
- Since $I_G = 0$: $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$
- $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_T)^2$
- $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback:** This is the *worst* bias stability — Q-point is extremely sensitive to $V_T$ variations. Used only for switching applications.

### **B. Self-Bias (Source Bias)**
The Gate is grounded through $R_G$, and the Source is connected to ground through $R_S$. The voltage drop across $R_S$ (due to $I_D$) provides the negative bias needed for an N-channel depletion MOSFET.

**Operating Logic:**
- Gate voltage $V_G = 0$ (no current flows through $R_G$)
- Source voltage $V_S = I_D R_S$
- Therefore: $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = -I_D R_S$
- Substitute into current equation: $I_D = K(-I_D R_S - V_T)^2$

This yields a **quadratic equation** in $I_D$:

$$K R_S^2 \, I_D^2 + (2K R_S V_T - 1) I_D + K V_T^2 = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For an N-channel depletion MOSFET** ($V_T < 0$), this gives a *finite, self-stabilized* $I_D$. **For an enhancement MOSFET** ($V_T > 0$), self-bias is **not possible** without a separate positive gate supply.

### **C. Voltage Divider Bias** (Most Stable — Industry Standard)
Two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a voltage divider from $V_{DD}$ to ground, providing a stable DC voltage to the Gate. A source resistor $R_S$ provides DC and AC negative feedback.

**Operating Logic:**
- Thevenin equivalent at the Gate: $V_{TH} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ and $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$
- Since $I_G = 0$: $V_G = V_{TH}$
- $V_{GS} = V_{TH} - I_D R_S$
- Substitute: $I_D = K(V_{TH} - I_D R_S - V_T)^2$

This expands to:

$$K R_S^2 \, I_D^2 + (2K R_S V_T - 2K R_S V_{TH} - 1) I_D + K(V_{TH} - V_T)^2 = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is voltage divider bias the most stable?**
> - Provides a *fixed, predictable* $V_{TH}$ independent of MOSFET parameters.
> - The source resistor $R_S$ provides *DC negative feedback*: if $I_D$ tries to increase (e.g., due to temperature), $V_{GS}$ decreases, automatically reducing $I_D$.

### **D. Drain-Feedback Bias**
The Gate is connected to the Drain through a large resistor $R_G$ (instead of to $V_{DD}$). The DC voltage developed at the Drain is used to bias the Gate.

**Operating Logic:**
- $V_{GS} = V_{DS} - I_D R_S$ (if Source resistor is present)
- $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$
- Combined: $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S) - I_D R_S = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + 2R_S)$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drain Current (Saturation) | $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_T)^2$ | **A** (mA range in practice) |
| 2 | Conduction Parameter | $K = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$ | **A/V²** |
| 3 | Overdrive Voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_T$ | **V** |
| 4 | Saturation Condition | $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$ | Must be satisfied |
| 5 | Fixed Bias $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$ | Direct from supply |
| 6 | Self-Bias $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ | $V_G = 0$ |
| 7 | Voltage Divider $V_{TH}$ | $V_{TH} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | Thevenin at Gate |
| 8 | Voltage Divider $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = V_{TH} - I_D R_S$ | General expression |
| 9 | Drain Voltage (any bias) | $V_D = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ | KVL at output loop |
| 10 | Source Voltage (any bias) | $V_S = I_D R_S$ | KVL at source |
| 11 | Drain-to-Source Voltage | $V_{DS} = V_D - V_S$ | Output across MOSFET |
| 12 | Stability Factor (Self-Bias) | $S = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial I_D} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + K \cdot 2(V_{GS}-V_T) R_S}$ | Numerical factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Q-Point Stability:** The stability factor $S$ quantifies how much $I_D$ changes for a unit change in $I_{DSS}$ (or $K$). Lower $S$ is better. Voltage divider bias typically gives $S \approx 1$ to $3$, whereas fixed bias has $S \to \infty$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

MOSFET biasing is the **foundation of every analog integrated circuit**:

- **Audio amplifiers** in mobile phones rely on carefully biased MOSFET pairs for low distortion.
- **Operational amplifiers** (e.g., the classic µA741 uses BJTs, but modern op-amps like the LMC662 use MOSFETs) require rail-to-rail stable bias.
- **Power electronics** — SMPS, motor drivers — use biased MOSFETs as switching elements in the *active region* with a stable Q-point.
- **RF front-ends** in communication systems depend on ultra-stable bias to maintain gain linearity and low noise figure.
